name: FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN

topics.title: Computer Hardware - CPU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Hardware - CPU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**: The CPU is the primary integrated circuit (IC) chip responsible for interpreting and executing instructions fetched from memory, performing arithmetic, logical, and control operations in a computer system. It acts as the **brain** of the computer and coordinates the activities of all other hardware components through the system bus architecture.

The CPU, also known as the **microprocessor**, consists of three main sub-systems working in synchronized harmony:

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, comparisons).
2. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs and orchestrates the flow of data and instructions by generating control signals.
3. **Register Set** — High-speed internal storage cells used for temporary data manipulation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of the CPU as a Post Office Sorting Facility.**
> - **Letters (Instructions)** arrive on a conveyor belt (the **bus**) into the **inbox (Instruction Register)**.
> - The **Sorting Manager (Control Unit)** reads the address and decides where each letter should go.
> - The **Math Workers (ALU)** process any special calculations required (e.g., priority, weight).
> - The **Personal Notepads (Registers)** keep track of which letters are being processed right now.
> - The **Master Clock (System Clock)** ticks like a metronome, ensuring everyone works in rhythm.
> - The **Supervisor's Stamp (Program Counter)** marks the next letter to be picked up.

### 1.3 Standard CPU Performance Metrics (Industry-Recognized Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Speed | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** — typically **GHz** for modern CPUs |
| Word Length | $n$ | **bits** (32-bit, 64-bit) |
| Throughput | — | **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) |
| Floating-Point Performance | — | **GFLOPS / TFLOPS** |
| Latency | $T$ | **nanoseconds (ns)** per cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to describe the **block diagram of a CPU**, explain the **fetch-decode-execute cycle**, define the role of **registers**, and compare **RISC vs CISC** architectures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the CPU

The CPU interacts with **memory** and **I/O devices** through three dedicated buses:

- **Data Bus** — Bidirectional; carries the actual data being processed (width = word length).
- **Address Bus** — Unidirectional; carries the memory address of data/instruction (width = addressable space).
- **Control Bus** — Bidirectional; carries timing and control signals (Read/Write, Interrupt, Clock).

### 2.2 The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle (FDE)

The CPU continuously loops through four sub-operations for every machine instruction:

1. **FETCH** — The instruction at the address held by the **Program Counter (PC)** is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)** via the **Memory Address Register (MAR)** and **Memory Data Register (MDR)**. The PC is then incremented.
2. **DECODE** — The **Instruction Decoder** within the Control Unit interprets the opcode and determines which micro-operations are required.
3. **EXECUTE** — The **ALU** performs the required arithmetic/logic operation, or the CU directs data movement between registers/memory.
4. **STORE** — The result is written back to a register, memory location, or I/O device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the cycle matter?** Every single task on a computer — opening a file, typing a letter, running a game — is broken down into millions of these FDE cycles. Understanding this loop is the **single most important concept** for KTU Module 1 evaluation.

### 2.3 Internal Registers of a CPU

| Register | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds the address of the **next** instruction to fetch |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the **current** instruction being executed |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the address of memory location to access |
| **MDR** | Memory Data Register | Temporarily stores data being transferred to/from memory |
| **ACC** | Accumulator | Primary register for ALU operations and results |
| **SP** | Stack Pointer | Points to the top of the stack in memory |
| **SR** | Status Register | Holds flag bits (Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are board-favorite. Memorize these for guaranteed marks.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | Seconds (s) |
| CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$ | Seconds |
| CPU Performance | $Perf = \dfrac{1}{T_{CPU}}$ | Higher is better |
| MIPS Rating | $MIPS = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million instructions/sec |
| Amdahl's Speedup | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors |
| Addressable Memory | $M = 2^{a}$ | $a$ = address bus width in bits |
| Data Bus Throughput | $BW = f_{clock} \times w_{data}$ | Bytes/second (B/s) |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by:
> - **Intel/AMD** engineers to benchmark CPUs (PassMark, Geekbench scores).
> - **Embedded system designers** to optimize IoT devices.
> - **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** architects to design supercomputers like *PARAM Siddhi*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between CPU Time, Clock Rate, and CPI

The **CPU Execution Time** is the cornerstone of all performance calculations. We derive it from first principles.

**Given:**
- $N_{inst}$ = Total number of instructions in a program
- $CPI$ = Average cycles per instruction (Cycles Per Instruction)
- $f_{clock}$ = Clock frequency in cycles per second (Hz)

**Step 1:** Total number of clock cycles required to execute the program is:

$$C_{total} = N_{inst} \times CPI$$

*Explanation:* If the program has 1000 instructions and each takes on average 3 cycles, then $C_{total} = 1000 \times 3 = 3000$ cycles.

**Step 2:** The time taken per clock cycle is the inverse of the clock frequency:

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$$

*Explanation:* A 3 GHz clock has a period of $1 / (3 \times 10^9) \approx 0.333$ nanoseconds.

**Step 3:** Total execution time is total cycles multiplied by time per cycle:

$$T_{CPU} = C_{total} \times T_{cycle}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3:

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$$

This is the **fundamental CPU performance equation** required for KTU derivations.

### 3.2 Worked Example: CPU Time & MIPS Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (Model KTU Question):** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz** with an average CPI of **2.4**. A program contains **$5 \times 10^{9}$** instructions. Calculate the execution time and MIPS rating.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given values.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{clock} &= 2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \\
CPI &= 2.4 \\
N_{inst} &= 5 \times 10^{9}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the CPU Time formula.

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$$

**Step 3:** Plug in the numerical values.

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{9} \times 2.4}{2.5 \times 10^{9}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^{9}}{2.5 \times 10^{9}} = 4.8 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate MIPS.

$$MIPS = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{9}}{4.8 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1041.67 \text{ MIPS}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: RISC vs CISC Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **guaranteed 7-mark KTU question**. Master the table below.

| Parameter | **RISC** (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) | **CISC** (Complex Instruction Set Computer) |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Set | Small, highly optimized | Large, varied |
| Instruction Length | Fixed (typically 32-bit) | Variable |
| Execution Cycles | 1 cycle per instruction (pipelined) | Multiple cycles per instruction |
| Memory Access | Load/Store architecture (only via LOAD/STORE) | Register-Memory (operands can be in memory) |
| Pipelining | Highly efficient | Difficult to pipeline |
| Examples | ARM, SPARC, MIPS, RISC-V | Intel x86, AMD x86-64 |
| Power Consumption | Low (ideal for mobile/IoT) | Higher |
| Code Size | Larger (more instructions needed) | Smaller (single complex instruction) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture

| Parameter | **Von Neumann** | **Harvard** |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Single shared memory for instructions and data | Separate memories for instructions and data |
| Bus | Single bus (bottleneck — "Von Neumann bottleneck") | Separate buses for instructions and data |
| Speed | Slower (fetch and data cannot occur simultaneously) | Faster (simultaneous access possible) |
| Used in | General-purpose PCs, laptops, servers | DSPs, microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, PIC) |
| Flexibility | High (program can modify itself) | Lower |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a CPU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPUBlock["CPU CHIP"]
        CU["Control Unit CU"]
        ALU["Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU"]
        REG["Register Set PC IR MAR MDR ACC"]
        DEC["Instruction Decoder"]
    end

    EXT_MEM[("Main Memory RAM")]
    IO_DEV["I/O Devices"]

    BUS_DATA["Data Bus bidirectional"]
    BUS_ADDR["Address Bus unidirectional"]
    BUS_CTRL["Control Bus bidirectional"]

    EXT_MEM <--> BUS_DATA
    EXT_MEM <--> BUS_ADDR
    EXT_MEM <--> BUS_CTRL
    IO_DEV <--> BUS_DATA
    IO_DEV <--> BUS_CTRL

    BUS_DATA <--> REG
    BUS_ADDR <--> MAR
    BUS_CTRL <--> CU
    REG --> DEC
    DEC --> CU
    CU --> ALU
    ALU <--> REG
```

### 4.2 The Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle (Finite State Machine)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START(["Start"]) --> F["1 FETCH"]
    F -->|"Read memory at PC"| IR["IR loads instruction"]
    IR -->|"PC = PC + 1"| D["2 DECODE"]
    D -->|"Opcode analyzed"| EX["3 EXECUTE"]
    EX -->|"ALU computes"| ST["4 STORE"]
    ST -->|"Result to register/memory"| END1["End of one cycle"]
    END1 --> F
    ST -.->|"HALT signal"| STOP(["STOP"])
```

### 4.3 CPU-Memory-I/O System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[("CPU Block")]
    CACHE["L1 L2 L3 Cache"]
    RAM[("Primary Memory RAM")]
    ROM[("ROM Firmware")]
    SEC[("Secondary Storage HDD SSD")]
    INPUT["Input Devices Keyboard Mouse"]
    OUTPUT["Output Devices Monitor Printer"]
    SYSBUS["System Bus Address Data Control"]

    CPU <--> CACHE
    CPU <--> SYSBUS
    SYSBUS <--> RAM
    SYSBUS <--> ROM
    SYSBUS <--> SEC
    SYSBUS <--> INPUT
    SYSBUS <--> OUTPUT
```

### 4.4 Generations of CPUs (Evolution Timeline)

```mermaid
timeline
    title Evolution of CPU Generations
    1970s : First Microprocessors : Intel 4004 (4-bit, 740 kHz)
    1980s : 16-bit and 32-bit Era : Intel 8086, 80386
    1990s : Pentium Series : Superscalar architecture
    2000s : Multi-core Revolution : Intel Core 2 Duo
    2010s : 64-bit Mobile Era : ARM Cortex, Apple A-series
    2020s : Many-core and AI : Apple M1/M2, AMD Ryzen 9, AI accelerators
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Central Processing Unit. List its three main components.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** is the primary hardware component of a computer that interprets and executes instructions, performs arithmetic and logical operations, and coordinates the activities of all other hardware devices. It is often called the **brain** of the computer system.

The three main components of the CPU are:

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, comparisons).
2. **Control Unit (CU)** — Directs, coordinates, and controls the flow of data and instructions within the CPU and to/from peripherals by generating appropriate control signals.
3. **Register Set** — A collection of high-speed internal memory locations (e.g., PC, IR, ACC, MAR, MDR) used for temporary data storage during processing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the function of the Program Counter (PC) and Instruction Register (IR)?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Program Counter (PC):** The PC is a special-purpose register that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched and executed. After each fetch operation, the PC is automatically incremented to point to the subsequent instruction, ensuring sequential program flow.

- **Instruction Register (IR):** The IR is a register that holds the **currently fetched instruction** during the decode and execute phases. The Instruction Decoder reads the contents of the IR to determine the opcode and operands required for execution. The IR acts as a temporary buffer between memory and the decoding circuitry.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose Either A or B

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a CPU. List the functions of the Control Unit, ALU, and Registers. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle in detail. Discuss the role of PC, MAR, MDR, and IR in each step. *(7 Marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — CPU Internal Architecture (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram:**

```
            ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │              CPU                        │
            │  ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐    │
   Bus ────►│  │  REGISTERS│◄──►│  CONTROL UNIT   │    │
            │  │ PC IR ACC│    │   (Instruction   │    │
            │  │ MAR MDR  │    │    Decoder +     │    │
            │  └────┬────┘    │    Timing Logic) │    │
            │       │         └────────┬─────────┘    │
            │       │                  │              │
            │       ▼                  ▼              │
            │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐        │
            │  │   ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT     │        │
            │  │   (Add, Sub, AND, OR, etc.) │        │
            │  └─────────────────────────────┘        │
            └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       ▲           ▲
                       │           │
                 Address Bus    Data Bus
```

**Functions:**

1. **Control Unit (CU):** Acts as the **central nervous system** of the CPU. It fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and generates the necessary control signals to direct the ALU, registers, and I/O devices. The CU does not process data itself; it only controls *when* and *where* data moves.

2. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all **arithmetic operations** (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and **logical operations** (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shift, rotate, comparison). It accepts inputs from registers, processes them, and places the result in the Accumulator (ACC). The ALU also updates the **Status Register (flags)** such as Zero flag, Carry flag, and Overflow flag.

3. **Register Set:** Provides the CPU with **ultra-high-speed temporary storage** that is directly accessible during instruction execution. Registers minimize the need to access slower main memory (RAM). Common registers include PC, IR, ACC, MAR, MDR, and SP.

#### Part (b) — Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle (7 Marks)

The CPU continuously repeats a four-step cycle for every instruction:

**Step 1: FETCH**
- The address in the **Program Counter (PC)** is transferred to the **Memory Address Register (MAR)**.
- A Read signal is sent via the control bus to memory.
- The instruction located at that address is loaded into the **Memory Data Register (MDR)**.
- The instruction is then moved from MDR to the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- The PC is **incremented by 1** to point to the next instruction.

**Step 2: DECODE**
- The **Instruction Decoder** within the Control Unit examines the opcode field of the IR.
- It determines which micro-operations must be performed and which registers/ALU functions are required.

**Step 3: EXECUTE**
- The CU activates the required circuit paths.
- If the instruction is arithmetic, the **ALU** is engaged with the appropriate operands.
- If the instruction is a data transfer, the CU routes data between registers and/or memory.

**Step 4: STORE**
- The result of the execution is written back to the **Accumulator (ACC)**, a general-purpose register, or sent to a specific memory location.
- Status flags (Zero, Carry, Sign) are updated in the **Status Register**.

> The cycle then **loops back to Step 1** for the next instruction. This continuous loop is the fundamental heartbeat of the CPU.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare RISC and CISC architectures in detail. Mention examples of processors for each. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A processor has a clock rate of **3.2 GHz**, an average CPI of **2.0**, and executes a program with **$8 \times 10^{9}$** instructions. Calculate:
  (i) CPU execution time
  (ii) MIPS rating
  (iii) If the clock rate is upgraded to **4.0 GHz** while CPI remains 2.0, what is the new execution time? *(7 Marks)*

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — RISC vs CISC (7 Marks)

| Parameter | **RISC** | **CISC** |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Reduced Instruction Set Computer | Complex Instruction Set Computer |
| Instruction Set | Small, simple, orthogonal | Large, complex, varied |
| Instruction Length | Fixed (typically 32 bits) | Variable length |
| Addressing Modes | Few (usually 3–5) | Many (12–24) |
| Memory Access | Load/Store architecture | Register-Memory or Memory-Memory |
| Execution Time | 1 cycle per instruction (pipelined) | Multiple cycles per instruction |
| Pipelining | Highly efficient and easy | Difficult to implement |
| Code Size | Larger (more instructions per task) | Compact (fewer instructions per task) |
| Compiler Complexity | Complex compiler, simple hardware | Simple compiler, complex hardware |
| Power Consumption | Low (ideal for mobile) | Higher |
| Examples | **ARM (Cortex-A series), SPARC, MIPS, RISC-V, Apple M1** | **Intel Core i7/i9, AMD Ryzen, x86-64** |

**Key Insight:** Modern x86 processors (CISC) internally **decode complex instructions into RISC-like micro-operations** before execution, blurring the historical divide.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Performance Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $f_{clock} = 3.2 \times 10^{9}$ Hz
- $CPI = 2.0$
- $N_{inst} = 8 \times 10^{9}$

**(i) CPU Execution Time:**

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{inst} \times CPI}{f_{clock}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{9} \times 2.0}{3.2 \times 10^{9}} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{9}}{3.2 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 5.0 \text{ seconds}$$

**(ii) MIPS Rating:**

$$MIPS = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{9}}{5.0 \times 10^{6}} = 1600 \text{ MIPS}$$

**(iii) New Execution Time at 4.0 GHz:**

$$T_{new} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{9} \times 2.0}{4.0 \times 10^{9}} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{9}}{4.0 \times 10^{9}} = 4.0 \text{ seconds}$$

**Speedup Achieved:**

$$S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} = \dfrac{5.0}{4.0} = 1.25\times \text{ faster}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Always convert clock frequency to **Hertz (Hz)** before substituting. Writing $3.2$ GHz directly will give a wrong answer of $5 \times 10^{-9}$ seconds. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 2. **MIPS Formula Trap:** MIPS = $N_{inst} / (T_{CPU} \times 10^{6})$, **NOT** $N_{inst} \times 10^{6} / T_{CPU}$. Many students invert the formula. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **FDE Cycle Trap:** Do not skip the **PC increment** step during FETCH. Examiners specifically test this. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 4. **RISC vs CISC Trap:** Always provide **examples** for both. Generic answers without ARM/Intel lose marks. **Lose 2 marks.**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — CPU)**

- ✅ **CPU = ALU + Control Unit + Registers** (the classic 3-part definition).
- ✅ **Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store** — the FDE cycle repeats billions of times per second.
- ✅ **PC** holds the *next* instruction address; **IR** holds the *current* instruction.
- ✅ **MAR ↔ Memory** for addressing; **MDR ↔ Memory** for data transfer.
- ✅ **Clock speed** is measured in **Hz (GHz)**; $T = 1/f$.
- ✅ **CPU Time** = $(N_{inst} \times CPI) / f_{clock}$ — the master equation.
- ✅ **MIPS** = Instructions / (Time × $10^6$) — higher MIPS = faster CPU.
- ✅ **RISC** = fixed length, 1 cycle, Load/Store, examples: **ARM, RISC-V, Apple M1**.
- ✅ **CISC** = variable length, multi-cycle, examples: **Intel x86, AMD Ryzen**.
- ✅ **Von Neumann** = shared memory & bus (PCs); **Harvard** = separate memory & bus (8051, DSPs).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law**: $S = 1 / ((1-P) + P/N)$ — used to find parallel speedup.
- ✅ **Addressable Memory** = $2^{a}$ where $a$ is address bus width.
- ✅ Modern CPUs are **multi-core**, **pipelined**, and use **cache hierarchies (L1/L2/L3)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory - Memory Hierarchy: Registers, Cache, Virtual Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Memory - Memory Hierarchy: Registers, Cache, Virtual Memory # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Memory Hierarchy? **Memory Hierarchy** is an architectural arrangement of different types of computer storage devices (registers, cache, RAM, disk) organized in a pyramid structure based on their **access speed**, **cost per bit**, and **storage capacity**. The fundamental goal is to provide the **illusion of a large, fast, and inexpensive memory** by intelligently combining the speed of smaller memories with the capacity of larger ones. The hierarchy is built on a simple but powerful premise: > *At any given time, the CPU needs only a tiny fraction of the total data. So we keep the "currently active" data in fast memory and the rest in slow memory.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > The memory hierarchy is a **core examinable topic**. Students must know the order of the hierarchy, the role of each level, the principle of locality, and how **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is computed. ## 1.2 The Five Levels of Memory Hierarchy From the **fastest/smallest/most-expensive** to the **slowest/largest/cheapest**, the hierarchy is: | Level | Memory Type | Typical Capacity | Access Time | Managed By | |:-----:|:-----------:|:----------------:|:-----------:|:----------:| | L0 | CPU Registers | 32 – 256 Bytes | **~0.25 – 1 ns** | Compiler / Software | | L1 | Level-1 Cache (SRAM) | 16 – 64 KB | **~1 – 3 ns** | Hardware | | L2 | Level-2 Cache (SRAM) | 256 KB – 1 MB | **~3 – 10 ns** | Hardware | | L3 | Main Memory (DRAM) | 4 – 32 GB | **~50 – 100 ns** | OS + Hardware | | L4 | Secondary / Virtual Memory (HDD/SSD) | 256 GB – 4 TB | **~0.1 – 10 ms** | Operating System | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Librarian's Desk" Imagine a **librarian** working in a massive library with **millions of books**: - **Registers** → The **books currently open on the librarian's desk**. She can read them in seconds, but her desk only holds 5–10 books. - **Cache (L1/L2/L3)** → The **nearby shelf** within arm's reach. Slightly slower to grab, but still very fast (a few seconds). - **Main Memory (RAM)** → The **front desk counter** where books are stored in an organized cart. Slower to retrieve (walk to counter, search), but holds thousands of books. - **Virtual Memory (Disk)** → The **main library stacks** deep in the basement. Holds millions of books, but retrieving one takes minutes (walking, searching catalogs, etc.). When the librarian needs a book, she first checks her **desk → shelf → counter → basement**. The books she uses most often naturally migrate upward — this is the essence of **cache hierarchy**. ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Board Exam Favorites) > [!NOTE] > **Definitions you must memorize verbatim for KTU exams:** - **Hit:** A successful access where data is found at the given memory level. Example — CPU finds the data in L1 cache. - **Miss:** A failed access; data must be fetched from a lower (slower) level. - **Hit Rate (h):** The fraction of accesses satisfied at a given level. - **Miss Rate (1 − h):** The fraction of accesses that go to a lower level. - **Hit Time:** Time to access data when there is a hit (includes time to determine hit/miss). - **Miss Penalty:** Extra time required to fetch data from the next lower level on a miss. - **Locality of Reference:** The principle that programs tend to access nearby data and recently used data repeatedly. ## 1.5 Principle of Locality — The Foundation of Memory Hierarchy The hierarchy works because of **locality**, which has two flavours: 1. **Temporal Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, it is likely to be referenced again soon.* (e.g., loop counters, sum accumulators) 2. **Spatial Locality:** *If a memory location is referenced, nearby locations are likely to be referenced soon.* (e.g., iterating over an array sequentially) > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Whenever KTU asks *"Why does memory hierarchy work?"*, the single-sentence answer is **"Because programs exhibit temporal and spatial locality of reference."** Full stop. No other answer gets full marks. ## 1.6 Registers — The Top of the Hierarchy **Registers** are tiny, high-speed storage cells built **directly inside the CPU chip**, typically made of **6–8 transistors per bit** (flip-flops or latches). They hold operands, addresses, and intermediate results the ALU needs immediately. - **Typical size of a register:** **32 bits or 64 bits** depending on the CPU architecture (x86, ARM). - **Number of registers:** Usually **16 – 64 general-purpose registers** in modern CPUs. - **Access time:** **~0.25 – 1 ns**, often equal to the CPU clock cycle itself. - **Managed by:** The **compiler** (it decides which variables go into registers during code generation — a process called *register allocation*). ## 1.7 Cache Memory — The Speed Booster **Cache** is a small, fast memory placed **between the CPU and main memory** to reduce the average memory access time. Modern CPUs have **three levels (L1, L2, L3)**, with L1 being the fastest and smallest, integrated into the CPU core. **Cache uses SRAM (Static RAM)** — each bit uses **6 transistors**, so it's faster but more expensive than DRAM. ### Cache Mapping Techniques (Frequently Tested) | Technique | How it works | Pros | Cons | |:---------:|:-------------|:-----|:----:| | **Direct Mapping** | Each main-memory block maps to exactly **one** cache line, determined by `(Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines)` | Simple, fast hardware | High conflict misses | | **Fully Associative** | A block can be placed in **any** cache line | Lowest miss rate | Expensive, complex comparator | | **Set-Associative (n-way)** | Cache is divided into **sets**; each block maps to one set, but can occupy any of the `n` lines within | Balance of speed + hit rate | Moderate complexity | ## 1.8 Virtual Memory — The Capacity Extender **Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique where the **OS uses the hard disk (or SSD) as an extension of RAM**. It gives each process the **illusion of having a large, contiguous address space**, even though physical RAM may be smaller. - The **CPU generates virtual addresses**. - These are translated into **physical addresses** by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**. - The unit of transfer between disk and RAM is called a **page** (typically **4 KB**). - The page table — kept in main memory — stores the mapping from virtual pages to physical frames. - A small, fast cache of recent translations called the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** accelerates this process. > [!NOTE] > **Why Virtual Memory?** > 1. Allows programs larger than physical RAM to run (demand paging). > 2. Provides memory protection — each process has its own address space. > 3. Simplifies programming — programmers can write as if memory is one big flat array. > 4. Enables efficient multitasking via context switching. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mathematics of Memory Access — AMAT Derivation The **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** is the single most important formula in this topic. KTU has asked variations of this in nearly every previous year paper. **Conceptual Setup:** When the CPU requests data, it first checks **Level 1** (e.g., L1 cache). If found → **hit**. If not found → **miss**, and it goes to Level 2, paying the **miss penalty**. **Step-by-step logic:** - Let the **hit rate** of Level 1 be `h`. Then the **miss rate** is `1 - h`. - On a hit, the access takes **Hit Time** `T₁`. - On a miss, we first spend `T₁` (to discover the miss) and then add the miss penalty `T₂`. - Expected time = `(Probability of Hit × T₁) + (Probability of Miss × (T₁ + T₂))`. **Two-Level Formula:** $$\text{AMAT}_2 = h_1 \cdot T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot (T_1 + T_2)$$ Simplifying: $$\text{AMAT}_2 = T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot T_2$$ **Three-Level Formula** (CPU Register, L1, L2, Main Memory): $$\text{AMAT}_3 = T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot \big[ T_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot T_3 \big]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Rule:** Each level's AMAT adds **its own hit time** plus **its miss rate multiplied by the AMAT of the next lower level**. This is recursive. ## 2.2 Cache Performance Formulas | Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | |:------:|:-------:|:---------------:| | `h` | Hit rate | $\dfrac{\text{Number of Hits}}{\text{Total CPU accesses}}$ | | `(1 − h)` | Miss rate | $\dfrac{\text{Number of Misses}}{\text{Total CPU accesses}}$ | | `AMAT` | Average Memory Access Time | $T_{hit} + (1 - h) \cdot T_{miss\_penalty}$ | | `CPI` | Cycles per Instruction | Base CPI + Memory-stall cycles per instruction | | `Memory Stall Cycles` | Extra cycles waiting for memory | $\dfrac{\text{Memory accesses}}{\text{Instruction}} \times \text{Miss rate} \times \text{Miss penalty}$ | | `CPU Time` | Total execution time | $\text{Instruction count} \times \text{CPI} \times \text{Clock cycle time}$ | **Cache Performance Speedup Formula:** $$\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{\text{Execution Time without cache}}{\text{Execution Time with cache}} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$$ ## 2.3 Virtual Memory — Address Translation Math **Virtual Address Breakdown:** A virtual address is split into three fields used by the MMU and TLB: $$\text{Virtual Address} = \underbrace{\text{VPN}}_{\text{Virtual Page Number}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{\text{Within-page byte}}$$ If the page size is $2^n$ bytes, then the offset is $n$ bits, and the remaining upper bits form the VPN. **Physical Address Breakdown:** $$\text{Physical Address} = \underbrace{\text{PFN}}_{\text{Physical Frame Number}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{\text{same as above}}$$ The offset is **unchanged** during translation — only the page number is replaced via the page table. **TLB Hit Rate Formula:** $$\text{Effective Access Time (EAT)} = h_{TLB} \cdot (T_{MMU} + T_{mem}) + (1 - h_{TLB}) \cdot (T_{MMU} + 2 \cdot T_{mem})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why `2 · T_mem` on a TLB miss?** > Because we must access the page table **once** in memory (cost $T_{mem}$), then access the actual data **again** in memory (another $T_{mem}$). The MMU lookup is fast and is often absorbed into $T_{mem}$. ## 2.4 Cache Mapping — Address Field Formulas For a memory with $2^N$ addresses, cache with $2^C$ lines, and block size of $2^B$ bytes: $$\underbrace{\text{N bits}}_{\text{Tag}} \;\;\big|\;\; \underbrace{\text{(N − B − C) bits}}_{\text{Set / Line Index}} \;\;\big|\;\; \underbrace{\text{B bits}}_{\text{Block Offset}}$$ - **Tag bits** = Total memory address bits − Index bits − Offset bits - **Number of sets** = $2^{N-B-C}$ - **Set-associativity** = Lines per set = $2^C / 2^{N-B-C}$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | How Memory Hierarchy Is Used | |:------:|:-----------------------------| | **CPU Design (Intel, AMD, ARM)** | L1/L2/L3 caches determine IPC (Instructions Per Cycle) and benchmark scores. | | **Database Systems** | Buffer pools in PostgreSQL/Oracle mimic cache hierarchy. | | **Operating Systems** | Virtual memory paging directly impacts system responsiveness and swap usage. | | **Embedded Systems** | Tight memory budgets force careful register allocation and cache-friendly code. | | **Web Servers** | TLB miss storms can degrade performance; *huge pages* are used to reduce TLB pressure. | ## 2.6 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Pin This!) | # | Formula | Use Case | |:-:|:--------|:---------| | 1 | $\text{AMAT} = T_h + (1-h) \cdot T_m$ | Cache hit/miss calculation | | 2 | $\text{Memory Stall Cycles} = \text{Mem accesses/instr} \times \text{Miss Rate} \times \text{Miss Penalty}$ | CPU performance | | 3 | $\text{EAT} = h \cdot (T_{m}+T_{mem}) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{m}+2T_{mem})$ | TLB-based virtual memory | | 4 | $\text{Page Number} = \lfloor \dfrac{\text{Virtual Address}}{\text{Page Size}} \rfloor$ | Address decomposition | | 5 | $\text{Offset} = \text{Virtual Address} \mod \text{Page Size}$ | Within-page offset | | 6 | $\text{Physical Address} = (\text{PFN} \times \text{Page Size}) + \text{Offset}$ | Final physical address | | 7 | $\text{Tag bits} = N - B - C$ | Cache address fields | | 8 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Comparing systems | # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a 3-Level Cache **Given Problem (KTU-style):** A system has an L1 cache with hit rate 95% and hit time of 1 ns. On a miss, L2 is accessed with hit rate 90% and hit time of 5 ns. On a second miss, main memory is accessed with access time of 100 ns. Compute the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**. **Step 1 — Define all variables clearly:** $$ \begin{aligned} h_1 &= 0.95 \quad \text{(L1 hit rate)} \\ T_1 &= 1 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(L1 hit time)} \\ h_2 &= 0.90 \quad \text{(L2 hit rate, conditional on L1 miss)} \\ T_2 &= 5 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(L2 hit time)} \\ T_3 &= 100 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(Main memory access time, miss penalty for L2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write the recursive AMAT formula:** $$ \text{AMAT} = T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot \big[ T_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot T_3 \big] $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values into the formula:** $$ \text{AMAT} = 1 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot \big[ 5 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 100 \big] $$ **Step 4 — Compute the inner bracket (the L2 AMAT):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Inner bracket} &= 5 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 100 \\ &= 5 + 0.10 \cdot 100 \\ &= 5 + 10 \\ &= 15 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute back to compute the final AMAT:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT} &= 1 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 15 \\ &= 1 + 0.05 \cdot 15 \\ &= 1 + 0.75 \\ &= 1.75 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Interpret the result:** > The CPU, on average, waits only **1.75 ns** to get data — a **57× speedup** compared to going directly to main memory (100 ns). This proves why multi-level caches are indispensable in modern CPUs. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: TLB-Based Effective Access Time **Given Problem:** A system has a TLB with hit rate of 80%. Memory access time is 200 ns. TLB lookup time is 20 ns. Compute the **Effective Access Time (EAT)**. **Step 1 — Identify variables:** $$ \begin{aligned} h_{TLB} &= 0.80 \quad \text{(TLB hit rate)} \\ T_{MMU} &= 20 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(TLB lookup time)} \\ T_{mem} &= 200 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(Memory access time)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — On a TLB hit:** Only one memory access is needed (for the actual data). Time taken: $$ T_{hit} = T_{MMU} + T_{mem} = 20 + 200 = 220 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 3 — On a TLB miss:** The page table must be accessed first (cost $T_{mem}$), then the actual data is accessed again (another $T_{mem}$). Total time: $$ T_{miss} = T_{MMU} + 2 \cdot T_{mem} = 20 + 2 \cdot 200 = 420 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the EAT formula:** $$ \text{EAT} = h_{TLB} \cdot T_{hit} + (1 - h_{TLB}) \cdot T_{miss} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** $$ \text{EAT} = 0.80 \cdot 220 + 0.20 \cdot 420 $$ **Step 6 — Compute each term:** $$ \begin{aligned} 0.80 \cdot 220 &= 176 \text{ ns} \\ 0.20 \cdot 420 &= 84 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Final summation:** $$ \text{EAT} = 176 + 84 = 260 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 8 — Interpretation:** > Compared to a system with **no TLB** (which would always take $T_{MMU} + 2T_{mem} = 420$ ns), the TLB provides a speedup of $420 / 260 \approx 1.62\times$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Virtual-to-Physical Address Translation **Given Problem:** A system uses **virtual memory** with the following parameters: - Virtual address space = **32 bits** - Physical address space = **24 bits** - Page size = **4 KB** Compute: **(a)** number of virtual pages, **(b)** number of physical frames, **(c)** offset bits, **(d)** size of page table. **Step 1 — Convert page size to bits:** Page size = 4 KB = $4 \times 1024 = 4096$ bytes = $2^{12}$ bytes. Therefore, **offset bits = 12**. **Step 2 — Compute number of virtual pages:** $$ \text{Virtual pages} = \dfrac{\text{Virtual address space}}{\text{Page size}} = \dfrac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{20} $$ So there are **$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ virtual pages**, and the Virtual Page Number (VPN) field is **20 bits**. **Step 3 — Compute number of physical frames:** $$ \text{Physical frames} = \dfrac{\text{Physical address space}}{\text{Page size}} = \dfrac{2^{24}}{2^{12}} = 2^{12} $$ So there are **$2^{12} = 4096$ physical frames**, and the Physical Frame Number (PFN) field is **12 bits**. **Step 4 — Compute page table size:** Each entry must store: a PFN (12 bits) + Valid/Dirty/Protection bits (~4 bits) ≈ 16 bits = **2 bytes** (rounded up to a word = **4 bytes** per entry). $$ \text{Page table size} = \text{Number of virtual pages} \times \text{Entry size} $$ $$ \text{Page table size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 5 — Address structure summary:** $$ \underbrace{\text{20 bits}}_{\text{VPN}} \;\;\big|\;\; \underbrace{\text{12 bits}}_{\text{Offset}} $$ $$ \underbrace{\text{12 bits}}_{\text{PFN}} \;\;\big|\;\; \underbrace{\text{12 bits}}_{\text{Offset}} $$ **Step 6 — Example translation:** If virtual address = `0x0000_3F00` (hexadecimal) and the page table maps VPN = 0 to PFN = 5: $$ \text{Physical Address} = (5 \times 4096) + 0x000 = 20480 = 0x0000_5000 $$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cache Hit Rate and Speedup Calculation **Given Problem:** A CPU runs at **2 GHz**, has a base CPI of 1.0 (excluding memory), makes **1.3 memory accesses per instruction**, and has an L1 cache with **95% hit rate** and miss penalty of **200 cycles**. Compute the **actual CPI** and **execution time for 500 million instructions**. **Step 1 — Compute memory stall cycles per instruction:** $$ \text{Memory Stall Cycles} = \text{Memory accesses/instr} \times \text{Miss rate} \times \text{Miss penalty} $$ $$ \text{Memory Stall Cycles} = 1.3 \times 0.05 \times 200 = 13 \text{ cycles/instruction} $$ **Step 2 — Compute total CPI:** $$ \text{CPI}_{total} = \text{CPI}_{base} + \text{Memory Stall Cycles} = 1.0 + 13 = 14 $$ **Step 3 — Compute CPU clock cycle time:** $$ T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.5 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 4 — Compute execution time:** $$ \text{Execution Time} = \text{Instruction count} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clock} $$ $$ \text{Execution Time} = 500 \times 10^6 \times 14 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9} = 3.5 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** > Without cache, every memory access would stall 200 cycles, making the system **13× slower**. This shows the dramatic impact of even a single-level cache. ## 3.5 Numerical Insight: Why Hierarchy Is a Pyramid $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Registers} &: 0.5 \text{ ns, } 256 \text{ bytes} \rightarrow \text{cost: } \approx \$50/\text{GB equivalent} \\ \text{L1 Cache} &: 1 \text{ ns, } 32 \text{ KB} \rightarrow \text{cost: } \approx \$500/\text{GB equivalent} \\ \text{RAM (DRAM)} &: 100 \text{ ns, } 16 \text{ GB} \rightarrow \text{cost: } \approx \$5/\text{GB} \\ \text{SSD} &: 100 \text{ µs, } 1 \text{ TB} \rightarrow \text{cost: } \approx \$0.10/\text{GB} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Notice the **inverse relationship**: as access time increases by ~10× at each level, capacity also increases by ~10×, while cost per GB drops by ~100×. This is the economic + engineering foundation of the pyramid. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU["CPU CORE

VPN=42 OFFSET=10"] --> TLBlookup["TLB Lookup"] TLBlookup -->|"HIT"| PFN1["PFN=17"] TLBlookup -->|"MISS"| PT["Page Table Walk in RAM"] PT --> PFN2["PFN=17"] PFN1 --> PA["Physical Address
17 << 10 plus OFFSET"] PFN2 --> PA ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]` **Q: Explain the principle of locality of reference. Why is it essential for memory hierarchy design?** **Model Answer:** The principle of locality of reference states that programs do not access memory uniformly — instead, they exhibit **two predictable patterns**: 1. **Temporal Locality:** A memory location that has been recently accessed is likely to be accessed again in the near future. Example: a loop counter `i++` is accessed in every iteration. 2. **Spatial Locality:** Once a memory location is accessed, neighbouring locations are likely to be accessed soon. Example: sequential traversal of an array `arr[0], arr[1], arr[2]...` It is **essential** for memory hierarchy because: the CPU accesses only a small subset of data at any time. By storing this subset in **fast, small memory (cache/registers)** and the rest in **slow, large memory (RAM/disk)**, the average access time is dramatically reduced. Without locality, the hierarchy would constantly miss, defeating its purpose. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Q: Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM. Why is SRAM used in cache and DRAM in main memory?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | SRAM (Static RAM) | DRAM (Dynamic RAM) | |:-------:|:------------------:|:-------------------:| | Construction | 6 transistors per bit | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit | | Speed | **Faster** (~1–10 ns) | **Slower** (~50–100 ns) | | Density | Lower (takes more space) | **Higher** (compact) | | Cost | **Expensive** per bit | **Cheap** per bit | | Refresh | Not required | Must be refreshed every few ms | SRAM is used in **cache** because speed is critical — caches are accessed every cycle. DRAM is used in **main memory** because capacity and cost dominate — we need many GBs of memory at low cost, and the slower access time is acceptable since main memory is only accessed on cache misses. **(3 marks)** --- ## 📝 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` #### **(a) Explain the concept of memory hierarchy with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each level.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Memory hierarchy is the organisation of different storage types in a layered fashion to balance **speed, cost, and capacity**. From top (fastest) to bottom (slowest): 1. **Registers (~0.25 ns):** Built inside the CPU; store operands and addresses the ALU needs immediately. Managed by the compiler. *(1 mark)* 2. **L1 Cache (~1 ns):** Small SRAM inside the CPU core; holds recently accessed data/instructions. *(1 mark)* 3. **L2 Cache (~5 ns):** Larger SRAM, shared per core or across cores; backup for L1 misses. *(1 mark)* 4. **L3 Cache (~15 ns):** Larger still, shared across all cores; reduces DRAM accesses. *(1 mark)* 5. **Main Memory / DRAM (~100 ns):** Holds running programs and data; managed by the OS. *(1 mark)* 6. **Secondary Storage / Virtual Memory (~ms):** SSD/HDD; used as overflow when RAM is full; managed by OS via paging. *(1 mark)* The **diagram** (already shown in Section 4.1) is a **pyramid** with registers at the apex and disk at the base. **(1 mark)** for diagram + explanation of why hierarchy exists (locality). #### **(b) A system has an L1 cache with hit rate 90% and hit time 2 ns. Miss penalty (to L2) is 20 ns. Compute the AMAT. If we double the L1 size and the hit rate improves to 95%, what is the new AMAT and percentage improvement?** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Write the AMAT formula: $$ \text{AMAT} = T_{hit} + (1 - h) \times T_{miss} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the original AMAT: $$ \text{AMAT}_{old} = 2 + (1 - 0.90) \times 20 = 2 + 0.10 \times 20 = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the new AMAT: $$ \text{AMAT}_{new} = 2 + (1 - 0.95) \times 20 = 2 + 0.05 \times 20 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ns} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the percentage improvement: $$ \text{Improvement} = \dfrac{\text{AMAT}_{old} - \text{AMAT}_{new}}{\text{AMAT}_{old}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4 - 3}{4} \times 100\% = 25\% $$ **Step 5:** Interpret: > A 5% improvement in hit rate (from 90% to 95%) yields a **25% reduction in average access time** — illustrating that small hit-rate improvements have **outsized impact** on performance. **(7 marks total)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` #### **(a) What is virtual memory? Explain paging with a diagram. How does the TLB improve performance?** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Virtual Memory (1 mark):** A memory management technique where the OS uses the disk as an extension of RAM, giving each process the illusion of a large private address space. **Paging (3 marks):** Memory is divided into fixed-size blocks: - **Pages** — fixed-size blocks in virtual memory (typically 4 KB). - **Frames** — fixed-size blocks in physical memory (same size). - **Page Table** — a per-process data structure that maps virtual pages to physical frames. - **MMU** (Memory Management Unit) hardware performs the translation on every memory access. **Diagram (1 mark):** ``` Virtual Address --> [VPN | Offset] | v +---------------+ | Page Table | +---------------+ | v Physical Address --> [PFN | Offset] ``` **TLB (2 marks):** The Translation Lookaside Buffer is a small, fast associative cache inside the MMU that stores recent virtual-to-physical translations. On a TLB hit, the page table walk is avoided — saving one entire memory access. Typical TLB hit rates are >95%. #### **(b) Consider a system with 32-bit virtual address, 24-bit physical address, and page size of 4 KB. The TLB has 90% hit rate. Memory access time = 100 ns, TLB lookup time = 20 ns. Compute the Effective Access Time (EAT). Also compute the page table size assuming 4 bytes per entry.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute EAT using the standard formula: $$ \text{EAT} = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_{mem}) $$ **Step 2:** Substitute values: $$ \text{EAT} = 0.90 \cdot (20 + 100) + 0.10 \cdot (20 + 2 \cdot 100) $$ **Step 3:** Compute each term: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.90 \cdot 120 &= 108 \text{ ns} \\ 0.10 \cdot 220 &= 22 \text{ ns} \\ \text{EAT} &= 108 + 22 = 130 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute page table size. - Page size = 4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes → offset = **12 bits** - Virtual pages = $2^{32} / 2^{12} = 2^{20}$ - Page table size = $2^{20} \times 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ MB}$ **Step 5:** Final answers: - **EAT = 130 ns** - **Page Table Size = 4 MB** > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Avoid These!):** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting that on a **TLB miss**, memory is accessed **twice** — once for the page table entry and once for the actual data. Students often write `T_mem` only, losing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Mixing up **AMAT** (used for cache) and **EAT** (used for virtual memory / TLB). AMAT adds the **miss penalty** directly; EAT includes the **TLB lookup** plus memory accesses. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Not stating the **unit (ns)** in the final answer. Even with the correct numerical value, an answer without units may lose a mark in strict evaluation. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **page size** with **number of pages**. Page size = $2^n$ bytes per page; number of pages = (address space) / (page size). > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting the **diagram** in the 7-mark question — KTU explicitly awards 1 mark for a labelled diagram, and skipping it is a guaranteed loss. --- ## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **🎯 The 60-Second Revision Checklist for "Memory Hierarchy":** - ✅ **Hierarchy Order (Top→Bottom):** Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → RAM → Virtual Memory (Disk) - ✅ **Speed Rule:** Each level is roughly **5–10× slower** than the one above, but **5–10× larger** - ✅ **Cost Rule:** Each level is roughly **5–10× cheaper per byte** than the one above - ✅ **Locality:** Temporal (recently used) + Spatial (nearby locations) - ✅ **AMAT Formula:** $\text{AMAT} = T_h + (1 - h) \cdot T_m$ - ✅ **EAT Formula (TLB):** $h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_{mem}) + (1 - h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2T_{mem})$ - ✅ **Memory Stall Cycles:** accesses/instr × miss rate × miss penalty - ✅ **Page Table Size:** (# virtual pages) × (entry size, typically 4 bytes) - ✅ **Cache Mapping:** Direct (simple, high conflict), Fully Associative (best, expensive), Set-Associative (balanced) - ✅ **TLB = cache for page table entries** - ✅ **Page Size** is a power of 2 (typically 4 KB); **offset = log₂(page size)** - ✅ **Virtual Address = VPN + Offset**, **Physical Address = PFN + Offset** - ✅ **MMU does translation in hardware**; **OS handles page faults** > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 Top 5 High-Yield KTU Concepts to Memorize:** > 1. The **AMAT recursive formula** — appears in every paper. > 2. The **EAT formula with TLB** — almost guaranteed in 7-mark questions. > 3. **Locality of reference** — must be defined precisely. > 4. **Page Table size calculation** — easy marks if done correctly. > 5. **Comparison: Cache vs Virtual Memory** (speed, unit of transfer, managed by, hit/miss terminology).

topics.title: Motherboard - Computer Peripherals - I/O Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motherboard - Computer Peripherals - I/O Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Motherboard — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Motherboard)**
> The **Motherboard** (also called the *Mainboard*, *System Board*, or *Logic Board*) is the central printed circuit board (PCB) of a computer system. It is the primary platform that physically and electrically interconnects the **CPU (Central Processing Unit)**, **RAM (Random Access Memory)**, storage devices, expansion cards, and all peripheral components through a complex network of conductive **traces**, **bus interconnects**, **chipsets**, and standardized **slots/sockets**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the motherboard is treated as the **backbone substrate** of a computing system — without it, no component can communicate with another.

### Conceptual Analogy — The City Analogy

Think of a computer as a **bustling modern city**:
- The **CPU** is the *City Hall* (where all decisions are made).
- The **RAM** is the *Downtown Office District* (where active work happens).
- The **Motherboard** is the *road network, traffic signals, bridges, and power grid* of the city.
- The **Peripherals** (keyboard, printer, monitor) are the *suburbs and special districts* connected to the city.
- The **I/O Devices** are the *airports, train stations, and highways* — the gateways through which people (data) enter and leave.

Without the motherboard, the CPU is a stranded mayor with no roads, no power, and no communication lines.

---

## 1.2 Computer Peripherals — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Peripherals)**
> A **Computer Peripheral** is any auxiliary hardware device connected to a computer externally or internally that **expands** its functionality but is **not part of the core CPU-RAM-Motherboard triad**. Peripherals are categorized as **Input Peripherals**, **Output Peripherals**, **Input/Output Peripherals (I/O)**, and **Storage Peripherals**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Human Senses & Limbs

A computer's peripherals are analogous to **human senses and limbs**:
- **Eyes and Ears** = Input peripherals (Keyboard, Mouse, Microphone, Scanner)
- **Mouth and Hands** = Output peripherals (Monitor, Printer, Speakers)
- **Touch** = Touchscreen, Haptic devices (bidirectional I/O)
- **Memory (long-term)** = Storage peripherals (Hard Disk, SSD, USB Drive)

---

## 1.3 I/O Devices — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (I/O Devices)**
> **Input/Output (I/O) Devices** are a specific subclass of peripherals that perform **bidirectional data transfer** — they can both *receive* data *from* the computer (output mode) and *send* data *to* the computer (input mode). The communication is governed by standardized I/O interfaces, ports, and protocols (e.g., USB, SATA, PCIe, Thunderbolt, HDMI).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Way Radio

Imagine a **walkie-talkie**:
- You press the *Push-to-Talk* button → you **send** your voice (Input to the other radio).
- You release the button → you **receive** the other person's voice (Output to you).

An I/O device works exactly the same way — a touchscreen *outputs* a display image **and** *inputs* your finger taps; a network card *outputs* data packets **and** *inputs* incoming packets.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Metrics Used Throughout This Topic (Bolded for Quick Recall)**
> - **Bus Width:** measured in **bits** (typical: 8, 16, 32, 64 bits)
> - **Bus Frequency:** measured in **Hz** (typical: 400 MHz, 1 GHz, 8 GT/s)
> - **Data Transfer Rate (DTR):** measured in **bytes per second** (B/s), KB/s, MB/s, GB/s
> - **Form Factor standards:** **ATX (305 × 244 mm)**, **Micro-ATX (244 × 244 mm)**, **Mini-ITX (170 × 170 mm)**, **E-ATX (305 × 330 mm)**
> - **Standard I/O Port Voltages:** **USB 2.0 = 5V / 500 mA**, **USB 3.0 = 5V / 900 mA**, **USB-C PD = up to 20V / 5A (100W)**
> - **SATA Speeds:** **SATA I = 1.5 Gbps**, **SATA II = 3 Gbps**, **SATA III = 6 Gbps**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Motherboard Architecture — The Layered Anatomy

The motherboard is not a single uniform piece; it is an **orchestrated hierarchy** of subsystems. Let us dissect it layer by layer.

### 2.1.1 The Chipset — The Traffic Controller

The chipset is the **intelligence layer** of the motherboard. It is traditionally split into two logical components:

- **Northbridge (Memory Controller Hub — MCH):**
  * Manages high-speed communication between the **CPU**, **RAM**, and **PCIe graphics slots**.
  * In modern CPUs, the Northbridge functionality is **integrated into the CPU die itself** (e.g., Intel's *Memory Controller* in the CPU since Nehalem, 2008).

- **Southbridge (I/O Controller Hub — ICH / PCH):**
  * Manages **low-speed** and **medium-speed** peripherals: USB, SATA, Audio, LAN, BIOS, Real-Time Clock.
  * Always remains a **discrete chip** on the motherboard, connected to the CPU via the **DMI (Direct Media Interface)** in Intel systems or **xGMI / Infinity Fabric** in AMD systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Split?**
> The Northbridge-Southbridge split exists because high-speed components (RAM, GPU) generate massive data flows and must be physically close to the CPU to minimize latency. Low-speed peripherals (audio, USB) can tolerate a longer pathway through the Southbridge.

### 2.1.2 The BIOS / UEFI — The Bootstrap Brain

- **BIOS (Basic Input/Output System):** Legacy firmware stored in a **non-volatile ROM/Flash chip** on the motherboard. Performs the **POST (Power-On Self-Test)** and loads the bootloader.
- **UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface):** Modern replacement for BIOS, introduced in 2005. Supports **GPT (GUID Partition Table)**, **Secure Boot**, **mouse-driven GUI**, and disks larger than **2 TB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMOS Battery**
> A small **CR2032 lithium coin-cell battery (3V)** powers the **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) RAM chip** that stores BIOS settings (date, time, boot order) when the system is off. The battery typically lasts **3–10 years**.

### 2.1.3 Buses, Slots, and Sockets

- **CPU Socket:** The physical connector (e.g., Intel LGA 1700, AMD AM5). Determines which CPUs are compatible.
- **DIMM Slots:** For **DDR (Double Data Rate) RAM** modules. Modern boards use **DDR4** or **DDR5**.
- **PCIe Slots (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express):** High-speed serial lanes for GPUs, NVMe SSDs, network cards. Each generation doubles bandwidth:
  * **PCIe 3.0 ×1 = 8 Gbps** (≈ 985 MB/s)
  * **PCIe 4.0 ×1 = 16 Gbps** (≈ 1.97 GB/s)
  * **PCIe 5.0 ×1 = 32 Gbps** (≈ 3.94 GB/s)
- **M.2 Slot:** A small form-factor connector for **NVMe SSDs** and **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth cards**. Supports both **SATA** and **PCIe ×4** protocols.
- **SATA Ports:** For connecting **HDDs, SATA SSDs, and optical drives**. Current standard: **SATA III (6 Gbps)**.
- **Power Connectors:** **24-pin ATX** for the board, **8-pin EPS** for the CPU.

### 2.1.4 Form Factors — The Physical Standards

| Form Factor | Dimensions (mm) | Expansion Slots | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ATX** | 305 × 244 | 7 | Standard desktop |
| **Micro-ATX** | 244 × 244 | 4 | Budget/small desktop |
| **Mini-ITX** | 170 × 170 | 1 | Compact/HTPC builds |
| **E-ATX** | 305 × 330 | 7+ | Workstation/Server |
| **Nano-ITX** | 120 × 120 | 1 | Embedded/IoT |

> [!NOTE]
> The form factor determines **mounting hole positions**, **I/O shield layout**, and **maximum expansion capability**. Choosing the wrong case-form-factor for a motherboard-form-factor is a common build error.

---

## 2.2 Computer Peripherals — Full Classification

Peripherals are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:
1. **Direction of data flow** → Input / Output / Bidirectional (I/O)
2. **Connection topology** → Internal / External

### 2.2.1 Input Peripherals

| Device | Interface | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Keyboard** | USB / PS/2 (legacy) / Bluetooth | Text and command entry |
| **Mouse** | USB / Bluetooth / RF (2.4 GHz) | Pointing and clicking |
| **Scanner** | USB | Digitizing physical documents |
| **Microphone** | 3.5 mm jack / USB / XLR | Audio capture |
| **Webcam** | USB | Video capture |
| **Graphics Tablet** | USB | Pen-based input for designers |
| **Barcode Reader** | USB / PS/2 | Reading 1D/2D barcodes |
| **Joystick / Gamepad** | USB / Bluetooth | Gaming input |
| **Biometric Scanner** | USB | Fingerprint / retina / face |

### 2.2.2 Output Peripherals

| Device | Interface | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitor** | HDMI / DisplayPort / DVI / VGA | Visual display |
| **Printer** | USB / LAN / Wi-Fi | Hard-copy output |
| **Speakers** | 3.5 mm / USB / Bluetooth / S/PDIF | Audio output |
| **Headphones** | 3.5 mm / USB / Bluetooth | Private audio output |
| **Projector** | HDMI / VGA | Large-screen display |

### 2.2.3 Input/Output (I/O) Peripherals

| Device | Interface | Input Mode | Output Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Touchscreen** | USB / Internal | Touch coordinates | Display image |
| **Network Card (NIC)** | PCIe / M.2 | Incoming packets | Outgoing packets |
| **Modem** | RJ-11 / PCIe | Download data | Upload data |
| **Multifunction Printer** | USB / LAN | Scans / faxes | Prints |
| **External SSD** | USB-C / Thunderbolt | Reads from host | Writes to host |
| **Headset (with mic)** | 3.5 mm / USB | Microphone audio | Headphone audio |

### 2.2.4 Storage Peripherals (a Sub-class)

> Storage peripherals are technically I/O devices, but the KTU syllabus often lists them separately due to their **persistence** characteristic.

- **HDD (Hard Disk Drive):** Magnetic spinning platters, capacity up to **20 TB**, interface **SATA III** or **SAS**.
- **SSD (Solid State Drive):** NAND flash memory, no moving parts, much faster than HDD.
- **NVMe SSD:** Uses **PCIe lanes** (typically ×4), bandwidth up to **7+ GB/s** (PCIe 4.0).
- **Optical Drives:** CD / DVD / Blu-ray, interface SATA, max Blu-ray speed **6× = 216 Mbps**.
- **USB Flash Drive:** Portable NAND flash, USB 3.2 speeds up to **20 Gbps** (USB 3.2 Gen 2×2).
- **Memory Card:** SD, microSD used in cameras and phones.
- **Tape Drive:** Used in enterprise backups, capacities in **TB range**.

---

## 2.3 I/O Communication — How the CPU Talks to the World

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core I/O Principle**
> The CPU does **not** directly drive peripherals. Communication happens through a **layered stack**:
> **Application Program → Operating System → Device Driver → Controller Chip → Bus → Peripheral Device**

### 2.3.1 I/O Methods (Examined at Architecture Level)

1. **Programmed I/O (Polling):** The CPU continuously checks a status register — *busy-waiting*. Simple but CPU-wasteful.
2. **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** The peripheral raises an **IRQ (Interrupt Request)** line; the CPU services it via the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Efficient and widely used.
3. **DMA (Direct Memory Access):** A dedicated **DMA controller** transfers data between peripheral and RAM **without CPU involvement**, freeing the CPU. Used by HDDs, SSDs, NICs, sound cards.
4. **Channel I/O:** Used in mainframe systems; the I/O channel is a specialized processor.

### 2.3.2 I/O Port Types (Physical Connectors)

| Port Type | Typical Use | Hot-Pluggable? |
|---|---|---|
| **USB-A 2.0/3.0/3.1** | Keyboard, mouse, flash drive | Yes |
| **USB-C / Thunderbolt 4** | Modern universal connector | Yes |
| **HDMI 2.1** | Monitor, TV | Yes |
| **DisplayPort 1.4** | Monitor, multi-display | Yes |
| **RJ-45 (Ethernet)** | Wired network (Cat 6 = 10 Gbps) | Yes |
| **3.5 mm Audio Jack** | Headphones, mic | Yes |
| **PS/2** | Legacy keyboard/mouse | No |
| **VGA / DVI** | Legacy monitors | No |
| **SATA** | Internal HDD/SSD | No |
| **M.2** | Internal NVMe SSD | No |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Bus & Data Transfer)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most-tested formulas** in KTU university exams for this topic. Memorize the structure, not just the numbers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bus Bandwidth (DTR)** | $\text{DTR} = \text{Bus Width} \times \text{Bus Frequency}$ | bits/s | Divide by 8 for bytes/s |
| 2 | **PCIe Lane Bandwidth** | $\text{Lane BW} = \text{GT/s} \times \text{Encoding Factor}$ | Gbps | PCIe uses **128b/130b** (Gen 3/4/5) → factor ≈ 0.985 |
| 3 | **Total PCIe Bandwidth** | $\text{Total BW} = \text{Lane BW} \times \text{Number of Lanes}$ | Gbps | Lanes = ×1, ×4, ×8, ×16 |
| 4 | **Memory Bandwidth** | $\text{Mem BW} = \text{Bus Width} \times \text{Mem Clock} \times 2^{\text{(DDR multiplier)}}$ | bytes/s | DDR4/DDR5 doubles per cycle |
| 5 | **Effective SATA Speed** | $\text{Effective} = \text{Raw Speed} \times 0.80$ | MB/s | SATA III → ≈ 600 MB/s raw, ≈ 480 MB/s effective |
| 6 | **USB Raw vs Effective** | $\text{Effective} \approx \text{Raw} \times 0.80$ | MB/s | USB 3.0 = 5 Gbps raw → ≈ 400 MB/s effective |
| 7 | **File Transfer Time** | $\text{Time} = \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Transfer Rate (MB/s)}}$ | seconds | Add 10% overhead for protocol |
| 8 | **Throughput Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Data}}{\text{Total Data Transferred}} \times 100$ | % | Always $\leq 100$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **system integration engineering**, **data center design**, **embedded systems bandwidth planning**, and **IoT firmware optimization**. A network engineer sizing a server's NIC must know that **PCIe 3.0 ×4** can sustain at most ≈ 3.5 GB/s, so a 10 GbE NIC saturates comfortably but a 100 GbE NIC requires **PCIe 4.0 ×16**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **worked-out in full**. KTU examiners award **partial marks** for every correct intermediate step, so even if your final answer slips, these transitions carry you safely to a 5–6 mark band.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PCIe Bandwidth From First Principles

### Problem Statement
> A graphics card is connected via **PCIe 4.0 ×16**. The signaling rate is **16 GT/s (Giga-Transfers per second)**. Calculate the **raw bandwidth in Gbps** and the **effective bandwidth in GB/s**.

### Step 1: Establish the Encoding Scheme

PCIe Generation 3, 4, and 5 use **128b/130b encoding** to embed clock information into the data stream. This means:
- For every **130 bits** transmitted on the wire, only **128 bits** are actual data.
- The remaining 2 bits are protocol overhead.

$$\text{Encoding Efficiency} = \frac{128}{130}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Raw Bits per Second per Lane

$$\text{Raw Bit Rate per Lane} = 16 \text{ GT/s} \times 1 \text{ bit/transfer} = 16 \text{ Gbps}$$

> **Logic:** Each transfer carries 1 bit (PCIe is serial). At 16 billion transfers per second, we get 16 billion bits per second.

---

### Step 3: Apply the Encoding Overhead

$$\text{Effective Bit Rate per Lane} = 16 \text{ Gbps} \times \frac{128}{130}$$

$$\text{Effective Bit Rate per Lane} = 16 \times 0.9846 \text{ Gbps} = 15.754 \text{ Gbps}$$

> **Logic:** Only 98.46% of the wire-rate carries payload; the rest is framing overhead.

---

### Step 4: Multiply by the Number of Lanes (×16)

$$\text{Total Effective Bandwidth} = 15.754 \text{ Gbps} \times 16$$

$$\text{Total Effective Bandwidth} = 252.06 \text{ Gbps}$$

---

### Step 5: Convert Gbps to GB/s

$$\text{GB/s} = \frac{\text{Gbps}}{8} = \frac{252.06}{8}$$

$$\text{GB/s} = 31.51 \text{ GB/s}$$

> **Logic:** There are 8 bits in 1 byte, so we divide by 8.

---

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{PCIe 4.0} \times 16 \text{ delivers} \approx 31.5 \text{ GB/s effective bidirectional bandwidth}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** KTU problems often quote the *raw* speed. Always check the question — if it says *raw*, use 16 Gbps × 16 = 256 Gbps = **32 GB/s**. If it says *effective*, use 31.5 GB/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Memory Bandwidth of DDR4-3200

### Problem Statement
> A motherboard has **dual-channel DDR4-3200** RAM. Each module has a **64-bit (8-byte) bus width**. Calculate the **peak theoretical memory bandwidth in GB/s**.

### Step 1: Understand DDR's "Double Data Rate"

The "3200" in DDR4-3200 refers to the **transfer rate in MT/s (Mega-Transfers per second)**, not the actual clock frequency.

$$\text{Transfer Rate} = 3200 \text{ MT/s}$$

$$\text{Base Clock} = \frac{3200}{2} = 1600 \text{ MHz}$$

> **Logic:** DDR transfers data **twice per clock cycle** (once on the rising edge, once on the falling edge), so the effective transfer rate is double the base clock.

---

### Step 2: Compute Bytes per Second per Channel

$$\text{Bandwidth per Channel} = \text{Transfer Rate} \times \text{Bus Width (in bytes)}$$

$$\text{Bandwidth per Channel} = 3200 \times 10^6 \text{ transfers/s} \times 8 \text{ bytes/transfer}$$

$$\text{Bandwidth per Channel} = 25.6 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes/s} = 25.6 \text{ GB/s}$$

---

### Step 3: Account for Dual-Channel Architecture

In **dual-channel** mode, the memory controller accesses **two modules simultaneously**, doubling the effective bus width.

$$\text{Total Bandwidth} = 2 \times 25.6 \text{ GB/s}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Bandwidth} = 51.2 \text{ GB/s}}$$

---

### Step 4: Real-World Efficiency (Optional Add-on)

Real-world efficiency is typically **70–80%** of peak:

$$\text{Realistic Bandwidth} = 51.2 \times 0.75 \approx 38.4 \text{ GB/s}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why video editing and 3D rendering software scale beautifully with dual/quad-channel memory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — File Transfer Time Over USB 3.0

### Problem Statement
> A user copies a **4 GB** file from a USB 3.0 flash drive to the computer. The USB 3.0 raw bit rate is **5 Gbps**. Calculate the **theoretical minimum transfer time**.

### Step 1: Convert File Size to Bits

$$\text{File Size in Bits} = 4 \text{ GB} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \times 1024^3 \text{ bytes/GB}$$

$$\text{File Size} = 4 \times 8 \times 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{File Size} = 34{,}359{,}738{,}368 \text{ bits} \approx 34.36 \text{ Gbits}$$

---

### Step 2: Apply Effective Speed (80% Rule)

USB protocols have **≈20% protocol overhead** (packet headers, acknowledgments, bus arbitration):

$$\text{Effective Rate} = 5 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.80 = 4.0 \text{ Gbps}$$

---

### Step 3: Compute Transfer Time

$$\text{Time} = \frac{\text{File Size (bits)}}{\text{Effective Rate (bps)}}$$

$$\text{Time} = \frac{34.36 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}}{4.0 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}}$$

$$\text{Time} = 8.59 \text{ seconds}$$

---

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\text{4 GB file via USB 3.0} \approx 8.6 \text{ seconds (theoretical minimum)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world check:** The actual time is usually **10–12 seconds** because flash drives have internal write latency and the OS adds filesystem overhead.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — USB-C Power Delivery (PD) Budget

### Problem Statement
> A laptop supports **USB-C PD up to 100 W**. The negotiated voltage is **20 V**. Calculate the **maximum current draw** and verify against the **USB-C cable rating (5 A)**.

### Step 1: Recall the Power Equation

$$P = V \times I$$

---

### Step 2: Solve for Current

$$I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{100 \text{ W}}{20 \text{ V}}$$

$$I = 5 \text{ A}$$

---

### Step 3: Verify Against Cable Specification

The standard **USB-C cable** is rated for **5 A continuous current**. The 5 A draw is at the **maximum rated limit**, so the system negotiates exactly to the safe boundary.

$$\boxed{I_{\max} = 5 \text{ A at 20 V} = 100 \text{ W (USB-C PD upper limit)}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why cheap, non-e-marked USB-C cables can melt when used with high-wattage chargers — they cannot safely carry 5 A.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| PCIe bandwidth | Gen, Lanes | GB/s | $\text{Lanes} \times \text{GT/s} \times \frac{128}{130 \times 8}$ |
| Memory bandwidth | Type, Channel | GB/s | $\text{Channels} \times \frac{\text{MT/s} \times \text{Bus Bytes}}{10^9}$ |
| Transfer time | Size, Speed | Seconds | $\dfrac{\text{Size (MB)}}{\text{Rate (MB/s)}}$ |
| Power budget | V, W | A | $I = \dfrac{P}{V}$ |
| SATA effective | Raw Gbps | Effective MB/s | $\text{Raw} \times \frac{1000}{8} \times 0.80$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Motherboard Block Architecture

The diagram below maps the **functional interconnections** between the major motherboard subsystems, viewed from a top-down architecture perspective.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR[24-Pin ATX Power Connector + 8-Pin EPS]:::power
    CPU[CPU Socket - LGA 1700 or AM5]:::core
    NB[Integrated Memory Controller IMC inside CPU]:::core
    RAM[DIMM Slots - DDR4 or DDR5]:::memory
    GPU[PCIe x16 Slot - for Graphics Card]:::expansion
    M2A[M.2 Slot 1 - PCIe x4 NVMe]:::expansion
    M2B[M.2 Slot 2 - SATA or PCIe]:::expansion
    PCH[Southbridge PCH Chip]:::io
    SIO[Super I-O Chip]:::io
    BIOS[BIOS or UEFI Flash Chip]:::firmware
    CMOS[CMOS Battery CR2032 + RTC]:::firmware
    SATA1[SATA Port 1]:::storage
    SATA2[SATA Port 2]:::storage
    USB[USB Ports USB 2.0 3.0 3.1 Type-C]:::io
    LAN[Gigabit Ethernet RJ-45]:::io
    AUD[HD Audio Codec + Jacks]:::io
    FAN[CPU Fan + Chassis Fan Headers]:::io
    BUS_PCH[DMI Link to PCH]:::bus
    BIOS_BUS[SPI Bus to BIOS]:::bus
    LPC[LPC Bus to Super I-O]:::bus

    PWR --> CPU
    PWR --> RAM
    PWR --> PCH
    CPU --> NB
    NB <--> RAM
    CPU <--> GPU
    CPU <--> M2A
    PCH <--> M2B
    CPU <--> BUS_PCH
    BUS_PCH --> PCH
    PCH <--> SATA1
    PCH <--> SATA2
    PCH <--> USB
    PCH <--> LAN
    PCH <--> AUD
    PCH <--> FAN
    PCH <--> BIOS_BUS
    BIOS_BUS --> BIOS
    BIOS --> CMOS
    PCH <--> LPC
    LPC --> SIO

    classDef power fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef expansion fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#5a2d82,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef firmware fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#fefefe,stroke:#333,stroke-dasharray: 5 5,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows indicate **active data pathways**. Dashed-line boxes (`bus`) represent **control/command buses** rather than bulk data paths. The CPU contains the integrated memory controller (modern architecture) and connects to the PCH (Southbridge) via the **DMI (Direct Media Interface)** in Intel systems.

---

## 4.2 I/O Device Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PER[Computer Peripherals]:::root
    IN[Input Devices]:::input
    OUT[Output Devices]:::output
    IO[Input Output Bidirectional Devices]:::bidir
    STO[Storage Devices]:::storage

    PER --> IN
    PER --> OUT
    PER --> IO
    PER --> STO

    IN --> IN1[Keyboard]
    IN --> IN2[Mouse]
    IN --> IN3[Scanner]
    IN --> IN4[Microphone]
    IN --> IN5[Webcam]
    IN --> IN6[Barcode Reader]

    OUT --> OUT1[Monitor]
    OUT --> OUT2[Printer]
    OUT --> OUT3[Speakers]
    OUT --> OUT4[Projector]
    OUT --> OUT5[Headphones]

    IO --> IO1[Touchscreen]
    IO --> IO2[Network Card NIC]
    IO --> IO3[Modem]
    IO --> IO4[External SSD]
    IO --> IO5[Multifunction Printer]
    IO --> IO6[Headset with Microphone]

    STO --> STO1[HDD Hard Disk]
    STO --> STO2[SSD Solid State]
    STO --> STO3[NVMe M.2]
    STO --> STO4[USB Flash Drive]
    STO --> STO5[Optical CD DVD Blu-ray]
    STO --> STO6[Memory Card SD microSD]

    classDef root fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
    classDef bidir fill:#b4a7d6,stroke:#351c75,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 CPU–I/O Communication Stack (Layered Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Program - Word, Chrome]:::app
    B[Operating System - Windows, Linux]:::os
    C[Device Driver - Keyboard.sys]:::drv
    D[Controller Chip - USB Host Controller in PCH]:::ctrl
    E[Physical Bus - USB 3.0 Differential Pair]:::bus
    F[Peripheral Device - External Keyboard]:::dev

    A -->|System Call API| B
    B -->|IRP I/O Request Packet| C
    C -->|Memory-Mapped or Port I/O| D
    D -->|Electrical Signals Differential| E
    E -->|Voltage Transitions| F
    F -.->|Scancode Data Back| E
    E -.->|Differential Signaling| D
    D -.->|Interrupt IRQ to CPU| C
    C -.->|Data Passed Up| B
    B -.->|Delivered to App| A

    classDef app fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000
    classDef os fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#741b47,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,stroke-dasharray: 4 4,color:#000
    classDef dev fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#741b47,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the bidirectional flow:** Data flows *down* the stack when the user presses a key, and *up* the stack when the resulting character reaches the application. This is the essence of **layered I/O architecture**.

---

## 4.4 Interrupt-Driven I/O Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant PCH as PCH Chip
    participant KBD as Keyboard
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine
    participant APP as Application

    Note over CPU,KBD: System is running normally. CPU is executing other tasks.
    KBD->>PCH: User presses 'A' key. Scancode 0x1C arrives.
    PCH->>CPU: Raise IRQ #1 line (High voltage)
    CPU->>CPU: Suspend current task, save state
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to ISR for IRQ #1
    ISR->>PCH: Read scancode from port 0x60
    PCH-->>ISR: Returns 0x1C
    ISR->>ISR: Convert scancode to ASCII 'A'
    ISR->>APP: Place 'A' into keyboard buffer
    ISR->>CPU: Send EOI (End of Interrupt) signal
    CPU->>CPU: Resume previously suspended task
    Note over CPU,APP: Total interrupt latency: typically < 10 microseconds
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
