### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **I/O Management Hierarchy:** Application → OS Independent Layer → Device Driver → Controller → Physical Device.
- **Three I/O Techniques:** Programmed I/O (polling, simple but wasteful), Interrupt-driven I/O (event-based, balanced), DMA (bulk transfer, fastest).
- **DMA Modes:** Burst (fastest, blocks CPU), Cycle Stealing (CPU and DMA share bus), Transparent (DMA waits for CPU idle).
- **Addressing:** MMIO shares the memory address space; PMIO uses a separate I/O port space. For an *A*-bit address bus, **$2^{A}$ ports** are addressable.
- **Key Disk Formulas:**
  - $T_{rot} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{RPM}$ ms
  - $T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$
  - $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Data}}{\text{Total Time}}$
- **CPU Utilization:** $\eta_{CPU} = 1 - P \times T_{IO}$ for a single device.
- **OS Functions:** Buffering, Spooling, Caching, Scheduling, Error Handling, Protection.
- **I/O Scheduling Algorithms:** FCFS, SSTF, SCAN (elevator), C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK.
- **Interrupt Latency:** Time from IRQ raised to ISR start — keep below **10 μs** for real-time systems.
- **KTU Mnemonic — "PIG-DMA":** **P**olling, **I**nterrupt, **G**eneral, **D**irect **M**emory **A**ccess — the canonical order from simplest to most complex.
- **High-yield numbers to remember:** 7200 RPM → 4.17 ms rotational latency; 10,000 RPM → 3.0 ms; 15,000 RPM → 2.0 ms.
- **Standard sector sizes:** 512 bytes (legacy HDD) and 4096 bytes (4K Advanced Format, modern HDD/SSD).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface Cards - Buses - Firmware - Boot Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Interface Cards - Buses - Firmware - Boot Process # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Interface Cards (Expansion / I/O Cards) ### Formal KTU Definition An **Interface Card** (also called an *expansion card*, *add-in card*, or *peripheral card*) is a printed circuit board (PCB) that is inserted into an expansion slot on the computer's motherboard to provide additional functionality or to act as an interface between the CPU and external peripherals. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, these are the modular components that extend the base capability of the system unit. ### Real-World Analogy Think of your motherboard as a **municipal office building** with pre-built rooms (CPU, RAM). An interface card is like a **specialized kiosk** that you bolt onto the building whenever the office needs a new service: a passport counter, a bill payment window, or a help desk. Each kiosk speaks a specific language to its own customers (drivers) and connects to the building's main hallway (the bus). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Interface cards are physical hardware modules that *expand* I/O capability beyond what the chipset natively offers. ### Common Types of Interface Cards - **GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) Card** – Renders video output to displays. - **NIC (Network Interface Card)** – Connects to LAN/WAN via Ethernet or Wi-Fi. - **Sound Card** – Processes audio I/O (largely integrated today). - **Storage Controller Card** – Manages SATA, SAS, or NVMe drives. - **USB Expansion Card** – Adds extra USB ports. - **Capture Card / TV Tuner Card** – Ingests video signals. > [!IMPORTANT] > Modern interface cards use the **PCI Express (PCIe)** standard. Legacy standards such as **ISA**, **VESA**, and **AGP** are now obsolete, though they remain in the KTU historical syllabus for context. --- ## 1.2 Buses ### Formal KTU Definition A **Computer Bus** is a communication subsystem that transfers data between computer components — either between the CPU and memory (system bus) or between the CPU and peripheral devices (I/O or expansion bus). A bus is fundamentally a *shared set of wires or signal traces* governed by a bus protocol. ### Real-World Analogy Imagine a **multi-lane highway system**: - **Data Bus** = Cargo trucks (carrying the goods). - **Address Bus** = GPS coordinates on the truck's dashboard (telling the destination). - **Control Bus** = Traffic lights and road signs (managing flow). - The **Bus Width** = Number of lanes; the **Bus Speed (MHz)** = Speed limit. ### The Three Sub-Buses of the System Bus 1. **Data Bus** – Bidirectional. Carries the actual data values. Its width (e.g., **32-bit**, **64-bit**) defines how much data travels per cycle. 2. **Address Bus** – Unidirectional (CPU → Memory). Carries memory locations. Its width determines the addressable memory (e.g., **32-bit address bus = 2³² = 4 GB**). 3. **Control Bus** – Bidirectional. Carries control signals such as *Read/Write*, *Interrupt*, *Clock*, *Reset*, *Bus Request*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric:** Bus Bandwidth = Bus Width (bits) × Bus Frequency (Hz) ÷ 8 = Bytes/sec. This is the *theoretical maximum data transfer rate*. ### Classification of Buses - **Internal (System) Bus** – Between CPU, RAM, chipset. - **External (Expansion) Bus** – Between CPU and peripherals (PCIe, USB). - **Serial vs Parallel** – Serial transmits bits one at a time (USB, SATA, PCIe); Parallel transmits multiple bits simultaneously (old PCI, ISA). - **Synchronous vs Asynchronous** – Synchronous is clocked; asynchronous is event-driven. --- ## 1.3 Firmware ### Formal KTU Definition **Firmware** is a specific class of software that is permanently programmed into a non-volatile memory device (ROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) and provides the low-level control instructions for the hardware. In personal computers, the most famous firmware is the **BIOS** or its modern successor **UEFI**. ### Real-World Analogy Firmware is the **DNA of a device** — a pre-installed "operating manual" written into the device's memory at the factory. When you buy a microwave, before any user input is needed, firmware already knows how to drive the magnetron, the timer, and the keypad. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Firmware sits *below* the Operating System (OS) in the system software hierarchy. The OS is loaded *by* the firmware; firmware is loaded *by* the hardware. ### Key Properties of Firmware - Stored in **non-volatile memory** (retains data without power). - Tightly coupled to specific hardware. - Rarely updated by end-users (but updates exist, e.g., *BIOS flash*). - Provides **bootstrapping** — the initial instructions a CPU executes on power-up. ### Types of ROM Used for Firmware - **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** – Mask-programmed at factory, cannot be changed. - **PROM (Programmable ROM)** – One-time programmable by the user. - **EPROM (Erasable PROM)** – Erasable via UV light, then re-programmable. - **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM)** – Erasable electronically, byte-by-byte. - **Flash Memory** – A modern type of EEPROM erased in blocks; used for **BIOS/UEFI** today. --- ## 1.4 Boot Process ### Formal KTU Definition The **Boot Process** (or *Bootstrapping*) is the sequence of operations a computer performs from the moment power is applied until the operating system is fully loaded and ready to accept user input. It is a multi-stage firmware-driven procedure. ### Real-World Analogy Booting is like **waking up a deep sleeper**: 1. **Alarm rings** (Power button pressed). 2. **Stretch and check surroundings** (POST — Power-On Self-Test). 3. **Recall where you kept the morning routine** (BIOS/UEFI loads boot device). 4. **Execute the day's plan** (OS Kernel loads). 5. **Greet the world** (User Login Screen). > [!IMPORTANT] > The term *bootstrap* comes from the phrase *"pulling oneself up by one's own bootstraps"* — because the computer loads an OS using a tiny pre-loaded program before the OS exists in memory. ### The Five Major Stages of the Modern Boot Process 1. **Power Supply & CPU Reset** – Motherboard stabilizes voltages; CPU begins at reset vector. 2. **POST (Power-On Self-Test)** – Firmware checks RAM, drives, keyboard, video. 3. **Firmware Configuration Load** – BIOS/UEFI settings are read from CMOS. 4. **Boot Device Selection & OS Loader** – MBR/GPT is read, then OS bootloader (e.g., GRUB, Windows Boot Manager). 5. **Kernel Initialization & User Space** – OS kernel loads drivers, mounts filesystems, starts `init`/`systemd`, presents login. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Interface Cards — Deeper Dive ### Architecture of an Expansion Card Every interface card consists of: - **Edge Connector / Gold Fingers** – Contacts that insert into the slot. - **Bracket** – Metal tab that screws into the chassis (also exposes external ports). - **On-board Controller Chip** – The "brain" of the card (e.g., Realtek NIC chip). - **Optional External Connector** – HDMI, RJ-45, USB, SATA, etc. - **Memory** – Some cards have local VRAM (GPU) or buffer memory. - **Heat Sink / Fan** – For thermal dissipation on high-power cards. ### Plug-and-Play (PnP) Modern cards support **Plug-and-Play**, meaning the OS detects, configures, and loads drivers automatically. The BIOS/UEFI assigns IRQ lines, I/O ports, and memory addresses dynamically. > [!NOTE] > The OS communicates with interface cards through **device drivers** — software translators between OS and hardware. The card itself is dumb without its driver. ### Modern Slot Types and Their Roles - **PCIe x16** – Primarily for GPUs. - **PCIe x4 / x8** – For NVMe SSDs, high-speed NICs. - **PCIe x1** – For low-bandwidth cards (USB, sound). - **M.2 Slot** – For compact wireless cards, NVMe SSDs (technically a PCIe + USB/PCIe hybrid socket). --- ## 2.2 Buses — Deeper Dive ### Bus Architecture Patterns 1. **Single Bus Architecture** – All components share one bus. Simple but creates a bottleneck (used in early computers). 2. **Hierarchical (Multi-Bus) Architecture** – A high-speed local bus connects CPU/RAM; a slower I/O bus connects peripherals via a *bridge*. This is the modern norm (Northbridge/Southbridge, or modern SoC). 3. **Switched Fabric** – A crossbar switch replaces shared bus; used in modern PCIe and networks. ### Bus Arbitration When multiple devices want to use the bus, an **arbiter** decides who goes first. Methods include: - **Daisy Chain Arbitration** – Priority by physical position. - **Centralized Parallel Arbitration** – Dedicated request/grant lines. - **Distributed Arbitration** – Devices self-arbitrate using a collision-detection bus. ### Modern Bus Standards — Snapshot Table | Standard | Type | Speed | Notes | |----------|------|-------|-------| | **PCIe 1.0** | Serial | 2.5 GT/s per lane | 250 MB/s per lane | | **PCIe 4.0** | Serial | 16 GT/s per lane | ~2 GB/s per lane | | **SATA III** | Serial | 6 Gbps | For HDDs/SSDs | | **NVMe** | Serial (PCIe) | Up to 32 GT/s (PCIe 5) | For modern SSDs | | **USB 3.2 Gen 2x2** | Serial | 20 Gbps | For peripherals | | **DDR5** | Parallel | Up to 6400 MT/s | RAM bus | ### The Formula: PCIe Bandwidth A PCIe link is built of **lanes** (x1, x4, x8, x16). Each lane is a separate differential pair for sending and receiving. $$\text{Bandwidth} = \text{Encoding Overhead} \times \text{Lanes} \times \text{Raw Bit Rate} \div 8$$ PCIe uses **128b/130b encoding** (Gen 3+), so the overhead ratio is $\frac{128}{130}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical takeaway:** A PCIe 3.0 x16 slot (used by GPUs) has ~15.75 GB/s of bandwidth — more than enough for current-generation GPUs. --- ## 2.3 Firmware — Deeper Dive ### BIOS vs UEFI | Feature | BIOS (Legacy) | UEFI (Modern) | |---------|---------------|---------------| | Mode | 16-bit Real Mode | 32/64-bit Protected Mode | | Max Boot Drive | 2.2 TB (MBR limit) | 9.4 ZB (GPT limit) | | Boot Speed | Slower | Faster (skip POST on warm boots) | | Interface | Text menus | Graphical, mouse-driven | | Secure Boot | No | Yes (cryptographic verification) | | Networking | No | Yes (HTTP/PXE boot) | ### CMOS Memory The BIOS/UEFI configuration (time, boot order, password) is stored in a tiny **CMOS RAM** chip powered by a **coin-cell battery** (typically CR2032, **3 V**). If the battery dies, settings revert to defaults. ### Firmware Update ("Flashing") Updating firmware writes new code to the Flash chip. Risky: a power loss during flashing can render the motherboard unusable ("bricked"). > [!WARNING] > Always flash firmware with a UPS or full battery. A failed flash = dead motherboard. --- ## 2.4 Boot Process — Deeper Dive ### Stage-by-Stage Memory Map On reset, the x86 CPU fetches its first instruction from a hardwired address called the **Reset Vector**: $$\text{Reset Vector} = \texttt{0xFFFF:0x0000} \quad (\text{Real Mode, 16-bit}) = \texttt{0xFFFFFFF0} \quad (\text{64-bit mode})$$ This is where firmware code begins. ### MBR vs GPT Partitioning - **MBR (Master Boot Record)** – 512 bytes at sector 0. First 446 bytes = bootloader; next 64 bytes = partition table (max 4 primary partitions); last 2 bytes = magic number `\texttt{0x55AA}`. - **GPT (GUID Partition Table)** – Modern. Supports up to **128 partitions**, drives >2 TB, includes backup table and CRC32 checksums. ### Bootloaders - **Windows:** Windows Boot Manager → `winload.exe` → loads `ntoskrnl.exe`. - **Linux:** GRUB2 → loads `vmlinuz` (kernel) and `initramfs` (initial RAM disk). - **macOS:** boot.efi (directly via UEFI). ### Kernel Initialization After loading, the kernel: 1. Initializes core hardware. 2. Mounts the root filesystem. 3. Starts `init` (PID 1) or `systemd`. 4. Begins user-space services. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | |---------|-----------------|------| | Addressable Memory | $2^n$ where $n$ = address bus width | Bytes | | Bus Bandwidth | $\frac{\text{Width} \times \text{Frequency}}{8}$ | Bytes/sec | | PCIe Gen $g$ per lane | $(2^{g+1}) \times 10^8 \times \frac{128}{130}$ Bytes/s (Gen 3+) | Bytes/s | | MBR Max Disk | $2^{32} \times 512$ bytes $\approx 2.2$ TB | TB | | MBR Max Partitions | 4 primary (extendable via EBR) | Count | | GPT Max Disk (theoretical) | $2^{64}$ sectors | Bytes | | GPT Max Partitions | 128 (default) | Count | | CMOS Battery Voltage | **3 V** (CR2032) | V | | POST Memory Test Size | Equals installed RAM | Bytes | > [!NOTE] > For derivations of address space and PCIe bandwidth, see Section 3. ### Real-World Utility - **Embedded systems** (IoT, cars, ATMs) rely heavily on custom firmware; understanding boot flow is essential for debugging bricking. - **Cybersecurity** – Firmware rootkits (e.g., LoJax) are persistent because they hide below the OS; understanding UEFI Secure Boot is critical. - **Performance tuning** – Knowing PCIe lanes helps in choosing GPU vs NVMe placement to avoid bottlenecks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width ### Problem Statement Given an address bus of width $n$ bits, derive the maximum addressable memory. ### Step-by-Step Derivation Each address line can carry one of two states: logic 0 or logic 1. The number of unique combinations of $n$ lines is: $$N_{\text{addresses}} = 2^n$$ Because each address corresponds to exactly **one memory byte** (in byte-addressable architectures), the maximum addressable memory is: $$M_{\text{addressable}} = N_{\text{addresses}} \times 1 \text{ byte} = 2^n \text{ bytes}$$ ### Worked Example (32-bit Address Bus) $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{addressable}} &= 2^{32} \text{ bytes} \\ &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\ &= \frac{4{,}294{,}967{,}296}{1024^2} \text{ MiB} \\ &= 4{,}096 \text{ MiB} \\ &= 4 \text{ GiB} \end{aligned}$$ ### Worked Example (64-bit Address Bus — Theoretical) $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{addressable}} &= 2^{64} \text{ bytes} \\ &= 16 \text{ Exabytes (EB)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > Practical 64-bit CPUs (e.g., AMD64, ARMv8) use only **48-bit** virtual addressing, giving 256 TB of virtual address space — still enormous. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PCIe Bandwidth per Lane ### Problem Statement Compute the theoretical bandwidth of a single PCIe 3.0 lane, and of a PCIe 3.0 x16 slot (typical GPU slot). ### Given - PCIe 3.0 raw bit rate per lane = **8 GT/s** (GigaTransfers per second). - Encoding: **128b/130b** (PCIe Gen 3 and later). ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Convert GT/s to Gbps. PCIe serializes data at 8 GigaTransfers per second, and each transfer on PCIe Gen 3 carries 1 bit per direction (across the differential pair). So: $$R_{\text{raw}} = 8 \text{ GT/s} \times 1 \text{ bit/transfer} = 8 \text{ Gbps}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the encoding overhead. 128b/130b encoding sends 130 bits on the wire to carry 128 bits of data. The useful data rate is: $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{data}} &= R_{\text{raw}} \times \frac{128}{130} \\ &= 8 \text{ Gbps} \times 0.9846 \\ &\approx 7.877 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to Bytes per second. $$R_{\text{data, B/s}} = \frac{7.877 \text{ Gbps}}{8} = 0.9846 \text{ GB/s} \approx 985 \text{ MB/s}$$ **Step 4:** For an x16 link, multiply by 16. $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{x16}} &= 16 \times 0.9846 \text{ GB/s} \\ &= 15.75 \text{ GB/s (single direction)} \end{aligned}$$ PCIe is full-duplex (separate transmit and receive differential pairs), so aggregate is **~31.5 GB/s** in both directions combined. ### Summary Table — PCIe Lane Bandwidths | PCIe Version | Raw Rate | Encoding | Bandwidth/lane | x16 Total (one-way) | |--------------|----------|----------|----------------|---------------------| | PCIe 1.0 | 2.5 GT/s | 8b/10b | ~250 MB/s | ~4.0 GB/s | | PCIe 2.0 | 5.0 GT/s | 8b/10b | ~500 MB/s | ~8.0 GB/s | | **PCIe 3.0** | **8.0 GT/s** | **128b/130b** | **~985 MB/s** | **~15.75 GB/s** | | PCIe 4.0 | 16 GT/s | 128b/130b | ~1.97 GB/s | ~31.5 GB/s | | PCIe 5.0 | 32 GT/s | 128b/130b | ~3.94 GB/s | ~63 GB/s | --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CMOS Battery Backup Time Estimation ### Problem Statement A CR2032 coin cell has a capacity of approximately **220 mAh** at **3 V**. The CMOS/RTC circuit draws **5 µA** continuously. Estimate the theoretical battery life. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Convert capacity to coulombs or ampere-hours. $$Q = 220 \text{ mAh} = 0.220 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the discharge equation: $$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{Q}{I} \\ &= \frac{0.220 \text{ Ah}}{5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}} \\ &= \frac{0.220}{0.000005} \text{ hours} \\ &= 44{,}000 \text{ hours} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to years. $$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{44{,}000}{24 \times 365} \\ &= \frac{44{,}000}{8{,}760} \\ &\approx 5.02 \text{ years} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > Real-world life is shorter (typically **3–5 years**) due to self-discharge, temperature, and other leakage paths. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Legacy vs Modern Boot | Aspect | Legacy BIOS Boot | UEFI Boot | |--------|-------------------|-----------| | CPU Mode at start | 16-bit Real Mode | 32/64-bit Protected Mode | | First instruction from | 0xFFFF:0x0000 (1 MB boundary) | 0xFFFFFFF0 (4 GB boundary) | | Disk partitioning | MBR (≤2.2 TB, 4 partitions) | GPT (≥9 ZB, 128 partitions) | | Bootloader discovery | Reads MBR, runs code in it | Reads EFI System Partition (ESP), finds `.efi` files | | Driver loading | Drivers loaded by OS | UEFI drivers may load during boot | | User-visible UI | Text-based | Graphical | | Secure Boot | Absent | Present | | Network boot (PXE) | Requires add-on card | Native | | POST time on warm boot | Full | May skip (Fast Boot) | --- ## 3.5 Boot Stage Memory State — Analytical | Stage | RAM contents | CPU Mode | Code executing | |-------|--------------|----------|----------------| | Reset | Empty (undefined) | Real Mode (16-bit) | Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) | | POST in progress | Partial (RAM test) | Real Mode | Firmware | | Bootloader loaded | Bootloader code copied to 0x7C00 | Real Mode | Bootloader | | Kernel loaded | Kernel in high memory | Switched to Protected/Long Mode | Kernel entry point | | OS running | Full OS in RAM | Long Mode (64-bit) | Kernel + user processes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System Bus Architecture (Block Diagram) This diagram shows how the CPU, memory, and I/O subsystems communicate through the three sub-buses. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU Chip"] CU["Control Unit"] ALU["ALU"] REG["Registers"] end subgraph SYSMEM["System Memory"] RAM["Main RAM"] end subgraph EXP["I/O and Expansion"] NIC["NIC Card"] GPU["GPU Card"] BIOS["Firmware ROM"] KEYB["Keyboard Controller"] DRV["Storage Drives"] end ADDR["Address Bus
Unidirectional"] DATA["Data Bus
Bidirectional"] CTRL["Control Bus
Bidirectional
Read / Write / IRQ / Clock"] CPU --- ADDR CPU --- DATA CPU --- CTRL ADDR --- RAM DATA --- RAM CTRL --- RAM ADDR --- EXP DATA --- EXP CTRL --- EXP CTRL_BOX["Bus Arbitration Unit"] -.- CTRL classDef bus fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cpu fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mem fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef io fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class ADDR,DATA,CTRL bus class CU,ALU,REG cpu class RAM mem class NIC,GPU,BIOS,KEYB,DRV io ``` --- ## 4.2 The Boot Process — Detailed Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Power Button Pressed
PSU delivers stable voltages"] --> B["CPU Reset
Fetch from Reset Vector"] B --> C["POST Power On Self Test
RAM test, drives, keyboard, video"] C --> D{"POST Passed?"} D -- No --> E["Beep Codes / Error Display
System Halted"] D -- Yes --> F["Load BIOS or UEFI Settings
from CMOS"] F --> G["Identify Boot Devices
per configured boot order"] G --> H["Read MBR or GPT from Disk"] H --> I["MBR Partition Scheme"] H --> J["GPT Partition Scheme"] I --> K["Run Bootloader Code
at 0x7C00"] J --> L["Locate EFI System Partition
ESP FAT32 with .efi files"] K --> M["Load OS Kernel
e.g., ntoskrnl.exe or vmlinuz"] L --> M M --> N["Switch to Protected or Long Mode
64-bit"] N --> O["Initialize Drivers and Mount Root FS"] O --> P["Launch init or systemd
PID 1 starts user space"] P --> Q["Display Login Screen or Desktop
Boot Complete"] classDef phase1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phase2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phase3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phase4 fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#7e22ce,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef phase5 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fail fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff class A,B phase1 class C,D,F phase2 class G,H,I,J,K,L phase3 class M,N,O,P phase4 class Q phase5 class E fail ``` --- ## 4.3 Interface Card-to-Slot Connection Model ```mermaid flowchart LR MB["Motherboard"] SLOT_PCIE16["PCIe x16 Slot
for GPU"] SLOT_PCIE4["PCIe x4 Slot
for NVMe or NIC"] SLOT_PCIE1["PCIe x1 Slot
for low bandwidth cards"] SLOT_M2["M.2 Slot
for compact cards"] CARD_GPU["GPU Card"] CARD_NIC["NIC Card"] CARD_USB["USB Card"] CARD_WIFI["Wi-Fi M.2 Card"] MB --> SLOT_PCIE16 MB --> SLOT_PCIE4 MB --> SLOT_PCIE1 MB --> SLOT_M2 SLOT_PCIE16 --> CARD_GPU SLOT_PCIE4 --> CARD_NIC SLOT_PCIE1 --> CARD_USB SLOT_M2 --> CARD_WIFI DEV1["Monitor"] --> CARD_GPU DEV2["Ethernet Cable"] --> CARD_NIC DEV3["USB Devices"] --> CARD_USB DEV4["Antenna"] --> CARD_WIFI classDef mb fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef slot fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef card fill:#cffafe,stroke:#155e75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dev fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class MB mb class SLOT_PCIE16,SLOT_PCIE4,SLOT_PCIE1,SLOT_M2 slot class CARD_GPU,CARD_NIC,CARD_USB,CARD_WIFI card class DEV1,DEV2,DEV3,DEV4 dev ``` --- ## 4.4 Firmware Update Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Manufacturer releases firmware update"] --> B["Download to USB or via OS utility"] B --> C{"Update method"} C --> D["From inside BIOS or UEFI menu"] C --> E["From inside OS vendor tool"] D --> F["Reboot to firmware update mode"] E --> F F --> G["Verify file integrity checksum"] G --> H{"Checksum valid?"} H -- No --> Z1["Abort - corrupted file"] H -- Yes --> I["Erase Flash chip blocks"] I --> J["Write new firmware to Flash"] J --> K{"Power loss?"} K -- Yes --> Z2["Bricked motherboard
recover via dual BIOS"] K -- No --> L["Verify written data"] L --> M["Reboot with new firmware"] M --> N["Update complete"] classDef start fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef good fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M start class N good class Z1,Z2 bad ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — 3 Mark Questions ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Define an Interface Card. Give two examples of modern interface cards and the slot type they typically use.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Interface Card** (also called an *expansion card*) is a printed circuit board inserted into a motherboard's expansion slot to provide additional functionality or act as an interface between the CPU and external peripherals. (2 marks) Examples: 1. **Graphics Card (GPU)** – uses **PCIe x16** slot to render and output video. (0.5 marks) 2. **Network Interface Card (NIC)** – uses **PCIe x1** or **PCIe x4** slot for LAN/WAN connectivity. (0.5 marks) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Differentiate between BIOS and UEFI in three points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | BIOS | UEFI | |--------|------|------| | CPU Mode | 16-bit Real Mode (limitation: 1 MB) | 32/64-bit Protected Mode (full memory access) | | Disk Support | MBR only, max 2.2 TB | GPT support, drives > 2.2 TB, up to 128 partitions | | Secure Boot | Not supported | Supported (cryptographic verification of bootloader) | (1 mark each — any 3 valid points) --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Part (a)** Explain the three sub-buses of the system bus with neat diagrams. Discuss how a 32-bit address bus can address only 4 GB of memory. (7 marks) **Part (b)** What is Firmware? Explain the different types of ROM used for firmware storage and highlight why Flash memory is preferred for BIOS/UEFI today. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) The system bus is composed of three sub-buses: **1. Data Bus (Bidirectional):** - Carries the actual data being transferred between CPU, memory, and I/O. - Width (e.g., 32 or 64 bits) determines how many bits can be transferred in one cycle. - Example: A 64-bit data bus can transfer 8 bytes per transfer. **2. Address Bus (Unidirectional, CPU → Memory/I/O):** - Carries the memory location (address) the CPU wants to read from or write to. - Width determines addressable memory. - Derivation for 32-bit address bus: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Addressable memory} &= 2^{32} \text{ bytes} \\ &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \\ &= 4 \text{ GB} \end{aligned}$$ Hence a 32-bit address bus can address exactly **4 GB** of memory, regardless of how much physical RAM is installed. To address more, the system must use a 64-bit address bus (PAE is a partial historical workaround). **3. Control Bus (Bidirectional):** - Carries control and timing signals: Read/Write, Interrupt Request (IRQ), Bus Request (BR), Bus Grant (BG), Clock, Reset. - Synchronizes operations between CPU and peripherals. **Diagram (in words for the exam):** Draw a CPU block on the left, a Memory block on the right, and three parallel lines between them labelled `Data Bus`, `Address Bus`, `Control Bus` with arrows showing direction (Address = CPU→Memory; Data = bidirectional; Control = bidirectional). **Why this matters:** Modern CPUs use 64-bit address buses for very large memory spaces (theoretical 16 EB), though only 48 bits are used in current implementations (256 TB virtual). *(Award: 1 mark Data Bus, 1.5 marks Address Bus + derivation, 1.5 marks Control Bus, 1 mark diagram, 2 marks explanation of 4 GB limit.)* --- ### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) **Definition (2 marks):** **Firmware** is a class of software permanently programmed into a non-volatile memory chip (ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash) on a hardware device. It provides low-level control instructions for the device's hardware and is typically the first code executed on power-up. **Types of ROM used for firmware storage (4 marks):** | ROM Type | Programmable By | Erasable By | Re-programmable | Use Case | |----------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------|----------| | **ROM** | Manufacturer (mask) | Not possible | No | Mass-produced devices, very old | | **PROM** | User (one-time) | Not possible | No | Small-batch custom firmware | | **EPROM** | User | UV light (~20 min) | Yes (limited cycles) | Old motherboards | | **EEPROM** | User | Electrical signal (byte-level) | Yes (~1M cycles) | Small firmware storage | | **Flash Memory** | User | Electrical signal (block-level) | Yes (~100K cycles) | Modern BIOS/UEFI, SSDs, phones | **Why Flash is preferred for BIOS/UEFI today (1 mark):** 1. **Electrically erasable in blocks** — faster erase than EEPROM's byte-by-byte process. 2. **High density** — can store MBs of firmware (UEFI needs ~16 MB or more). 3. **In-system reprogrammable** — users can update BIOS/UEFI without removing the chip. 4. **Sufficient endurance** for typical update frequencies (a few times per year). 5. **Low cost per bit** compared to EEPROM. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Flash" (a type of EEPROM) with the "Flash drive" (a USB storage device). They are *named after* the same memory technology but are different products. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** **Part (a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the complete boot process of a personal computer from power-on to OS login. Highlight the role of POST and the bootloader. (7 marks) **Part (b)** Explain the PCIe bus architecture. Derive the theoretical bandwidth of a single PCIe 3.0 lane and a PCIe 3.0 x16 slot. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) **The Boot Process (Flowchart – Draw this in the exam, 3 marks):** 1. **Power-On** — PSU delivers stable voltages; CPU is held in reset. 2. **CPU Reset Vector Execution** — CPU fetches first instruction from address `0xFFFFFFF0` (UEFI) or `0xFFFF:0x0000` (legacy BIOS). 3. **POST (Power-On Self-Test)** — Firmware tests RAM, video adapter, keyboard, drives. On failure, beeps/POST codes are emitted. 4. **Firmware Configuration Load** — BIOS/UEFI reads settings from CMOS (date, boot order). 5. **Boot Device Selection** — Firmware scans configured boot devices (HDD, SSD, USB, network). 6. **Partition Scheme Reading** — Reads MBR or GPT from the selected disk. 7. **Bootloader Execution** — Runs GRUB2/Windows Boot Manager from MBR/ESP. 8. **Kernel Load** — OS kernel (`ntoskrnl.exe`, `vmlinuz`) is loaded into RAM. 9. **Mode Switch to Long Mode (64-bit)** — CPU enters 64-bit protected mode. 10. **OS Initialization** — Drivers loaded, root filesystem mounted, `init`/`systemd` started. 11. **Login Screen** — User authentication, desktop environment launched. **Role of POST (2 marks):** - Verifies that essential hardware components are functional before booting the OS. - Detects RAM size, errors in video adapter, presence of keyboard, etc. - Without POST, the system could attempt to boot with faulty hardware, leading to data corruption or undefined behavior. **Role of the Bootloader (2 marks):** - Bridges the gap between firmware and the OS kernel. - Can present a multi-boot menu (e.g., GRUB showing Windows + Linux). - Loads the kernel and initial RAM disk (`initramfs`) into memory. - Passes control to the OS kernel via a defined entry point. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *"POST checks the operating system"* — this is **wrong**. POST runs *before* the OS exists. POST only checks hardware. --- ### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) **PCIe Architecture (3 marks):** PCI Express (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) is a **serial, point-to-point, switched-fabric** bus standard replacing legacy parallel PCI. It uses: - **Lanes** — Each lane is a full-duplex differential pair (one TX pair + one RX pair). - **Links** — Bundles of lanes: x1, x2, x4, x8, x16, x32. - **Switched Topology** — A PCIe switch connects multiple devices to the CPU's root complex, eliminating the shared-bus arbitration bottleneck. - **Hot-Plug Support** — Devices can be added/removed while system is running. - **Power Management** — Multiple low-power states (L0, L0s, L1, L2, L3). **Derivation — PCIe 3.0 Single Lane (2 marks):** Given: - Raw bit rate = 8 GT/s (GigaTransfers per second). - Encoding = 128b/130b (PCIe 3 onwards). - 1 transfer = 1 bit (in differential serial). $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{raw}} &= 8 \text{ Gbps} \\ R_{\text{data}} &= 8 \times \frac{128}{130} \approx 7.877 \text{ Gbps} \\ \text{Bandwidth per lane} &= \frac{7.877}{8} \approx 0.985 \text{ GB/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Derivation — PCIe 3.0 x16 Slot (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Bandwidth}_{x16} &= 16 \times 0.985 \text{ GB/s} \\ &\approx 15.75 \text{ GB/s (per direction)} \end{aligned}$$ Since PCIe is full-duplex, the aggregate bandwidth across both TX and RX paths is **~31.5 GB/s**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Forgetting the encoding overhead** in PCIe bandwidth calculations: many students write *"8 Gbps = 1 GB/s"* — this loses 1 mark. Always apply 128b/130b for Gen 3+. > 2. **Confusing parallel and serial buses**: PCIe is *serial*, not parallel. Students saying "PCIe has 16 parallel lanes" lose a mark. > 3. **Writing "POST checks OS"**: incorrect. POST = hardware test only. > 4. **Stating BIOS stores OS**: incorrect. BIOS stores firmware; OS is on disk. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Interface Cards, Buses, Firmware, Boot Process** ### Interface Cards - ☐ Interface cards = modular PCBs that expand motherboard capability. - ☐ Common cards: GPU, NIC, sound card, storage controller, USB card. - ☐ Modern cards use **PCIe slots** (x1, x4, x8, x16). - ☐ **M.2** is a compact slot for SSDs and wireless cards. - ☐ Cards require **drivers** to communicate with the OS. ### Buses - ☐ Three sub-buses: **Data**, **Address**, **Control**. - ☐ Address bus is **unidirectional** (CPU → Memory); data and control are **bidirectional**. - ☐ Addressable memory = $2^n$ bytes where $n$ = address bus width. - ☐ 32-bit address bus = **4 GB** address space limit. - ☐ **PCIe** is the dominant modern bus; it is **serial** and uses **lanes**. - ☐ PCIe Gen 3 uses **128b/130b encoding**. - ☐ PCIe 3.0 x16 ≈ **15.75 GB/s** one-way. ### Firmware - ☐ Firmware = software in **non-volatile memory**. - ☐ ROM types: ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → **Flash**. - ☐ Flash is the modern choice (erasable in blocks, high density). - ☐ **BIOS** = legacy 16-bit firmware; **UEFI** = modern 32/64-bit firmware. - ☐ CMOS chip holds BIOS settings; backed by **3 V** coin cell (~3–5 yr life). - ☐ UEFI supports **GPT**, **Secure Boot**, and large drives. ### Boot Process - ☐ Stages: Power → Reset → **POST** → Firmware settings → Boot device → Bootloader → **Kernel** → OS init → Login. - ☐ **POST** checks hardware only (not OS). - ☐ Reset vector: BIOS `0xFFFF:0x0000`, UEFI `0xFFFFFFF0`. - ☐ **MBR** = max 2.2 TB, 4 partitions; **GPT** = much larger, 128 partitions. - ☐ Bootloaders: GRUB2 (Linux), Windows Boot Manager, `boot.efi` (macOS). - ☐ Modern boot uses **Long Mode (64-bit)** after kernel load.

topics.title: Basic CPU Architecture - ALU, Registers, Control Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic CPU Architecture - ALU, Registers, Control Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Central Processing Unit (CPU) — Formal Definition

The **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** is the principal hardware component of a computer system that interprets and executes instructions fetched from memory. It is frequently termed the *brain* or *engine* of the computer. The CPU continuously cycles through the **Fetch → Decode → Execute** pipeline, coordinating with memory, input, and output subsystems to perform arithmetic, logical, control, and data-transfer operations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **GXEST203**, the CPU is studied at the *block-architecture* level (not at the transistor level), focusing on three internal sub-units:

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**
2. **Register File (Registers)**
3. **Control Unit (CU)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to *draw and label a block diagram of the CPU*, *explain the role of each sub-unit*, and *describe the data/instruction flow* between them.

## 1.2 The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)

**Definition:** The ALU is the computational core of the CPU. It performs all arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shifts, comparisons) on binary data supplied through its inputs. The result is produced on its output line, and status flags (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) are updated in the **Flag Register / Status Register**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the ALU as a **highly skilled chef in a restaurant kitchen**. The chef (ALU) takes two raw ingredients (operands) from the *counter* (registers), cooks them according to a specific recipe (operation code / opcode), and places the finished dish (result) back on the counter. The chef also flips small indicator lights on the oven panel (status flags) to say things like *"the dish is zero-spice"* (Zero flag) or *"the pot overflowed"* (Carry flag).

## 1.3 Registers

**Definition:** Registers are **high-speed, small-capacity storage elements** built directly inside the CPU using flip-flops. They hold operands, intermediate results, addresses, and special control data that the CPU is currently processing. Because they are physically located on the CPU chip, their access time is typically in the order of **nanoseconds (10⁻⁹ s)**.

**Types of Registers (KTU 2024 syllabus scope):**

| Register | Full Name | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of the *next* instruction to fetch |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the *currently executing* instruction |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the memory address to be accessed |
| **MBR / MDR** | Memory Data Register | Holds the data being transferred to/from memory |
| **AC / A** | Accumulator | Principal register for ALU operations |
| **GPRs** | General Purpose Registers (R0–Rn) | Hold temporary data and operands |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Registers are the **post-it notes stuck on a clerk's desk**. The clerk (CPU) cannot keep every document in immediate reach, so the most urgent, currently-needed information is written on small notes (registers) within arm's reach. The desk itself is *infinitely faster* than the filing cabinet (RAM), but only a few notes can fit on it.

## 1.4 The Control Unit (CU)

**Definition:** The Control Unit is the *traffic controller* of the CPU. It does not process data; instead, it **directs and coordinates** the activities of the ALU, registers, memory, and I/O devices. It decodes the opcode portion of an instruction and generates the precise sequence of **control signals** (timing and gating pulses) that activate the appropriate data paths.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
The Control Unit is the **conductor of an orchestra**. The violinist (ALU), the percussionist (memory), and the trumpeter (I/O) are the musicians. The conductor (CU) reads the musical score (instruction), raises the baton, and signals *who* plays *what* and *when*. Without the conductor, the musicians would play out of sync and the music would collapse into noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Examiner Insight:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, examiners often expect a **labelled block diagram** of the CPU. Memorising the arrows between PC → MAR → Memory → MDR → IR → CU is critical.

## 1.5 The System Bus — Connecting Everything

Although technically outside the CPU chip, the CPU communicates with memory and I/O through three logical bus groups:

- **Data Bus** — bidirectional, carries the actual data (operand / instruction word). Width: **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits**.
- **Address Bus** — unidirectional, carries the memory address from the CPU to memory. Width determines **addressable memory = 2ⁿ locations** where *n* is the address bus width.
- **Control Bus** — bidirectional, carries timing and command signals (Read, Write, Interrupt Acknowledge, Clock, Reset).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Operation of the CPU — The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle

The CPU repeatedly performs the following micro-operations. Let *IR* denote the Instruction Register and *PC* the Program Counter.

### Phase 1: Fetch
1. The address in **PC** is loaded into **MAR**.
2. A *memory-read* signal is asserted on the control bus.
3. The addressed instruction travels from memory through the **data bus** and is loaded into **MDR** (Memory Data Register).
4. The contents of **MDR** are copied into **IR**.
5. The **PC** is incremented (PC ← PC + 1) to point to the next instruction.

### Phase 2: Decode
1. The **opcode** field of the instruction in **IR** is sent to the **Control Unit**.
2. The CU consults its internal microprogram / hardwired logic to determine which operations must be performed and on which registers.

### Phase 3: Execute
1. If operands are required, the CU issues *read* signals to fetch them into registers.
2. The ALU is signalled to perform the operation (e.g., ADD, AND, COMPARE).
3. The result is written back to a destination register (commonly the Accumulator) or to memory.
4. **Status flags** (Z, C, S, V, P) are updated based on the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Every program you have ever written — from `printf("Hello")` to training a neural network — ultimately decomposes into billions of these tiny Fetch–Decode–Execute cycles.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Clock Cycle Time | $T_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}}$ | seconds; $f_{clock}$ in **Hertz (Hz)** |
| 2 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clock} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clock}}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Cycles per Instruction (avg) | $CPI = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{N_{instructions}}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | CPU Time (expanded) | $T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Throughput (MIPS) | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | **Million Instructions Per Second** |
| 6 | Addressable Memory | $M = 2^{n}$ | locations; $n$ = address bus width in bits |
| 7 | Memory Data Capacity | $C = 2^{n} \times w$ | **bits**; $w$ = word size in bits |
| 8 | Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $Speedup = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced, $S$ = enhancement factor |
| 9 | Clock Frequency | $f_{clock} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clock}}$ | **Hz** |
| 10 | Word Size (general) | $W$ bits per CPU register | typical = **8, 16, 32, 64 bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** When writing a formula containing absolute value or "such that", use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. **Never** use the keyboard pipe `\vert` inside a markdown table row — it breaks the column structure.

## 2.3 Why These Three Sub-Units? — The Engineering Rationale

- **ALU** is *specialised for combinational arithmetic*; placing it on the CPU chip avoids the round-trip latency of sending data to an external coprocessor.
- **Registers** solve the *memory-wall problem*. Random Access Memory (RAM) has access times of **50–100 ns**, while registers operate in **~1 ns**. Keeping intermediate data in registers means the ALU is never starved.
- **Control Unit** is essential because CPUs are *synchronous sequential machines*. Without a central timing authority, asynchronous races between the ALU, registers, and memory would corrupt the program state.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same architectural philosophy underpins:

- **Smartphone SoCs** (System on Chip) — e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple M-series.
- **Embedded microcontrollers** — e.g., ARM Cortex-M used in washing machines, drones, and IoT devices.
- **Supercomputers** — where the ALU is replicated across thousands of cores.
- **GPU shader cores** — thousands of tiny ALUs in parallel.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's trick question:** *"Why is the ALU not connected directly to main memory?"* — Correct answer: *Because ALU operations occur at GHz speeds, while RAM access is 10–100× slower. Direct connection would force the ALU to wait, wasting cycles. The register file acts as a high-speed buffer.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — CPU Execution Time and Throughput (MIPS)

We start from the most fundamental relationship: *clock period* is the reciprocal of *clock frequency*.

$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}}
$$

The total CPU time to execute a program is the product of the *number of clock cycles* required and the *duration of each cycle*.

$$
T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clock}
$$

Substituting the first equation into the second:

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clock}}
$$

Now, the *average number of cycles per instruction* is defined as:

$$
CPI = \frac{N_{cycles}}{N_{instructions}}
$$

Rearranging for $N_{cycles}$:

$$
N_{cycles} = CPI \times N_{instructions}
$$

Substituting this into the $T_{CPU}$ expression:

$$
T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock}
$$

This is the **canonical CPU performance equation** used in every computer-architecture textbook and in KTU numerical problems.

Finally, *throughput in MIPS* is derived by normalising to one million instructions and one second:

$$
MIPS = \frac{N_{instructions}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}
$$

Substituting $T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock}$ and noting $T_{clock} = 1/f_{clock}$:

$$
MIPS = \frac{N_{instructions}}{N_{instructions} \times CPI \times \frac{1}{f_{clock}} \times 10^{6}}
$$

The $N_{instructions}$ cancel:

$$
MIPS = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Addressable Memory Space

The number of uniquely identifiable memory locations is determined by the width of the address bus, $n$ bits. Each bit has two states (0 or 1), and bits operate independently. Therefore:

$$
M_{locations} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n}
$$

If each location stores one *word* of $w$ bits, the total memory capacity is:

$$
C_{memory} = 2^{n} \times w \quad \text{(bits)}
$$

Converting to bytes (1 byte = 8 bits):

$$
C_{memory} = \frac{2^{n} \times w}{8} \quad \text{(bytes)}
$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** A 16-bit address bus with 8-bit word size:
- $M = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ locations
- $C = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288$ bits = **64 KB**

## 3.3 Worked Example Problem (KTU 3-Mark Type)

> **Problem:** A processor runs at **500 MHz** with an average **CPI of 2.5**. Compute (a) the clock period, (b) MIPS rating, and (c) execution time for a program of **20 million** instructions.

### (a) Clock Period
$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}} = \frac{1}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ns}
$$

### (b) MIPS Rating
$$
MIPS = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{6}}{2.5 \times 10^{6}} = 200 \text{ MIPS}
$$

### (c) Execution Time
$$
T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock} = 20 \times 10^{6} \times 2.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-9} = 0.1 \text{ s}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CPU Sub-Units

| Attribute | ALU | Registers | Control Unit |
|-----------|-----|-----------|--------------|
| **Primary Function** | Computation (arithmetic + logic) | Temporary data storage | Sequencing & control signal generation |
| **Operates On** | Operands from registers | Addresses, data, instructions | Opcode bits from IR |
| **Output** | Result + status flags | Stored values to bus or ALU | Control signals to all units |
| **Speed** | Combinational (1 cycle) | ~1 ns access | Decoded in ~1 cycle |
| **Capacity** | 2 input operands → 1 result | Typically **8 / 16 / 32 / 64** registers | Encoded microcode / hardwired logic |
| **Loss-of-Marks Pitfall** | Confusing it with the whole CPU | Confusing with Cache or RAM | Confusing CU with ALU |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students write *"Registers store programs."* This is **wrong**. Programs are stored in **main memory (RAM)**. Registers hold only the *currently relevant subset* of data and instructions. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this conflation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal CPU Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *functional block architecture* of a single-core CPU, showing the data and control paths among the ALU, Register File, Control Unit, and external system bus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph extBus["EXTERNAL SYSTEM BUS"]
        dataBus["DATA BUS (bidirectional)"]
        addrBus["ADDRESS BUS (unidirectional)"]
        ctrlBus["CONTROL BUS (read/write/clk)"]
    end

    subgraph cpuCore["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        regFile["REGISTER FILE\nPC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC, GPRs"]
        aluBlock["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT\nADD SUB MUL AND OR XOR CMP"]
        cuBlock["CONTROL UNIT\nInstruction Decoder + Sequencer"]
        flagReg["FLAG / STATUS REGISTER\nZ C S V P"]
    end

    subgraph memIo["MEMORY & I/O SUBSYSTEM"]
        mainMem["MAIN MEMORY RAM\nROM"]
        ioDev["I/O DEVICES\nKeyboard Display Disk"]
    end

    regFile -- "Operand A, Operand B" --> aluBlock
    aluBlock -- "Result" --> regFile
    aluBlock -- "Status updates" --> flagReg
    flagReg -- "Flag bits" --> cuBlock

    cuBlock -- "Read/Write + Timing signals" --> regFile
    cuBlock -- "Operation select lines" --> aluBlock
    cuBlock -- "Bus control signals" --> ctrlBus

    regFile -- "Memory address out" --> addrBus
    regFile -- "Instruction/Data in/out" --> dataBus
    addrBus --> mainMem
    dataBus <--> mainMem
    ctrlBus <--> mainMem
    mainMem <--> ioDev
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle

This sequence diagram shows the chronological interaction of the CPU sub-units during **one complete machine cycle** (instruction: `ADD R1, R2`).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant CU as Control Unit
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant MAR as Memory Address Register
    participant MEM as Main Memory
    participant MDR as Memory Data Register
    participant IR as Instruction Register
    participant ALU as Arithmetic Logic Unit
    participant AC as Accumulator
    participant FL as Flag Register

    CU->>PC: Step 1 Read current address
    CU->>MAR: Step 2 MAR <- PC
    CU->>MEM: Step 3 Assert READ signal
    MEM-->>MDR: Step 4 Instruction word loaded
    CU->>IR: Step 5 IR <- MDR
    CU->>PC: Step 6 PC <- PC + 1
    CU->>CU: Step 7 DECODE opcode field
    CU->>ALU: Step 8 Signal ADD operation
    ALU->>AC: Step 9 Result -> Accumulator
    ALU->>FL: Step 10 Update Zero Carry Overflow flags
    CU->>CU: Step 11 Cycle repeats
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy Diagram — Register File Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    regFile["REGISTER FILE (on-chip)"]
    regFile --> pcNode["PC - Program Counter"]
    regFile --> irNode["IR - Instruction Register"]
    regFile --> marNode["MAR - Memory Address Register"]
    regFile --> mdrNode["MDR - Memory Data Register"]
    regFile --> acNode["AC - Accumulator"]
    regFile --> gprNode["GPR0 ... GPRn - General Purpose Registers"]
    regFile --> flagNode["FLAGS - Z C S V P"]

    pcNode -.->|"Holds next instruction address"| memExt["External Memory"]
    irNode -.->|"Holds current instruction"| decode["CU Decoder"]
    marNode -.->|"Specifies memory location"| memExt
    mdrNode -.->|"Data buffer to/from memory"| memExt
    acNode -.->|"Default ALU operand + result"| aluCore["ALU"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to *"draw and explain the CPU block diagram"*, the examiner awards marks for: (1) all three sub-units drawn distinctly, (2) labelled arrows showing *data* and *control* flow, (3) the System Bus shown externally, and (4) a *clear, single-line caption* below the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and the Control Unit (CU) of a CPU.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ALU | Control Unit |
|--------|-----|--------------|
| **Function** | Performs arithmetic (add, subtract, multiply, divide) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations on data. | Directs and coordinates the operations of the CPU and external units by generating control signals. |
| **Data Handling** | *Processes* data — takes operands and produces results. | *Sequences* data flow — does not modify data values. |
| **Output** | Result on output line + status flags (Z, C, S, V). | Control signals on the control bus (Read/Write, clock gating, register select). |
| **Analogy** | The chef who cooks. | The conductor who directs. |
| **Implements** | Combinational digital logic (adders, multiplexers, shifters). | Decoders, state machines, microprogram sequencer. |

> *[3 marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark for clear distinction]*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six special-purpose registers found inside a CPU and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **PC (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **IR (Instruction Register):** Holds the *currently executing* instruction word. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Holds the memory location that is to be read from or written to. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **MDR (Memory Data Register / MBR):** Acts as a two-way buffer holding data transferred to/from memory. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **AC (Accumulator):** Default operand register and result destination for ALU operations. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Flag / Status Register:** Stores condition codes (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow, Parity) reflecting the result of the last ALU operation. *(0.5 mark)*

> *[3 marks: 0.5 mark each]*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Both choices cover the same module but with different cognitive emphasis.*

---

### Question B1A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Draw a neat block diagram of the CPU showing the ALU, Register File, and Control Unit. Label the data and control buses connecting them. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a step-by-step explanation, describe the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle. Clearly show the role of the PC, MAR, MDR, IR, and CU. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Draw a block diagram with the following mandatory features:

- **Three labelled rectangular blocks** inside a large rectangle labelled "CPU": (1) ALU, (2) Register File, (3) Control Unit.
- **Two arrows** from the Register File to the ALU labelled "Operand A" and "Operand B".
- **One return arrow** from ALU to Register File labelled "Result".
- **Control Unit** with arrows pointing to ALU (control lines), Register File (read/write strobes), and downward to the external Control Bus.
- **Address Bus** and **Data Bus** drawn below the CPU rectangle, with bidirectional arrows to a separate "Main Memory" block.
- A small "Flag Register" attached to the ALU output feeding back to the CU.

*Mark distribution:*
- 3 marks for correct block structure
- 2 marks for correct arrows and labels
- 1 mark for external bus and memory
- 1 mark for neatness and caption

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Step-by-step explanation of the Fetch–Decode–Execute cycle:

**Step 1 — Fetch Instruction:**
The address currently in the **PC** is copied into the **MAR**. The CU then asserts a *memory-read* signal on the control bus. The memory subsystem reads the instruction at that address and places it on the data bus; the **MDR** latches it.

**Step 2 — Load IR:**
The contents of the **MDR** are transferred to the **IR**, where the instruction is now visible to the Control Unit. The **PC** is then incremented (PC ← PC + 1) so that the next instruction can be fetched in the following cycle.

**Step 3 — Decode:**
The **CU** examines the *opcode* field of the instruction in the IR. Using a hardwired decoder or a microprogram, the CU determines: (i) the operation to be performed, (ii) the source and destination registers, and (iii) the sequence of micro-operations required.

**Step 4 — Operand Fetch (if required):**
If the instruction references memory operands, the CU issues another memory-read sequence using the MAR and MDR to retrieve the operand(s) into general-purpose registers.

**Step 5 — Execute:**
The CU activates the ALU with the appropriate operation code. The ALU reads its operands from the register file, performs the computation, and produces a result. Simultaneously, status flags (Z, C, S, V) in the flag register are updated.

**Step 6 — Store Result:**
The result is written back to the destination register (commonly the Accumulator) or stored in memory via the MDR.

**Step 7 — Repeat:**
The CU increments the internal cycle counter and returns to Step 1 to fetch the next instruction.

*Mark distribution for 7 marks:*
- 1 mark for the fetch step
- 1 mark for the IR load + PC increment
- 1 mark for decode
- 1 mark for operand fetch
- 1 mark for execute
- 1 mark for result store
- 1 mark for completeness and cycle continuity

---

### Question B1B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the functions of the ALU, Register File, and Control Unit in a CPU. Briefly describe the types of operations the ALU can perform. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A processor has a clock frequency of **2.4 GHz** and an average CPI of **1.8**. For a program containing **50 million instructions**, calculate:
- (i) Clock period
- (ii) Total CPU execution time
- (iii) MIPS rating of the processor

*(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ALU Functions:**
- Performs arithmetic operations: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division.
- Performs logical operations: AND, OR, NOT, XOR, NOR, NAND.
- Performs shift and rotate operations: logical shift left/right, arithmetic shift, circular rotate.
- Performs comparison operations: sets flags based on equality, greater-than, less-than relations.
- Updates the **Flag Register** (Z, C, S, V, P) after every operation.

**Register File Functions:**
- Provides the *fastest* storage directly accessible to the ALU.
- Holds operands, intermediate results, addresses, and the current instruction.
- Includes **PC, IR, MAR, MDR, AC, and GPRs**.
- Reduces the number of memory accesses, thus accelerating program execution.

**Control Unit Functions:**
- **Fetches** instructions (via PC and MAR).
- **Decodes** opcodes (using decoder / microprogram).
- **Sequences** micro-operations by issuing control signals.
- **Coordinates** the ALU, registers, memory, and I/O devices.
- Handles **interrupts**, **branch conditions**, and **reset / halt** signals.

*Mark distribution:*
- 2 marks for ALU
- 2 marks for Register File
- 2 marks for Control Unit
- 1 mark for the ALU operation categories

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $f_{clock} = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2.4 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}$
- $CPI = 1.8$
- $N_{instructions} = 50 \times 10^{6}$

**(i) Clock Period:**
$$
T_{clock} = \frac{1}{f_{clock}} = \frac{1}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.4167 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \approx 0.417 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) CPU Execution Time:**
$$
T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock} = 50 \times 10^{6} \times 1.8 \times 0.4167 \times 10^{-9}
$$
$$
T_{CPU} = 90 \times 10^{6} \times 0.4167 \times 10^{-9} = 37.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.0375 \text{ s} = 37.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) MIPS Rating:**
$$
MIPS = \frac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{9}}{1.8 \times 10^{6}} = 1333.33 \text{ MIPS} \approx 1.33 \text{ GIPS}
$$

*Mark distribution:*
- 2 marks for clock period
- 3 marks for execution time
- 2 marks for MIPS

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (This Topic):**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes** — Forgetting to convert GHz to Hz, or giving clock period in milliseconds instead of nanoseconds. *(Loss: 1 mark per error)*
> 2. **Confusing PC and MAR** — PC holds the *next* instruction's address; MAR holds the *currently accessed* memory address. The PC is copied *into* MAR, not the other way around.
> 3. **Calling the CU the "brain"** — In a Part B answer, calling the CU the brain of the CPU will lose you 1 mark. The *CPU* is the brain; the *CU* is the *traffic controller inside the brain*.
> 4. **Skipping the increment of PC** — Many students forget that PC must be incremented *after* fetching, before executing. The cycle cannot repeat without this.
> 5. **Drawing unlabelled arrows** — A block diagram without labelled arrows is treated as half-answered and capped at 3/7 marks for that part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — it covers ~90% of marks typically awarded for this topic.**

- ✅ **CPU = ALU + Register File + Control Unit**, interconnected by the *internal CPU bus*.
- ✅ **ALU** does the *computation*; **CU** does the *coordination*; **Registers** do the *fast temporary storage*.
- ✅ **Fetch → Decode → Execute** is the heartbeat of every CPU.
- ✅ **PC** holds the next address; **MAR** holds the current memory address; **MDR** is the data buffer; **IR** holds the current instruction.
- ✅ **Clock Period** $T_{clock} = 1 / f_{clock}$.
- ✅ **CPU Time** $T_{CPU} = N_{instructions} \times CPI \times T_{clock}$.
- ✅ **MIPS** $= f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^{6})$.
- ✅ **Addressable memory** $= 2^{n}$ where $n$ is the address bus width.
- ✅ **Status Flags** updated by ALU: **Z** (Zero), **C** (Carry), **S** (Sign), **V** (Overflow), **P** (Parity).
- ✅ **Three external buses**: Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional, CPU→Memory), Control (bidirectional, timing & commands).
- ✅ The CU does *not* process data — it only generates *control signals*.
- ✅ Registers are *faster* but *smaller* than cache, which is *faster* but *smaller* than RAM.
- ✅ For a 3-mark diagram question: always include *labels*, *arrows*, and a *caption*.
- ✅ For a 14-mark cycle question: write the cycle as *numbered steps* — do not narrate loosely.
- ✅ For numerical problems: show *formula → substitution → final answer with unit*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Format and Assembly Language (Basics Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instruction Format and Assembly Language (Basics Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Instruction — The Vocabulary of the CPU

### Formal KTU Definition
An **Instruction** is a binary-coded command, stored in memory, that the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes. It is the lowest level of direct communication between the programmer (or compiler) and the processor hardware. The complete set of instructions a processor understands is called its **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus is on the **logical layout** of an instruction (its *format*), not on the silicon-level implementation. Mastering instruction format is the gateway to understanding why high-level languages like C, Java, or Python can be translated into machine code at all.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Coded Recipe"
Imagine you walk into a kitchen robot that only understands a tiny set of commands written on color-coded index cards:

* The **left half** of the card (e.g., "CHOP" in big red letters) tells the robot **what** to do.
* The **right half** of the card (e.g., "onion 250g") tells the robot **on what** to do it.
* Some cards ("TIMER 5 min") need no object at all — they just operate on the robot's *current state*.

A CPU instruction works exactly like this index card:

* The **Opcode (Operation Code)** = the verb (CHOP).
* The **Operands** = the object (onion 250g).
* **Zero-operand instructions** = the timer card — they act on an implicit storage area (the accumulator/stack).

## 1.2 Machine Language vs Assembly Language

| Property | Machine Language | Assembly Language |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Representation | Binary (0s and 1s) only | Mnemonic symbols (MOV, ADD, HLT) |
| Human-readable? | **No** | **Yes** (partially) |
| Executed directly by CPU? | **Yes** | No — needs an **assembler** |
| One-to-one mapping? | — | **Yes** — 1 assembly line = 1 machine instruction |
| KTU relevance | Rarely asked | **Frequently** asked (3/14 mark questions) |

### Formal Definition — Assembly Language
> **Assembly language** is a low-level programming language in which each statement (a *mnemonic*) corresponds almost one-to-one with a single machine-language instruction. Mnemonics are short alphabetic codes chosen as memory aids (e.g., `ADD` for addition, `MOV` for data movement, `HLT` for halt).

> [!NOTE]
> A program that converts assembly code into machine code is called an **Assembler**. It performs a 1:1 translation using a lookup table called the **Mnemonic Table** or **Op-table**. Unlike a compiler, an assembler does not perform major optimization or restructuring.

## 1.3 Instruction Format — The Skeleton of an Instruction

The **Instruction Format** is the bit-level layout of an instruction: how the total bit-width is partitioned into functional fields.

A generic instruction format contains up to **four** logical fields:

$$
\text{Instruction} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{\text{What to do}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Mode}}_{\text{How to find operand}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Address}_1}_{\text{Operand 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Address}_2}_{\text{Operand 2}} \;+\; \ldots
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **mode field** is optional in simple hypothetical machines (which the KTU syllabus assumes). When present, it specifies the *addressing mode* — i.e., how the operand value should be interpreted (direct, indirect, immediate, etc.). In the basic KTU-level treatment, we usually treat operands as direct addresses or immediate constants.

## 1.4 Common Instruction Formats (Address-Based Classification)

A KTU favourite. Instructions are classified by **how many explicit address fields** they carry:

| Format | # Address Fields | Typical Use | Example Notation |
|--------|------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Three-address** | 3 | Powerful RISC/CISC, complex arithmetic | `ADD R1, A, B` (i.e., R1 ← A + B) |
| **Two-address** | 2 | CISC machines (x86 legacy) | `ADD A, B` (i.e., A ← A + B) |
| **One-address** | 1 | Simple accumulator machines (classic textbook, 8085) | `ADD B` (i.e., AC ← AC + M[B]) |
| **Zero-address** | 0 | Stack-based machines (JVM bytecode) | `ADD` (i.e., TOS ← TOS + NOS) |

> [!TIP]
> A **0-address instruction** operates entirely on the **Top-Of-Stack (TOS)**. The KTU reference text (M. Mano) uses the one-address accumulator-based format heavily in Module 2 examples. Stick to the one- and two-address formats for exam safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical One-Address Instruction

For a classic single-accumulator machine (the KTU default), each instruction is laid out as:

| Opcode (k bits) | Address / Operand (n bits) |
|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Tells the Control Unit what operation to perform | Tells the CU **where** in memory (or what constant) to operate on |

Total instruction length:

$$
L = k + n \quad \text{(in bits)}
$$

where

* $k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ — number of opcode bits, $N$ = number of distinct opcodes.
* $n = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$ — number of address bits, $M$ = number of addressable memory locations.

## 2.2 Generalized n-Address Format Length

If the machine has $N$ operations, a memory of $M$ words, and uses an $a$-address instruction format, then:

$$
\boxed{\; L = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \; + \; a \cdot \lceil \log_2 M \rceil \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love to test the inverse problem: *"If the instruction length is 32 bits and there are 64 operations, how much memory can a two-address machine address?"* Work the formula backward.

## 2.3 Generalization with a Mode Field

If a $b$-bit **mode field** is also present (to indicate addressing mode), the formula extends to:

$$
\boxed{\; L = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \; + \; b \; + \; a \cdot \lceil \log_2 M \rceil \;}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Memory locations addressable by $n$ address bits | $M = 2^{n}$ | $M$ = locations, $n$ = bits |
| 2 | Opcode bits required for $N$ operations | $k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | $k$ = bits, $N$ = opcode count |
| 3 | One-address instruction length | $L = k + n$ | All in **bits** |
| 4 | Two-address instruction length | $L = k + 2n$ | All in **bits** |
| 5 | Three-address instruction length | $L = k + 3n$ | All in **bits** |
| 6 | Zero-address (stack) instruction length | $L = k$ | All in **bits** |
| 7 | General $a$-address with mode field | $L = k + b + a \cdot n$ | All in **bits** |
| 8 | Number of programs (with $P$-word program counter) | $\text{Programs} = 2^{P}$ | $P$ = PC bits |
| 9 | Program capacity in $P$-bit PC | $M_{\text{prog}} = 2^{P}$ words | $P$ = PC bits |
| 10 | Total memory size (in bytes) | $\text{Bytes} = 2^{n} \times w/8$ | $w$ = word size in bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not confuse $M$ (memory size) with $N$ (number of opcodes).** They use the same Greek letter? No — KTU uses $M$ for memory and $N$ for opcodes. Memorize: **M**emory → **M**any words; **N**umber of instructions → **N**ame it $N$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **ISA Design Trade-off**: Short opcodes (small $k$) leave more bits for operands, allowing direct addressing of larger memories. Long opcodes (large $k$) allow more instructions but shrink addressable space. This trade-off is **the central design question** behind every real processor (ARM vs x86 vs RISC-V).
* **Code Density**: Zero-address (stack) code is *denser* (each instruction is short), but requires more instructions to express the same algorithm — this is why JVM bytecode is stack-based.
* **Compiler Design**: A compiler's *code generator* must respect the target ISA's format. A backend targeting a one-address machine produces a *register-to-memory* style; a three-address backend produces an *SSA* (Static Single Assignment) style.
* **Embedded Systems**: Microcontrollers (8051, AVR, PIC) use fixed-length instruction formats to allow single-cycle fetch — the instruction format is a direct determinant of clock speed and power.

## 2.6 Functional Fields of a One-Address Instruction (Recap)

For the rest of this section, we adopt the **Mano-style one-address accumulator machine** as our KTU reference. In this machine:

* There is **one CPU register**: the **Accumulator (AC)**.
* Every arithmetic/logic operation implicitly uses AC as one operand and the addressed memory location as the other.
* The result is implicitly returned to AC.

Example flow of `ADD X` where $X$ is a memory address:

$$
\text{AC} \;\leftarrow\; \text{AC} \;+\; M[X]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module 2 syllabus treats this as a *baseline reference architecture*. You will not be required to draw transistor-level schematics; you will, however, be required to write symbolic execution of programs written in this notation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Length Formula

We derive the general instruction-length formula from first principles.

**Step 1** — The CPU must distinguish among $N$ distinct opcodes. With $k$ bits, the number of unique bit patterns is $2^{k}$. To represent all $N$ operations, we need:

$$
2^{k} \geq N
$$

Taking $\log_2$ on both sides:

$$
k \geq \log_2 N
$$

Since $k$ must be an integer, we take the ceiling:

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

**Step 2** — Similarly, an $n$-bit address field can select one of $2^{n}$ memory words:

$$
M = 2^{n} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil
$$

**Step 3** — In an $a$-address format, there are $a$ such address fields, contributing $a \cdot n$ bits, plus $k$ opcode bits, plus an optional $b$-bit mode field:

$$
\boxed{\;L \;=\; k \;+\; b \;+\; a \cdot n \;=\; \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \;+\; b \;+\; a \cdot \lceil \log_2 M \rceil\;}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — One-Address Machine

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A computer has **64 operations** and a memory of **4096 words**. Each instruction uses a **one-address format** (no mode field). Find the instruction length. How many bits are used for the opcode, and how many for the address?

**Step 1 — Compute opcode bits ($k$):**

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 64 \rceil = \lceil 6 \rceil = 6 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute address bits ($n$):**

$$
n = \lceil \log_2 4096 \rceil = \lceil 12 \rceil = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total length for a one-address format ($a=1$, $b=0$):**

$$
L = k + 1 \cdot n = 6 + 12 = 18 \text{ bits}
$$

**Final answer:** Opcode = **6 bits**, Address = **12 bits**, Instruction length = **18 bits**.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Two-Address Machine (Inverse)

> **Problem:** A two-address machine has a 32-bit instruction length, supports **128 operations**, and uses a 2-bit mode field. How much memory can it address?

**Step 1 — Compute opcode bits:**

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 128 \rceil = 7 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the length equation with $a=2$, $b=2$:**

$$
L = k + b + 2 \cdot n
$$

$$
32 = 7 + 2 + 2n
$$

$$
2n = 32 - 9 = 23
$$

$$
n = 11.5
$$

**Step 3 — Take the ceiling (since $n$ must be an integer), and re-derive $L$:**

If $n = 12$, total = $7 + 2 + 24 = 33$ bits — exceeds 32. So $n$ must be **11**, and the designer simply **leaves 1 bit unused** in the format.

**Step 4 — Memory capacity:**

$$
M = 2^{11} = 2048 \text{ words}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, the correct interpretation is: *"The machine can address 2,048 words; the leftover 1 bit is unused (or could be used as a future extension flag)."* Examiners reward this nuance.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Three-Address with Two Memory Sizes

> **Problem:** A computer has a 24-bit, three-address instruction format. There are 32 operations and **no mode field**. Find the size of the addressable memory.

**Step 1 — Opcode bits:**

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 32 \rceil = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Set up the length equation with $a=3$, $b=0$:**

$$
L = k + 3n \;\Longrightarrow\; 24 = 5 + 3n
$$

$$
3n = 19 \;\Longrightarrow\; n = 19/3 \approx 6.33
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to $n = 7$ and recalculate $L$:**

$$
L = 5 + 3(7) = 5 + 21 = 26 \text{ bits}
$$

This exceeds 24, so the original length 24 is **infeasible**. The closest valid solution is $n = 6$, giving $L = 5 + 18 = 23$ bits (1 bit unused), and:

$$
M = 2^{6} = 64 \text{ words}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often tests *feasibility*: students must check that the chosen $n$ does not push $L$ over the given instruction size. Always end with a feasibility check.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numeric Scenarios

| Instruction Length | # Operations | # Addresses | Mode bits | Addressable Memory | Result $L$ | Feasible? |
|-------------------|--------------|-------------|-----------|--------------------|-------------|-----------|
| 16 bits | 16 | 1 | 0 | $2^?$ | $4 + n = 16 \Rightarrow n=12$ | ✅ $M = 4096$ |
| 32 bits | 128 | 2 | 2 | $2^?$ | $7+2+2n=32 \Rightarrow n=11$ | ✅ $M = 2048$ |
| 24 bits | 32 | 3 | 0 | $2^?$ | $5+3n=24 \Rightarrow n=6.33$ | ❌ → $n=6$ |
| 36 bits | 64 | 2 | 4 | $2^?$ | $6+4+2n=36 \Rightarrow n=13$ | ✅ $M = 8192$ |
| 12 bits | 8 | 0 | 0 | (stack) | $L = 3$ | ✅ — stack format |

## 3.6 Symbolic Execution of a One-Address Program (Trace Table)

Consider this hypothetical assembly program (one-address accumulator machine):

| Line | Address | Mnemonic | Operand | Hex Code (Assume) | Symbolic Effect |
|------|---------|----------|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 20 | `LDA` | 30 | `14 1E` | AC ← M[30] |
| 2 | 21 | `ADD` | 31 | `58 1F` | AC ← AC + M[31] |
| 3 | 22 | `STA` | 32 | `5C 20` | M[32] ← AC |
| 4 | 23 | `HLT` | — | `C0 00` | Stop CPU |

Suppose $M[30] = 5$ and $M[31] = 3$.

**Trace:**

* After line 1: $\text{AC} = 5$.
* After line 2: $\text{AC} = 5 + 3 = 8$.
* After line 3: $M[32] = 8$, AC still $= 8$.
* Line 4: CPU halts.

This is the canonical style of a KTU trace question. The examiner expects you to show **state of AC and main memory after each instruction**.

## 3.7 Mapping Assembly Mnemonic → Machine Code (One-to-One)

The **assembler** maintains an *opcode table*. For our hypothetical machine, a snippet might be:

| Mnemonic | Opcode (Hex) | Length (words) | Meaning |
|----------|--------------|----------------|---------|
| `LDA` | 14 | 1 | Load to AC |
| `STA` | 5C | 1 | Store from AC |
| `ADD` | 58 | 1 | Add memory to AC |
| `SUB` | 59 | 1 | Subtract memory from AC |
| `HLT` | C0 | 1 | Halt CPU |
| `JMP` | 40 | 1 | Unconditional jump |

> [!NOTE]
> For a KTU answer, the **opcode-table values need not be memorized**. The syllabus focuses on the *concept of one-to-one mapping* and the *role of the assembler*. If the question provides a table, use it. If not, write the assembly lines with mnemonics and a one-line description of each.
