## 4.4 Assembly Language Program Flow (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

The KTU-style computer operates on a perpetual **fetch–decode–execute** cycle. The diagram below shows where the *instruction format* fields (opcode, address) are consumed in each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[Program Counter PC] --> MAR2[MAR]
    MAR2 --> RAM[Memory Read - IR]
    RAM --> IR[Instruction Register IR]
    IR --> DEC{Decode Opcode field}
    DEC --> EX1[Identify operation]
    IR --> MAR3[Address field to MAR]
    MAR3 --> RAM2[Memory Read - operand]
    RAM2 --> DR[Data Register]
    DR --> ALU2[ALU operates with AC]
    EX1 --> ALU2
    ALU2 --> AC2[AC updated]
    PC2[Increment PC] --> PC
    AC2 --> STA[If STA - write back to memory]

    style PC fill:#ffadad,stroke:#000
    style IR fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#000
    style DEC fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#000
    style ALU2 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#000
    style AC2 fill:#9bf6ff,stroke:#000
    style PC2 fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For exam answers, the *fetch–decode–execute* loop is **mandatory background** for any 14-mark question that asks you to *execute* an assembly program. The control unit's micro-operations are the "C-grade" answer; the format-driven interpretation is the "A-grade" answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
Differentiate between **Machine Language** and **Assembly Language**. State the role of an **assembler**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Aspect | Machine Language | Assembly Language |
|---|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Notation | Binary digits (0s and 1s) | Symbolic mnemonics (e.g., ADD, MOV) |
| 2 | Human readability | Not readable | Readable (semi) |
| 3 | Execution | Directly executed by the CPU | Must first be translated to machine code |
| 4 | Translator | — | **Assembler** (1:1 translation) |
| 5 | Error detection | Difficult | Easier (labels, comments) |

> The **assembler** is a system program that translates assembly-language mnemonics into their equivalent machine-code binary using a fixed opcode table, producing a relocatable object file as output.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
What is an **Instruction Format**? List the three common address-based instruction formats.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Instruction Format** is the internal bit-level organization of a machine instruction. It defines how many bits are allocated to the **opcode field** and to the **address/operand field(s)**.

The three common address-based formats are:

1. **Three-address format** — opcode + 3 address fields. Example: `ADD R1, A, B` (R1 ← M[A] + M[B]).
2. **Two-address format** — opcode + 2 address fields. Example: `ADD A, B` (M[A] ← M[A] + M[B]).
3. **One-address format** — opcode + 1 address field; the other operand is the accumulator. Example: `ADD X` (AC ← AC + M[X]).

*(A 4th, **zero-address / stack format**, is also accepted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Instruction Length Computation Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

(a) **Derive** the general formula for the length of an $a$-address instruction in a computer with $N$ operations, $M$ memory words, and a $b$-bit mode field. (7 marks)

(b) A computer has a **16-bit instruction length**, supports **8 operations**, and uses a **one-address format** with **no mode field**.

    (i) Find the size of the addressable memory. (3 marks)

    (ii) If the same machine is redesigned as a **two-address format** with a **2-bit mode field**, find the new addressable memory size. (4 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1* — Let $k$ be the number of opcode bits. To represent $N$ distinct operations, the $k$ bits must be able to generate at least $N$ unique patterns:

$$
2^{k} \ge N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil
$$

*Step 2* — Let $n$ be the number of address bits. To address $M$ memory words:

$$
2^{n} \ge M \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; n = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil
$$

*Step 3* — In an $a$-address format, there are $a$ address fields contributing $a \cdot n$ bits. A $b$-bit mode field contributes $b$ bits. The opcode contributes $k$ bits. Total instruction length:

$$
\boxed{\;L \;=\; k + b + a \cdot n \;=\; \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + b + a \cdot \lceil \log_2 M \rceil\;}
$$

(Conclude with: "This formula is central to ISA design; a longer $L$ allows more opcodes or larger memories but increases program size in memory.")

**(b) Part (i) — One-address, 16-bit, 8 ops, no mode:**

$$
k = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3 \text{ bits}, \quad b = 0, \quad a = 1
$$

$$
L = k + b + a \cdot n \;\Longrightarrow\; 16 = 3 + 0 + n
$$

$$
n = 13 \text{ bits} \;\Longrightarrow\; M = 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ words}
$$

**(b) Part (ii) — Two-address with 2-bit mode field:**

$$
L = k + b + 2n \;\Longrightarrow\; 16 = 3 + 2 + 2n
$$

$$
2n = 11 \;\Longrightarrow\; n = 5.5
$$

Round up to $n = 6$. Check feasibility: $L = 3 + 2 + 12 = 17$ bits — exceeds 16! Therefore, take $n = 5$, $L = 3 + 2 + 10 = 15$ bits (1 bit unused).

$$
M = 2^{5} = 32 \text{ words}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Adding a mode field *and* a second address field dramatically shrinks addressable memory from 8192 to 32 words — a key engineering trade-off.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Assembly Language & Program Trace Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

(a) Explain the **structure of an assembly language statement**, giving the four typical fields. Give **two examples** of assembler directives and explain their purpose. (7 marks)

(b) Consider the following assembly program for a one-address accumulator machine. **Trace** the contents of the accumulator (AC) and the relevant memory locations. Assume `M[20] = 6`, `M[21] = 4`, `M[22] = 0`, `M[30] = 0`. (7 marks)

| Line | Address | Mnemonic | Operand |
|------|---------|----------|---------|
| 1 | 10 | `LDA` | 20 |
| 2 | 11 | `MUL` | 21 |
| 3 | 12 | `STA` | 22 |
| 4 | 13 | `HLT` | — |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Assembly statement structure (7 marks):**

A typical assembly-language source line has **four logical fields**, separated by spaces:

$$
\underbrace{\text{label}}_{\text{optional}} \quad \underbrace{\text{mnemonic}}_{\text{opcode symbol}} \quad \underbrace{\text{operand}}_{\text{address or data}} \quad \underbrace{\text{comment}}_{\text{optional, starts with ;}}
$$

* **Label** — A symbolic name attached to an address. Used as a *jump target* or *data reference*. Example: `LOOP: STA RESULT`.
* **Mnemonic** — The symbolic operation code. The assembler looks it up in the *opcode table*. Example: `ADD`.
* **Operand field** — Specifies the data or address for the operation. May be a label, a literal constant, or a register name.
* **Comment** — Begins with `;` (or `//`, depending on the assembler). Ignored by the assembler; aids readability.

**Two assembler directives** (with purpose):

1. **`ORG <address>`** — *Origin directive*. Tells the assembler to begin placing the following machine code at the specified memory address. Example: `ORG 100` ⇒ next instruction will be loaded at address 100.
2. **`END`** — *End directive*. Marks the logical end of the assembly source file. The assembler stops processing beyond this line.

*(Other acceptable directives: `EQU` (equate a symbol to a constant), `DB` (define byte), `DW` (define word), `DS` (define storage).)*

**(b) Program trace (7 marks):**

| Step | IR | Operation | AC (after) | Memory changes |
|------|----|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| Init | — | Reset | $0$ (assumed) | — |
| 1 | `LDA 20` | AC ← M[20] | $\text{AC} = 6$ | — |
| 2 | `MUL 21` | AC ← AC × M[21] | $\text{AC} = 6 \times 4 = 24$ | — |
| 3 | `STA 22` | M[22] ← AC | $\text{AC} = 24$ | $M[22] = 24$ |
| 4 | `HLT` | Stop CPU | $\text{AC} = 24$ | — |

**Final state:** $\text{AC} = 24$, $M[22] = 24$, $M[20] = 6$, $M[21] = 4$, $M[30] = 0$ (unchanged).

> This program computes the **product of two memory operands** using only one-address instructions — equivalent to a C statement `result = a * b;` where `a` is at M[20], `b` at M[21], and `result` at M[22].

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-deduction traps** reported by KTU examiners (2024 Scheme):
>
> 1. **Confusing $M$ (memory) and $N$ (operations).** Writing $\log_2 M$ when you need $\log_2 N$ (or vice versa) costs **2 full marks** on a 7-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the ceiling** ($\lceil \cdot \rceil$). Bits must be whole numbers; fractional bits are *not* valid.
> 3. **Skipping the feasibility check.** After computing $n$, plug it back into the length equation. If $L$ exceeds the given instruction length, you must adjust (and possibly lose 1–2 bits to a wasted/unused field).
> 4. **Omitting the assembler role.** When the question says "explain assembly language," the *assembler* definition is **non-negotiable** — leaving it out can cost 1–2 marks even if your mnemonic/format explanation is perfect.
> 5. **Writing opcodes from memory.** KTU questions provide the opcode table in the question paper. Do **not** invent opcodes from a specific real architecture (e.g., 8085) — the KTU reference architecture is a *generic hypothetical machine*.
> 6. **No trace table.** A "trace this program" question without a clear **state-after-each-step** table will receive at most 50% of the marks allotted for that sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Instruction** = binary command stored in memory; it is the lowest-level user-visible programming unit.
* **Instruction format** = bit-level layout of an instruction; partitions the bit-width into **opcode**, optional **mode**, and one or more **address/operand** fields.
* **Opcode bits:** $k = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$, where $N$ = number of distinct operations.
* **Address bits:** $n = \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$, where $M$ = addressable memory words.
* **Address-based format classification:** **Zero-, One-, Two-, Three-address** formats. Each additional address field widens the instruction but reduces addressable memory for a fixed total length.
* **Master formula:** $\;L = k + b + a \cdot n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil + b + a \cdot \lceil \log_2 M \rceil$.
* **Assembly language** uses **mnemonics** for human readability; translation to machine code is performed 1:1 by an **assembler** using a fixed **opcode (mnemonic) table**.
* **Structure of an assembly statement:** `LABEL : MNEMONIC OPERAND ; COMMENT` — each part is optional except the mnemonic.
* **Assembler directives** (e.g., `ORG`, `END`, `EQU`, `DB`, `DW`) are *non-executable* instructions that guide the assembler; they consume no memory cycles at run time.
* **One-address accumulator machine** is the KTU default reference architecture; all operations are of the form `AC ← AC ◦ M[address]`.
* **Always perform a feasibility check** when given a fixed instruction length — round up $n$ and recompute $L$; if it overflows, the previous integer is the correct one (with possibly 1 bit unused).
* **Do not confuse** the *assembler* (1:1 translation, this module) with the *compiler* (high-level to assembly/machine, previous/future modules).
* **Key fact to commit:** Adding a mode field and a second address field *shrinks* addressable memory for a fixed instruction length — this is the **classic ISA trade-off** the KTU paper tests.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fetch-Execute Cycle and Instruction Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fetch-Execute Cycle and Instruction Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fetch-Execute Cycle** (also known as the **Instruction Cycle** or **Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle**) is the fundamental operational procedure performed by the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a computer to retrieve, interpret, and execute machine-level instructions stored in primary memory. It is the heartbeat of every computing device — from embedded microcontrollers to high-performance server processors.

According to the standard **Stored-Program Concept** formalized by John von Neumann in 1945, both *data* and *instructions* reside in the same memory. The CPU therefore repeatedly performs a four-phase loop:

$$\text{Fetch} \rightarrow \text{Decode} \rightarrow \text{Execute} \rightarrow \text{Store}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to diagram the Fetch-Execute cycle, identify the role of each CPU register (PC, MAR, MDR, IR, ACC), and explain how the **Control Unit** orchestrates the cycle using **control signals** and the **system clock**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef in a Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a head chef in a busy restaurant who must cook one dish at a time from a long recipe book:

| CPU Action | Chef Analogy |
|------------|--------------|
| **Program Counter (PC)** | The bookmark showing the *next* recipe to read |
| **Memory Address Register (MAR)** | The page number the chef turns to |
| **Memory Data Register (MDR)** | The actual words written on that page (the recipe line) |
| **Instruction Register (IR)** | The chef's *short-term memory* holding the line he just read |
| **Decode** | The chef *understanding* the instruction ("chop 3 onions") |
| **ALU (Execute)** | The chef's *hands* performing the cutting and cooking |
| **Store** | The plated dish being sent to the customer |
| **Clock Signal** | The kitchen timer ticking at a steady rhythm |

Just as the chef loops back to read the *next* line after finishing a step, the CPU loops back to the **fetch** stage, incrementing the PC, until the entire program is complete.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The Fetch-Execute cycle is *not* a single event — it is a **continuous, repetitive loop**. Modern pipelined processors overlap multiple cycles, but the *logical* steps remain identical.

## 1.3 The Two Sub-Cycles

The complete Instruction Cycle is conventionally partitioned into:

1. **Fetch Cycle** — retrieves the instruction from memory (universal for all instructions).
2. **Execute Cycle** — decodes and performs the operation (varies per instruction).

The fetch portion typically consumes a **fixed** number of clock cycles, while the execute portion varies based on the opcode and addressing mode. In KTU exam problems, students are often asked to calculate total execution time as:

$$T_{\text{total}} = N \times (C_{\text{fetch}} + C_{\text{execute}}) \times T_{\text{clock}}$$

where **N** = number of instructions, and **C** = clock cycles per phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Breakdown of the Cycle

The CPU performs the following micro-operations sequentially, governed by the **Control Unit (CU)**:

### **Step 1 — Fetch Phase**

- **MAR ← PC** : The address of the next instruction (held in the Program Counter) is loaded into the Memory Address Register.
- **Read Signal Asserted** : The CU activates the memory's read line.
- **MDR ← Memory[MAR]** : The instruction word travels from RAM into the Memory Data Register via the data bus.
- **IR ← MDR** : The instruction is copied into the Instruction Register for decoding.
- **PC ← PC + 1** : The PC is incremented (in byte-addressable systems, this may be `PC + 4` for 32-bit instructions) so that the next instruction is ready to be fetched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why increment PC after fetch (not after execute)?** Because the CPU may *jump* during execution, but the *current* instruction must be retained in the IR. The PC always points to the **next** instruction in linear flow.

### **Step 2 — Decode Phase**

- The **opcode** bits of the IR are fed into the CU's instruction decoder (typically a PLA or hardwired logic).
- The decoder activates the appropriate **control signals** (e.g., `ALU_OP = ADD`, `REG_WRITE = 1`).
- Operand fields are parsed to identify source/destination registers or memory addresses.

### **Step 3 — Execute Phase**

The action here is **instruction-dependent**:
- **Arithmetic (ADD, SUB):** Operands are routed into the ALU, the operation is performed, and the result is placed on the internal bus.
- **Memory Access (LOAD/STORE):** Effective address is calculated, then memory read/write occurs.
- **Control Flow (JUMP, BRANCH):** The PC is overwritten with the target address, breaking linear flow.
- **I/O Operations:** Data is transferred to/from peripheral registers (memory-mapped I/O).

### **Step 4 — Store (Write-Back) Phase**

- The result from the ALU (or memory) is written back to the **destination register** (e.g., ACC or a GPR).
- Status flags in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) are updated.

### **Step 5 — Repeat**

The CU resets the control signals and returns to Step 1, fetching the next instruction from the updated PC.

## 2.2 CPU Registers Involved in the Cycle

| Register | Full Name | Function | Width (typical) |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of next instruction | 32 / 64 bits |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Buffers address sent to memory | Same as address bus |
| **MDR** | Memory Data Register | Buffers data coming from / going to memory | Same as data bus |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the currently executing instruction | Word size (e.g., 32 bits) |
| **ACC / GPRs** | Accumulator / General Purpose Registers | Hold operands and results | 32 / 64 bits |
| **PSW** | Program Status Word | Holds condition flags (Z, C, S, V) | 8–32 bits |
| **SP** | Stack Pointer | Points to top of call stack | 32 / 64 bits |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — Master these formulas for the ESE (End Semester Exam).**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | Duration of one clock cycle | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{\text{exec}} = \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Execution time of one instruction | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{\text{program}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Total time to execute N instructions | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{CPU Time} = \dfrac{\text{Instructions} \times \text{Clock Cycles}}{\text{Clock Rate}}$ | Amdahl-style performance metric | seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}} \times \text{CPI}}$ | Instructions per second | inst/s |
| 6 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 7 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | Performance gain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = \text{PC}_{\text{old}} + \text{Instruction Size}$ | Default sequential increment | bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** is the *average* number of clock cycles required per instruction. For a simple RISC-like processor, CPI ≈ 1; for a complex CISC machine, CPI can range from 2 to 15+.

## 2.4 Instruction Format (Simplified)

A typical machine instruction contains two logical fields:

$$\underbrace{\texttt{OPCODE}}_{\text{operation type}} \;\; \big\vert \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{OPERAND(S)}}_{\text{data / address}}$$

For example, a 16-bit instruction like `ADD R1, R2` may be encoded as:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}\hline \texttt{Opcode (4 bits)} & \texttt{Dest Reg (4 bits)} & \texttt{Source Reg (4 bits)} & \texttt{Unused (4 bits)} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the Fetch-Execute cycle is foundational for:

- **Compiler Design** — code generation optimization (instruction scheduling to reduce pipeline stalls).
- **Embedded Systems** — deterministic timing analysis for real-time systems (RTOS scheduling).
- **Cybersecurity** — understanding *buffer overflow* exploits (attacker overwrites the saved return address on the stack, hijacking the PC).
- **Processor Design** — pipelining, superscalar execution, branch prediction all optimize this cycle.
- **Performance Engineering** — Dhrystone and CoreMark benchmarks rely on measuring cycles for synthetic instruction mixes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Worked Numerical Problem

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 — Typical 7-Mark Question):**

> A processor has a clock frequency of **2.5 GHz**. A program contains **3.2 × 10⁸** instructions. The average CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) for this workload is measured to be **4.8**. Calculate:
> (a) The clock period.
> (b) The total CPU execution time.
> (c) The throughput in MIPS.
> (d) The total number of clock cycles consumed.

---

### **Part (a) — Clock Period**

We know the fundamental relation between frequency and period:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the given frequency** in standard SI units (Hz):

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the division:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to picoseconds for readability:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 400 \text{ ps}
$$

> **Answer (a):** The clock period is **0.4 ns** (or **400 picoseconds**).

---

### **Part (b) — Total CPU Execution Time**

Apply the standard execution-time formula:

$$
T_{\text{program}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute all known values:**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = (3.2 \times 10^{8}) \times 4.8 \times (0.4 \times 10^{-9})
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply the constants first:**

$$
3.2 \times 4.8 = 15.36
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 0.4:**

$$
15.36 \times 0.4 = 6.144
$$

**Step 4 — Combine the powers of ten:**

$$
6.144 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{-9} = 6.144 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = 0.6144 \text{ seconds}
$$

> **Answer (b):** The total CPU execution time is **0.6144 seconds**.

---

### **Part (c) — Throughput in MIPS**

The MIPS rating formula is:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{4.8 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the powers of ten:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{3}}{4.8}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division:**

$$
\frac{2500}{4.8} = 520.833...
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\text{MIPS} \approx 520.83 \text{ MIPS}
$$

> **Answer (c):** The processor delivers approximately **520.83 Million Instructions Per Second**.

---

### **Part (d) — Total Number of Clock Cycles**

The total cycles is simply:

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = N \times \text{CPI}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute:**

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = (3.2 \times 10^{8}) \times 4.8
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply:**

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = 15.36 \times 10^{8} = 1.536 \times 10^{9} \text{ cycles}
$$

> **Answer (d):** The program consumes **1.536 × 10⁹ clock cycles** in total.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Fetch vs. Execute Phase Operations

The table below consolidates the **register transfer language (RTL)** for a generic instruction, which is a high-frequency exam topic:

| Stage | RTL Micro-Operation | Active Register(s) | Bus Used |
|-------|---------------------|--------------------|----------|
| **T0** | `MAR ← PC` | PC → MAR | Address Bus |
| **T1** | `MDR ← M[MAR]`, `Read = 1` | MAR → Memory, Memory → MDR | Data Bus |
| **T2** | `IR ← MDR`, `PC ← PC + 1` | MDR → IR; PC incremented | Internal |
| **T3** | `Decode IR[opcode]` | IR → CU | Internal |
| **T4** | `Operand fetch (if needed)` | IR → MAR; Memory → MDR | Address + Data |
| **T5** | `ALU executes operation` | Operands → ALU → ACC | Internal |
| **T6** | `Store result → destination` | ACC → Register/Memory | Data Bus |

## 3.3 Instruction Type vs. Execute Behavior

| Instruction Class | Example Opcode | Execute Action | Extra Cycles Needed |
|-------------------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Data Transfer** | MOV, LOAD, STORE | Register/Memory move | 1–2 |
| **Arithmetic** | ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV | ALU operation | 1 (DIV may take 10–40) |
| **Logical** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT | Bitwise ALU op | 1 |
| **Control Flow** | JMP, JZ, CALL, RET | PC ← target | 1 (plus pipeline flush) |
| **I/O** | IN, OUT | I/O bus transfer | 2–5 (handshake) |

## 3.4 Detailed Calculation — Branch Penalty in Pipelined Execution

While the GXEST203 module does not mandate pipeline internals, KTU frequently awards marks for understanding *why* branches disrupt flow.

**Example:** A 5-stage pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Writeback) with a **20% branch instruction rate** and a branch penalty of **2 cycles** (assuming no prediction). The effective CPI becomes:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{ideal}} + (\text{Branch Rate} \times \text{Branch Penalty})
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute ideal CPI = 1:**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + (0.20 \times 2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute:**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = 1 + 0.4 = 1.4
$$

> **Interpretation:** Branches inflate every "1-cycle" instruction into an average 1.4 cycles, reducing the throughput advantage of pipelining. This is why modern CPUs use **branch predictors** to reduce the penalty closer to 0.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Fetch-Execute Cycle

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical flow of the Instruction Cycle. It is safe-syntax compliant: all node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords are used, and labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B[POWER ON or RESET]
    B --> C[PC HOLDS ADDRESS OF NEXT INSTRUCTION]
    C --> D["FETCH PHASE: MAR LESS THAN PC"]
    D --> E[ASSERT MEMORY READ SIGNAL]
    E --> F[MDR LESS THAN MEMORY AT MAR]
    F --> G[IR LESS THAN MDR]
    G --> H[INCREMENT PC BY INSTRUCTION SIZE]
    H --> I[DECODE PHASE: CU READS IR OPCODE]
    I --> J{INSTRUCTION TYPE}
    J -->|ARITHMETIC| K[FETCH OPERANDS INTO ALU]
    J -->|MEMORY ACCESS| L[CALCULATE EFFECTIVE ADDRESS]
    J -->|BRANCH or JUMP| M[UPDATE PC WITH TARGET ADDRESS]
    K --> N[ALU PERFORMS OPERATION]
    L --> O[READ or WRITE MEMORY LOCATION]
    M --> P[FLUSH PIPELINE IF APPLICABLE]
    N --> Q[STORE PHASE: WRITE RESULT TO DESTINATION]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[UPDATE STATUS FLAGS IN PSW]
    R --> S{ALL INSTRUCTIONS DONE}
    S -->|NO| C
    S -->|YES| T[HALT or INTERRUPT]
    T --> U([END])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPU Internal Architecture

This diagram maps the **datapath** components used during each stage of the cycle. Use this as the reference for ESE diagrams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT]
        PC[PROGRAM COUNTER PC]
        MAR[MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER MAR]
        MDR[MEMORY DATA REGISTER MDR]
        IR[INSTRUCTION REGISTER IR]
        ACC[ACCUMULATOR ACC]
        ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU]
        CU[CONTROL UNIT CU]
        PSW[PROGRAM STATUS WORD PSW]
    end
    MEM[(PRIMARY MEMORY RAM)]
    BUS1{{SYSTEM BUS ADDRESS and DATA}}
    PC -->|ADDRESS| MAR
    MAR -->|READ SIGNAL| BUS1
    BUS1 -->|INSTRUCTION WORD| MDR
    MDR -->|COPY| IR
    IR -->|OPCODE| CU
    CU -->|CONTROL SIGNALS| ALU
    ACC -->|OPERANDS| ALU
    ALU -->|RESULT| ACC
    ALU -->|FLAGS| PSW
    CU -->|ENABLE| PC
    MEM --- BUS1
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Phases vs. Registers

A non-Mermaid ASCII schematic (allowed as fallback) showing the temporal sequence of register activity across one full instruction cycle:

```
+-------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
| CYCLE |   T0     |   T1     |   T2     |   T3     |  T4+   |
+-------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
|       | MAR<-PC  | MDR<-MEM | IR<-MDR  | DECODE   | EXEC   |
| ACTION|          | READ=1   | PC<-PC+1 | OPCODE   | ALU/   |
|       |          |          |          |          | MEM    |
+-------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
| BUS   | ADDRESS  | DATA IN  | INTERNAL | INTERNAL | DATA   |
| USED  | BUS      | BUS      | BUS      | BUS      | OUT    |
+-------+----------+----------+----------+----------+--------+
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Fetch-Execute cycle. List the four major phases of the cycle and briefly explain the role of the Program Counter (PC).**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **Fetch-Execute cycle** is the continuous operational loop performed by the CPU to retrieve, decode, and execute machine instructions stored in primary memory. The four major phases are:

1. **Fetch** — The address in the Program Counter is sent to memory (via MAR), and the instruction is read into the MDR, then transferred to the IR.
2. **Decode** — The Control Unit interprets the opcode bits of the IR to determine which operation to perform.
3. **Execute** — The ALU (or appropriate functional unit) carries out the operation.
4. **Store** — The result is written back to a register or memory, and status flags are updated.

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a special-purpose register that always holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched. After each fetch, the PC is automatically incremented (typically by the instruction size, e.g., 2, 4, or 8 bytes) so that sequential instructions are accessed in order. The PC is the *only* register that the Control Unit modifies to control program flow; **branch and jump instructions overwrite the PC** to redirect execution.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Fetch cycle and the Execute cycle. Why is the fetch cycle uniform across all instructions, while the execute cycle varies?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Aspect | Fetch Cycle | Execute Cycle |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| **Purpose** | Retrieves the *next* instruction from memory | Performs the operation specified by the instruction |
| **Uniformity** | **Uniform** — same steps for *every* instruction | **Variable** — depends on opcode and operands |
| **Registers Used** | PC, MAR, MDR, IR | IR, ACC, ALU, possibly MAR/MDR again |
| **Duration** | Fixed (constant clock cycles) | Variable (1 cycle for ADD, many for DIV) |
| **Bus Activity** | Always address + data bus read | May use data bus, ALU internal bus, or control bus |

**Reason for uniformity of fetch:** Every instruction, regardless of type, must first be *located* in memory and *loaded* into the IR for the CPU to act upon it. The hardware micro-operations for this are identical: send address, read memory, latch into IR, increment PC. Hence, the fetch microcode is fixed in the Control Unit.

**Reason for variability of execute:** Once decoded, an `ADD` requires the ALU, a `LOAD` requires memory access, a `JUMP` requires overwriting the PC, and an `I/O` instruction requires peripheral handshaking. Each requires different control signals, different datapaths, and different timing — hence, variable micro-operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[Internal Choice Provision per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]**
> Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a simple CPU** and describe the role of each register in the Fetch-Execute cycle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A processor operates at a clock frequency of **3 GHz**. A benchmark program consists of **1.5 × 10⁹ instructions** with an average CPI of **3.2**.
  - (i) Calculate the **clock period**.
  - (ii) Calculate the **total execution time** of the program.
  - (iii) Calculate the **MIPS rating** of the processor.
  - (iv) If the clock frequency is upgraded to **4 GHz** while CPI remains the same, what is the new execution time and the **speedup factor**? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A simple CPU consists of the following major units (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Control Unit (CU)** — Generates timing and control signals that orchestrate the fetch, decode, execute, and store phases. It contains the instruction decoder (typically a PLA).
2. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — Performs all arithmetic (add, subtract, multiply) and logical (AND, OR, XOR) operations. It outputs results to the accumulator and updates condition flags in the PSW.
3. **Register File** — A small, high-speed set of on-chip registers used for operands and intermediate results. Includes:
   - **PC (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction.
   - **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Buffers the address sent to memory.
   - **MDR (Memory Data Register):** Buffers the data read from or written to memory.
   - **IR (Instruction Register):** Holds the currently executing instruction.
   - **ACC (Accumulator):** Default destination for ALU results.
   - **PSW (Program Status Word):** Holds condition flags (Z, C, S, V).

**Sequence of register activity during one instruction:**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | `MAR ← PC` (address of next instruction sent to memory) |
| 2 | `Read = 1`; `MDR ← M[MAR]` (instruction fetched from RAM) |
| 3 | `IR ← MDR` (instruction loaded into decoder) |
| 4 | `PC ← PC + 1` (PC incremented for next cycle) |
| 5 | `Decode IR[opcode]` (CU activates control signals) |
| 6 | Execute: route operands to ALU, perform operation |
| 7 | `ACC ← ALU_result`; `PSW ← flags` |
| 8 | Return to step 1 |

*Mark distribution: 3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for register roles, 2 marks for sequence explanation.*

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Clock period:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 0.333 \text{ ns} = 333.33 \text{ ps}
$$

**(ii) Total execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{program}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{program}} = (1.5 \times 10^{9}) \times 3.2 \times (0.333 \times 10^{-9})
$$

$$
= 1.5 \times 3.2 \times 0.333 = 1.6 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iii) MIPS rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clock}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{9}}{3.2 \times 10^{6}} = 937.5 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(iv) New execution time at 4 GHz:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle,new}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.25 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = (1.5 \times 10^{9}) \times 3.2 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-9} = 1.2 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Speedup factor:**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1.6}{1.2} = 1.333
$$

*Mark distribution: (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 1.5 marks, (iv) 2 marks.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Stored-Program Concept** of von Neumann architecture. How does the Fetch-Execute cycle implement this concept? Discuss one major limitation of this architecture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A microcontroller has a clock frequency of **50 MHz**. It executes a control loop with the following instruction mix:

  | Instruction Type | Count (× 10⁶) | CPI |
  |------------------|---------------|-----|
  | Arithmetic       | 40            | 1   |
  | Memory Load      | 30            | 2   |
  | Memory Store     | 20            | 2   |
  | Branch           | 10            | 3   |

  - (i) Calculate the **average CPI** of the program.
  - (ii) Calculate the **total execution time**.
  - (iii) Suggest **two optimizations** the firmware engineer can apply to reduce execution time. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The **Stored-Program Concept**, proposed by **John von Neumann** in 1945, states that both *program instructions* and *data* are stored in the **same primary memory** (RAM) in binary form. The CPU fetches instructions from this memory sequentially, processes them, and writes results back to the same memory. This contrasts with earlier designs (e.g., ENIAC) where programs were hard-wired via plugboards.

**Implementation via Fetch-Execute cycle:**

1. The PC holds the address of the next instruction.
2. The fetch phase reads this instruction from memory into the IR (treating memory as a uniform storage).
3. The execute phase may read operands from the *same* memory (or registers), and write results back to it.
4. Because instructions and data share the same bus and memory space, the cycle naturally implements the stored-program model.

**Major Limitation — The von Neumann Bottleneck:**

The single shared bus between the CPU and memory can carry **either** an instruction **or** a data word at any given time. This means the CPU is frequently **idle** while waiting for the bus to become free (during memory fetches). The result is a severe throughput limitation — known as the **von Neumann bottleneck** — which modern architectures mitigate via **caches**, **separate instruction/data caches (Harvard architecture)**, and **prefetch buffers**.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for cycle mapping, 2 marks for limitation.*

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Average CPI:**

The total cycles = Σ(Count × CPI):

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = (40 \times 1) + (30 \times 2) + (20 \times 2) + (10 \times 3)
$$

$$
= 40 + 60 + 40 + 30 = 170 \text{ million cycles} = 1.7 \times 10^{8} \text{ cycles}
$$

Total instructions:

$$
N = (40 + 30 + 20 + 10) \times 10^{6} = 100 \times 10^{6} = 10^{8}
$$

Average CPI:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Total Cycles}}{N} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{8}}{10^{8}} = 1.7
$$

**(ii) Total execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{program}} = N \times \text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} \times T_{\text{cycle}} = 10^{8} \times 1.7 \times 20 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
= 1.7 \times 20 \times 10^{-1} = 3.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iii) Two optimizations:**

1. **Reduce Memory Accesses:** Use **register variables** and **inline assembly** for hot loops. For instance, accumulating a sum inside a register instead of repeatedly loading/storing it to RAM can eliminate many `CPI=2` memory instructions — directly lowering the average CPI.
2. **Minimize Branches or Use Branch Prediction:** The 10 million branch instructions consume 3 cycles each. **Loop unrolling** reduces branch count; using **conditional execution** (CMOV-type instructions) eliminates some branches entirely. If branches are reduced by half, total cycles fall by $10 \times 10^{6}$ (5 × 10⁶ cycles saved × 3 = 1.5 × 10⁷ cycles), reducing execution time by approximately 8.8%.

*Mark distribution: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert GHz to Hz** — a 2.5 GHz clock is $2.5 \times 10^9$ Hz, NOT $2.5$. Students often write $T = 1/2.5 = 0.4$ seconds (wrong by 9 orders of magnitude).
> 2. **Mixing up MIPS and MHz** — MIPS depends on CPI, not just frequency. Two processors at the same GHz can have very different MIPS.
> 3. **Not incrementing PC explicitly** — in flowchart answers, students often show "Fetch → Decode → Execute" but omit the `PC ← PC + 1` step. The KTU answer key deducts 1 mark for this.
> 4. **Confusing MAR and PC** — MAR is the *buffer* sent to memory's address pins; PC is the *logical* counter. They have the same value at fetch time but are different registers.
> 5. **Wrong unit for execution time** — always report time in seconds (or ms/µs/ns), NOT in clock cycles. Cycles must be multiplied by $T_{\text{cycle}}$ to give time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Read before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ The **Fetch-Execute cycle** has 4 phases: **Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store**, looping continuously.
- ☐ **Key registers**: PC, MAR, MDR, IR, ACC, PSW — know the full form and purpose of each.
- ☐ The **PC always increments** *after* the fetch, not after the execute. Branches/jumps overwrite the PC.
- ☐ **Fetch is uniform**; **Execute is variable** — this is a frequently asked KTU 2-mark question.
- ☐ Master the **5 formula families**:
   - $T_{\text{cycle}} = 1/f$
   - $T_{\text{program}} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$
   - $\text{MIPS} = f / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$
   - $\text{Speedup} = T_{\text{old}} / T_{\text{new}}$
   - $\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \Sigma (N_i \times \text{CPI}_i) / \Sigma N_i$
- ☐ **Stored-Program Concept** (von Neumann, 1945): instructions and data share the same memory.
- ☐ **Von Neumann Bottleneck**: shared bus limits throughput; mitigated by caches and Harvard-style separation.
- ☐ Always show the **RTL (Register Transfer Language)** sequence in your answer: `MAR ← PC`, `MDR ← M[MAR]`, `IR ← MDR`, `PC ← PC + 1`.
- ☐ Convert all units to **SI base units** (Hz, seconds) before plugging into formulas.
- ☐ Draw a **neat block diagram** with the CPU sub-block containing CU, ALU, Register File, and an external Memory block connected via the system bus.
- ☐ **CPI ranges**: RISC ≈ 1, CISC ≈ 2–15, simple accumulator machines ≈ 4–8.
- ☐ Branch and jump instructions **break linear flow** by overwriting the PC; always mention this when explaining control flow.
- ☐ Status flags in **PSW** (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow) are set/cleared by the ALU after every arithmetic operation.
- ☐ **Performance** improves either by increasing $f$, decreasing CPI, or reducing $N$ (instruction count via compiler optimization).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer System Software - Operating Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computer System Software - Operating Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computer Software — The Hierarchical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> *Software* is a collection of **instructions, data, and programs** that instruct a computer system on **what to do**, **how to do it**, and **when to do it**, in contrast to the physical hardware on which it runs. It is a non-tangible, logical component of a computing system.

Computer software is broadly classified into two master categories that sit on top of the raw hardware:

| Tier | Category | Core Purpose | Direct User Interaction? |
|------|----------|--------------|--------------------------|
| Layer 1 | **System Software** | Manages hardware resources and provides a platform for application software to run. | **No** (Indirect) |
| Layer 2 | **Application Software** | Performs specific end-user tasks (MS Word, Chrome, Photoshop). | **Yes** (Direct) |

### 1.1.1 System Software — The "Invisible Engine"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — System Software:**
> System software is a category of computer programs designed to **operate, control, and extend the processing capabilities of the computer hardware**. It acts as a *bridge* between the user, the application software, and the bare-metal hardware. It is **pre-installed** (in most cases) and is essential for the basic functioning of the system.

**Major Sub-Components of System Software:**
1. **Operating System (OS)** — The master control program (e.g., Windows 11, Ubuntu 24.04, macOS Sonoma, Android 15).
2. **Device Drivers** — Hardware-specific translators (e.g., NVIDIA GPU driver, Realtek Audio driver).
3. **Firmware** — Low-level software etched into ROM/Flash (e.g., BIOS/UEFI, router firmware).
4. **Utility Programs** — Maintenance tools (e.g., Disk Defragmenter, Antivirus, Backup software).
5. **Language Translators** — Assemblers, Compilers, Interpreters (e.g., GCC for C, javac for Java).
6. **Linkers and Loaders** — Combine and load object files into RAM for execution.

### 1.1.2 Application Software — The "User-Facing Layer"

These are programs written to fulfill specific user requirements. They are *dependent* on the system software (OS) for execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples:** Microsoft Office Suite, Google Chrome, WhatsApp Desktop, AutoCAD, MySQL Server, VLC Media Player, LibreOffice Calc.

---

## 1.2 Operating System — The Supreme Coordinator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition — Operating System:**
> An Operating System (OS) is a **large, complex, interrupt-driven, multitasking system program** that **manages all hardware and software resources** of a computer system. It provides a set of **services to application programs** and acts as an **interface between the user and the computer hardware**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Examinations):**
> Modern operating systems typically boot in **< 30 seconds** (measured as **Time-to-Ready**), maintain a process table capacity ranging from **few hundred to millions of PIDs** (Process IDs), and support **multi-user concurrency** with context-switch times in the order of **microseconds (μs)** to **milliseconds (ms)**.

### 1.2.1 The Three Foundational Roles of an OS

The OS simultaneously plays **three distinct roles**, which together form the **Trinity Model of Operating System Functions**:

**Role 1 — The OS as a Resource Manager (Referee / Traffic Police)**
- Allocates **CPU time** (via scheduling algorithms like Round Robin, FCFS).
- Allocates **RAM memory** (via paging, segmentation, virtual memory).
- Allocates **I/O devices** (via spooling, buffering, interrupt handling).
- Allocates **Storage space** (via file systems like NTFS, ext4, APFS).

**Role 2 — The OS as an Extended Machine / Virtual Machine (Translator)**
- **Hides the complexity** of hardware from the application programmer.
- Provides **abstract, clean APIs** (e.g., `open()`, `read()`, `write()` POSIX calls) instead of messy hardware register manipulation.
- Allows programmers to write portable code — *"Write Once, Run Anywhere."*

**Role 3 — The OS as a User Interface (Front Desk)**
- **CLI (Command Line Interface):** Bash, PowerShell, CMD, Zsh.
- **GUI (Graphical User Interface):** Windows Desktop, GNOME, KDE Plasma, macOS Aqua.
- **TUI (Touch-based UI):** Android, iOS, iPadOS.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Hotel Manager

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Operating System is like the Manager of a Grand Hotel.**
>
> - The **Hotel Building** = The Computer Hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, I/O).
> - The **Guests** = The Application Programs (Chrome, Word, Games).
> - The **Hotel Manager (OS)** receives guests, assigns rooms (memory), allocates dining time slots (CPU scheduling), manages the kitchen, housekeeping, and ensures guests don't fight over resources. Without the manager, the hotel would be chaos — rooms double-booked, food uneaten, guests lost.
>
> Just as the hotel manager **never sleeps**, the OS is **always running** (the kernel remains in memory from boot to shutdown — this is called *residing in kernel space*).

### 1.4 The System Software Stack — Visual Layering

```
+-----------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 4 : END-USER APPLICATION         |   (User's Browsers, Games, Editors)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 3 : APPLICATION SOFTWARE         |   (General Productivity Tools)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 2 : SYSTEM SOFTWARE (OS)         |   ★ The Operating System Kernel ★
+-----------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 1 : DEVICE DRIVERS + FIRMWARE    |   (Hardware-Specific Translators)
+-----------------------------------------+
|  LAYER 0 : RAW HARDWARE                 |   (CPU, RAM, SSD, GPU, NIC, Bus)
+-----------------------------------------+
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:**
> Students often confuse **firmware** with the **OS**. Firmware is stored in **non-volatile ROM/Flash** on the motherboard (e.g., BIOS/UEFI) and is the *first* code that runs at power-on. The OS is loaded *from disk into RAM* by the firmware. They are **distinct layers** in the boot sequence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Functions of an Operating System (KTU Board-Favorite Topic)

An OS performs **five core management functions**, which examiners frequently ask students to list and explain for 3 to 7 marks. Memorize this as a single mnemonic: **"P-M-F-I-D"** (Process, Memory, File, I/O, Deadlock/Security).

### Function 1 — Process Management
- A **process** is a program in execution. It is a *dynamic* entity, unlike a program (which is static code on disk).
- The OS is responsible for **creating, scheduling, pausing, resuming, and terminating** processes.
- Uses a **Process Control Block (PCB)** — a data structure that stores PID, state, program counter, registers, memory limits, etc.

### Function 2 — Memory Management (Primary / Main Memory / RAM)
- Tracks every byte of RAM — which block is in use, by whom, and for how long.
- Implements **virtual memory** so that processes can use more memory than physically present.
- Algorithms: **First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit** (contiguous allocation) and **Paging, Segmentation** (non-contiguous).

### Function 3 — File Management
- Organizes data into **files** and **directories** (folders) using a **File System** (FAT32, NTFS, ext4, APFS, ZFS, Btrfs).
- Handles **create, read, write, delete, rename, search** operations.
- Manages **file permissions** (Read/Write/Execute for Owner, Group, Others — the `rwx` triplet in Linux).

### Function 4 — I/O Device Management
- Manages communication with **keyboards, mice, printers, disk drives, network cards, USB devices**.
- Uses a **Device Driver** layer to abstract hardware-specific complexities.
- Employs techniques: **Buffering, Spooling (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line), Caching, DMA (Direct Memory Access).**

### Function 5 — Security, Protection & Deadlock Handling
- **Authentication** (passwords, biometrics, OTP).
- **Authorization** (permissions, access control lists).
- **Deadlock detection and recovery** (using the **Banker's Algorithm** by Dijkstra).

---

## 2.2 Types of Operating Systems (KTU Module 3 Sub-Topic)

| # | OS Type | Full Form | Key Trait | Real-World Example |
|---|---------|-----------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Batch OS** | Batch Operating System | Jobs batched & run sequentially. No user interaction. | IBM 1401, 1960s mainframes. |
| 2 | **Multiprogramming OS** | — | Multiple jobs in RAM; CPU switches when one waits for I/O. | Early Unix (1970s). |
| 3 | **Multitasking OS** | — | Logical concurrency; CPU time-sliced among many tasks. | Windows 11, macOS. |
| 4 | **Time-Sharing OS** | — | Many users share CPU via rapid switching. | Linux servers, mainframes. |
| 5 | **Real-Time OS (RTOS)** | Real-Time Operating System | Strict deadline-bound responses. | QNX, VxWorks (in cars, rockets). |
| 6 | **Distributed OS** | — | Manages a *group of networked computers* as one logical unit. | Google Borg/Kubernetes, Apache Mesos. |
| 7 | **Network OS** | — | Runs on a server; provides network services. | Windows Server 2022, CentOS Stream. |
| 8 | **Mobile OS** | — | Touch-optimized, battery-aware, sandboxed. | Android 15, iOS 18, HarmonyOS. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hard RTOS vs Soft RTOS:**
> - **Hard RTOS** — Missing a deadline is a *system failure* (e.g., airbags, pacemakers, anti-lock braking).
> - **Soft RTOS** — Missing a deadline is a *degradation* (e.g., video streaming, VoIP).

---

## 2.3 Booting — The Cold-Start to Fully-Loaded Journey

**Booting** is the sequence of events that occurs from the moment the power button is pressed until the OS login screen appears. It has **three major stages**:

**Stage 1 — Power-On Self-Test (POST)**
- Executed by the **firmware (BIOS/UEFI)** stored in ROM.
- Checks RAM size, keyboard presence, disk health, GPU basic functionality.
- Beep codes report errors (e.g., one short beep = success in Award BIOS).

**Stage 2 — Boot Loader Execution**
- The firmware searches for a bootable device (per configured boot order).
- Loads the **boot loader** (e.g., GRUB2 for Linux, Windows Boot Manager, systemd-boot).
- The boot loader locates the **kernel image** (e.g., `vmlinuz-6.8.0`) on the disk and loads it into RAM.

**Stage 3 — Kernel Initialization & User Space**
- The kernel initializes hardware drivers, mounts the root file system, and starts the **init system** (`systemd`, PID = 1).
- Background services (daemons) start, the login manager or desktop environment launches, and the user is presented with the **login prompt** (CLI or GUI).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Concept Cheat Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the most-asked quantities, formulas, and concept thresholds for the KTU 2024 ESE pattern. Use `\vert` (not `|`) when absolute value is needed in any future writing.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = 1 - p^{n}$ where $p$ = idle prob., $n$ = processes | Unitless, $0 \le U \le 1$ (or 0–100%) |
| 2 | **Throughput (Th)** | $Th = \dfrac{\text{Processes Completed}}{\text{Unit Time}}$ | processes / sec |
| 3 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = \text{Completion Time} - \text{Arrival Time}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 4 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - \text{Burst Time}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 5 | **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = \text{First CPU Allocation Time} - \text{Arrival Time}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 6 | **CPU Scheduling Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Burst Time}}{TAT} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ where $f$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = cores | Speedup factor |
| 8 | **Memory Fragmentation — Internal** | $I_{frag} = \text{Block Size} - \text{Process Size}$ | Bytes / KB |
| 9 | **Virtual Address Space (per process)** | $V = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = address bits (32-bit or 64-bit) | Bytes (4 GB or 16 EB) |
| 10 | **Context Switch Overhead (CSO)** | $CSO = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | microseconds (μs) |
| 11 | **Deadlock — Coffman Conditions** | All 4 must hold: Mutual Exclusion, Hold & Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait | Boolean (all TRUE = deadlock) |
| 12 | **Banker's Safe-State Test** | $Need_{i} \le Work$ for *some* $i$ at each iteration | Safe / Unsafe |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **TAT and WT** are the gold-standard metrics in **cloud computing SLA** (Service Level Agreements). Amazon EC2, Azure VMs quote response-time SLAs in the **99th-percentile latency**.
> - **Amdahl's Law** is the fundamental limit cited by chip designers at Intel, AMD, and ARM when justifying the move to **multi-core CPUs** instead of higher clock rates.
> - **Banker's Algorithm** is a teaching tool in academia; production kernels (Linux, Windows) use **wait-die / wound-wait** schemes for deadlock *prevention* instead.

---

## 2.5 OS Architecture — Kernel vs User Space

| Component | Privilege Ring | Who Uses It | Examples |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|----------|
| **Hardware (CPU, Disk, NIC)** | Ring 0 (Kernel) | OS Kernel, Device Drivers | x86 architecture: 4 rings (0–3) |
| **Kernel Mode** | Ring 0 | Core OS functions | Memory manager, scheduler, file system |
| **User Mode** | Ring 3 | Applications, Utilities | Chrome, MS Word, Notepad |
| **System Calls** | Bridge (Ring 0 $\leftrightarrow$ Ring 3) | Apps request OS services | `read()`, `write()`, `fork()`, `malloc()` |

> [!WARNING]
> A **system call** is **not** a regular function call. It involves a **mode switch** (trap to kernel) and back, costing roughly **100–1000 nanoseconds** — a measurable overhead in high-frequency trading and DBMS benchmarks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides fully worked-out step-by-step derivations and numerical solutions of the standard KTU 14-mark problems. **No steps are skipped**, and every algebraic transition is justified.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CPU Scheduling Metrics Calculation (FCFS Algorithm)

> **KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Pattern (Model Problem):**
> *The following processes arrive at time 0. Calculate the Average Waiting Time (AWT) and Average Turnaround Time (ATAT) using the FCFS (First Come First Serve) scheduling algorithm. Draw the Gantt chart.*
>
> | Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
> |---------|-------------------|-----------------|
> | P1      | 0                 | 4               |
> | P2      | 0                 | 3               |
> | P3      | 0                 | 1               |
> | P4      | 0                 | 2               |

### Step 1 — Establish the Order of Arrival

Since all four processes arrive at time $AT = 0$, we resolve ties by the **lexicographic order of process name** (P1 $\rightarrow$ P2 $\rightarrow$ P3 $\rightarrow$ P4), as is the standard convention in KTU answer keys.

### Step 2 — Construct the Gantt Chart

We track the cumulative time on the CPU timeline:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Time 0} \longrightarrow \text{Time 4 : P1 executes (Burst = 4)} \\
&\text{Time 4} \longrightarrow \text{Time 7 : P2 executes (Burst = 3)} \\
&\text{Time 7} \longrightarrow \text{Time 8 : P3 executes (Burst = 1)} \\
&\text{Time 8} \longrightarrow \text{Time 10 : P4 executes (Burst = 2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The Gantt chart is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\, & P1 \;\vert\; P2 \;\vert\; P3 \;\vert\; P4 \;\vert \\
0 \quad & 4 \quad 7 \quad 8 \quad 10
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note on absolute-value notation:** Per the KTU formatting protocol, we use the `\vert` character (not the table-breaking `pipe`) for the Gantt chart boundaries.

### Step 3 — Compute Completion Time (CT) for Each Process

The **Completion Time** is the right-edge of the Gantt chart block for that process:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 4 \\
CT_{P2} &= 4 + 3 = 7 \\
CT_{P3} &= 7 + 1 = 8 \\
CT_{P4} &= 8 + 2 = 10 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Turnaround Time (TAT)

Using the formula: $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 4 - 0 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P2} &= 7 - 0 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P3} &= 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P4} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Waiting Time (WT)

Using the formula: $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P2} &= 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P3} &= 8 - 1 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P4} &= 10 - 2 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Averages (Required for Full Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
AWT &= \dfrac{WT_{P1} + WT_{P2} + WT_{P3} + WT_{P4}}{4} \\
AWT &= \dfrac{0 + 4 + 7 + 8}{4} = \dfrac{19}{4} = 4.75 \text{ ms} \\
\\
ATAT &= \dfrac{TAT_{P1} + TAT_{P2} + TAT_{P3} + TAT_{P4}}{4} \\
ATAT &= \dfrac{4 + 7 + 8 + 10}{4} = \dfrac{29}{4} = 7.25 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> - **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** $= 4.75$ ms
> - **Average Turnaround Time (ATAT)** $= 7.25$ ms
> - FCFS is **non-preemptive** — once a process gets the CPU, it runs to completion.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Amdahl's Law Derivation for Multi-Core Speedup

> **KTU Model Problem:**
> *A program spends 80% of its time in parallelizable code and 20% in strictly serial code. Calculate the maximum theoretical speedup when executed on (a) 2 cores, (b) 4 cores, and (c) 16 cores. What is the asymptotic limit as the number of cores approaches infinity?*

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $f$ = parallelizable fraction of the program $= 0.80$ (i.e., **80%**)
- $(1-f)$ = serial fraction $= 0.20$ (i.e., **20%**)
- $N$ = number of processors / cores
- $T$ = original execution time (on a single core, $T = 1$ normalized unit)

### Step 2 — State Amdahl's Law (the Foundation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The reasoning: the serial portion $(1-f)$ cannot be parallelized at all, while the parallel portion $f$ is divided equally among $N$ processors, taking $f/N$ time.

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Speedup for N = 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(2) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{2}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.40} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.60} \\
&\approx 1.667 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Speedup for N = 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.40} \\
&= 2.500 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Speedup for N = 16

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{16}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.050} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.250} \\
&= 4.000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the Asymptotic Limit (N $\rightarrow \infty$)

As the number of cores $N$ grows without bound, the term $\dfrac{f}{N}$ approaches zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + 0} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1-f} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.80} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.20} \\
&= 5.000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The asymptotic speedup is **capped at 5x** even if we had **infinite cores**. This is precisely why **Amdahl's Law is the doom of pure multi-core scaling** — the serial portion becomes the bottleneck. Modern chip designers mitigate this by **reducing the serial fraction** through techniques like hardware-accelerated serialization points, lock-free data structures, and SIMD vectorization.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Banker's Algorithm Safe State Check

> **KTU Model Problem:**
> *A system has 5 processes (P0 to P4) and 3 resource types A, B, C. The current allocation, maximum demand, and available resources are given below. Determine if the system is in a safe state. If yes, print the safe sequence.*
>
> | Process | Allocation (A, B, C) | Maximum (A, B, C) |
> |---------|----------------------|---------------------|
> | P0      | (0, 1, 0)            | (7, 5, 3)           |
> | P1      | (2, 0, 0)            | (3, 2, 2)           |
> | P2      | (3, 0, 2)            | (9, 0, 2)           |
> | P3      | (2, 1, 1)            | (2, 2, 2)           |
> | P4      | (0, 0, 2)            | (4, 3, 3)           |
> | **Available** | **(3, 3, 2)** | — |

### Step 1 — Compute the Need Matrix

Using $Need_i = Max_i - Alloc_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Need_{P0} &= (7,5,3) - (0,1,0) = (7,4,3) \\
Need_{P1} &= (3,2,2) - (2,0,0) = (1,2,2) \\
Need_{P2} &= (9,0,2) - (3,0,2) = (6,0,0) \\
Need_{P3} &= (2,2,2) - (2,1,1) = (0,1,1) \\
Need_{P4} &= (4,3,3) - (0,0,2) = (4,3,1) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Initialize the Work Vector

Initial Work = Available = $(3, 3, 2)$.

### Step 3 — Iterative Safety Algorithm

We attempt to find a process $P_i$ such that $Need_i \le Work$, execute it, and then add its allocation back: $Work = Work + Alloc_i$.

**Iteration 1:**
Check $Need_{P0} = (7,4,3)$. Is $(7,4,3) \le (3,3,2)$? **No** — $7 > 3$.
Check $Need_{P1} = (1,2,2)$. Is $(1,2,2) \le (3,3,2)$? **Yes!**
Execute P1, then $Work = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,3,2)$. Mark P1 as Finished.

**Iteration 2:**
Check $Need_{P0} = (7,4,3)$. Is $(7,4,3) \le (5,3,2)$? **No** — $7 > 5$.
Check $Need_{P2} = (6,0,0)$. Is $(6,0,0) \le (5,3,2)$? **No** — $6 > 5$.
Check $Need_{P3} = (0,1,1)$. Is $(0,1,1) \le (5,3,2)$? **Yes!**
Execute P3, then $Work = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)$. Mark P3 as Finished.

**Iteration 3:**
Check $Need_{P0} = (7,4,3)$. Is $(7,4,3) \le (7,4,3)$? **Yes!**
Execute P0, then $Work = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3)$. Mark P0 as Finished.

**Iteration 4:**
Check $Need_{P2} = (6,0,0)$. Is $(6,0,0) \le (7,5,3)$? **Yes!**
Execute P2, then $Work = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,5)$. Mark P2 as Finished.

**Iteration 5:**
Check $Need_{P4} = (4,3,1)$. Is $(4,3,1) \le (10,5,5)$? **Yes!**
Execute P4, then $Work = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10,5,7)$. Mark P4 as Finished.

### Step 4 — Final Result

All five processes finished successfully. The system is in a **SAFE STATE** with the safe sequence:

$$
\boxed{\langle P1,\; P3,\; P0,\; P2,\; P4 \rangle}
$$

> [!TIP]
> If at *any* iteration no $Need_i \le Work$ condition is satisfied, the system is in an **unsafe state**, and the Banker's algorithm would *deny* a new resource request that would push the system into potential deadlock.

## 4.4 CPU Scheduling Flow — FCFS State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> nodeNew: Process Created
    nodeNew --> nodeReady: Admitted to Ready Queue
    nodeReady --> nodeRunning: Scheduler Dispatches
    nodeRunning --> nodeTerminated: Burst Complete
    nodeRunning --> nodeReady: Preemption FCFS NO PREEMPT
    nodeTerminated --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** In FCFS, the transition `nodeRunning → nodeReady` is *not used* (no preemption). This is why FCFS suffers from the **Convoy Effect** — one long process at the head of the queue blocks all short processes behind it.
