# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define an Operating System. List any four of its major functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> An Operating System (OS) is a system software program that acts as an **interface between the user and the computer hardware**, while managing all hardware and software resources of the system.

> **Four Major Functions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Process Management** — Creation, scheduling, and termination of processes.
> 2. **Memory Management** — Allocation and de-allocation of RAM among running processes.
> 3. **File Management** — Organization, storage, retrieval, and protection of files via a file system.
> 4. **I/O Device Management** — Coordination of input/output operations via device drivers and buffering.

> *(Optional 5th function for extra credit: Security & Protection / Deadlock handling.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between system software and application software with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | System Software | Application Software |
|--------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Purpose** | Manages hardware and provides a platform for apps | Performs specific user-defined tasks |
| **User Interaction** | Indirect (background) | Direct (foreground) |
| **Dependency** | Required to boot & run the system | Depends on system software to run |
| **Examples (1)** | Operating System (Windows 11) | MS Word |
| **Examples (2)** | Device Driver (NVIDIA GPU Driver) | Google Chrome |

> **Award 1 mark** for the distinction, **1 mark** for examples, and **1 mark** for the clear tabular comparison.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice A) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** Explain the major functions of an Operating System in detail with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system has the following process details. Compute the **Average Waiting Time (AWT)** and **Average Turnaround Time (ATAT)** using the **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** scheduling algorithm. Draw the Gantt chart. **(7 marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|
| P1      | 0                 | 7               |
| P2      | 2                 | 4               |
| P3      | 4                 | 1               |
| P4      | 5                 | 4               |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The OS performs the following **five major functions**:

1. **Process Management (1.5 marks):**
   - A *process* is a program in execution. The OS maintains a **Process Control Block (PCB)** for each.
   - The OS uses **CPU schedulers** (long-term, short-term, medium-term) to decide which process runs.
   - Examples: Round Robin, FCFS, SJF, Priority scheduling.

2. **Memory Management (1.5 marks):**
   - Allocates and de-allocates RAM to processes.
   - Implements **virtual memory** to allow processes to use more memory than physically available.
   - Techniques: Paging, Segmentation, Swapping.
   - Example: Linux swap partition / Windows `pagefile.sys`.

3. **File Management (1.5 marks):**
   - Provides a hierarchical directory structure.
   - Manages file metadata, permissions, and storage.
   - Examples of file systems: **NTFS** (Windows), **ext4** (Linux), **APFS** (macOS).

4. **I/O Device Management (1.5 marks):**
   - Uses **device drivers** to communicate with peripherals.
   - Employs **buffering, caching, spooling, and DMA** for efficient I/O.
   - Example: A printer spooler queues print jobs while the user continues working.

5. **Security & Protection (1 mark):**
   - Enforces **authentication** (login) and **authorization** (file permissions).
   - Handles **deadlock detection** using the Banker's algorithm or Ostrich algorithm.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Apply SJF (Shortest Job First, Non-Preemptive) policy:**

At time $t = 0$, only P1 is in the ready queue. P1 starts execution (despite being long, there is no choice yet).

P1 runs from $t=0$ to $t=7$. At $t=7$, the ready queue contains P2, P3, P4 (all have arrived: P2 at 2, P3 at 4, P4 at 5).

Choose the process with the **shortest burst time**:
- P2: burst = 4
- P3: burst = 1  ← **shortest**
- P4: burst = 4

P3 runs from $t=7$ to $t=8$. After P3 finishes, choose between P2 and P4 (both have burst 4). Tie-break by arrival time: P2 (arrived at 2) before P4 (arrived at 5), so P2 runs next.

P2 runs from $t=8$ to $t=12$. P4 runs last from $t=12$ to $t=16$.

**Gantt Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert\, P1 \;\vert\; P3 \;\vert\; P2 \;\vert\; P4 \;\vert \\
0 \quad 7 \quad 8 \quad 12 \quad 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CT, TAT, and WT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 7, \quad TAT_{P1} = 7-0 = 7, \quad WT_{P1} = 7-7 = 0 \\
CT_{P2} &= 12, \quad TAT_{P2} = 12-2 = 10, \quad WT_{P2} = 10-4 = 6 \\
CT_{P3} &= 8, \quad TAT_{P3} = 8-4 = 4, \quad WT_{P3} = 4-1 = 3 \\
CT_{P4} &= 16, \quad TAT_{P4} = 16-5 = 11, \quad WT_{P4} = 11-4 = 7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AWT &= \dfrac{0 + 6 + 3 + 7}{4} = \dfrac{16}{4} = 4.0 \text{ ms} \\
ATAT &= \dfrac{7 + 10 + 4 + 11}{4} = \dfrac{32}{4} = 8.0 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** AWT = 4.0 ms, ATAT = 8.0 ms. Note that SJF achieves a **better AWT than FCFS** for the same workload.

---

### Question B1 (Choice B) [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model Paper]
**(a)** Describe the **boot process** of a computer system in detail, listing all three major stages with the components involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **virtual memory** and **paging**. How does the OS use the **page table** for address translation? A system uses a **32-bit logical address** with a **page size of 4 KB**. Compute the number of pages and the number of frames. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The **booting process** is the sequence of operations that brings a computer from a powered-off state to a fully operational one. It is divided into **three major stages**:

**Stage 1 — Power-On Self-Test (POST) [2 marks]:**
- Triggered the moment the power button is pressed.
- The **firmware (BIOS — Basic Input/Output System, or its modern replacement UEFI — Unified Extensible Firmware Interface)** stored in a ROM chip on the motherboard begins execution.
- The POST checks critical hardware: **RAM size and integrity, keyboard presence, mouse, monitor, disk drives, and basic GPU functionality**.
- Failures are reported via **beep codes** (e.g., Award BIOS: 1 short beep = success, 1 long + 2 short = video card failure).

**Stage 2 — Boot Loader Execution [2.5 marks]:**
- After POST, the firmware scans configured boot devices (HDD, SSD, USB, DVD) in the user-defined **boot order**.
- It locates the **Master Boot Record (MBR)** (first 512 bytes of disk) for legacy BIOS, or the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** for UEFI.
- The boot loader (e.g., **GRUB2** for Linux, **Windows Boot Manager** for Windows) is loaded into RAM and executed.
- The boot loader's job is to find the **OS kernel image** on the disk (e.g., `vmlinuz-6.8.0-generic` for Ubuntu, `ntoskrnl.exe` for Windows).

**Stage 3 — Kernel Initialization [2.5 marks]:**
- The kernel image is **loaded into RAM** and execution transfers to the kernel's entry point.
- The kernel initializes its own data structures: **process table, memory manager, file system caches, and device drivers**.
- The kernel **mounts the root file system** (e.g., `/dev/sda1` on Linux, `C:\` on Windows).
- The **init system** (e.g., `systemd` on Linux with PID 1) is started.
- Background daemons (network manager, display manager, audio server) come online.
- Finally, the **login manager** (GDM, LightDM, Windows Logon) presents the user with the **login screen**.

> **Typical Boot Time on Modern Systems:** 10–25 seconds (Windows 11 with NVMe SSD: ~12 seconds; Ubuntu 24.04: ~8 seconds).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Explain Virtual Memory [2 marks]:**
- **Virtual memory** is a memory management technique that creates the **illusion of a large, contiguous logical address space** for each process, even if the physical RAM is smaller.
- It allows processes to use more memory than physically present by **swapping pages between RAM and disk** (the *swap space*).
- Benefits: (i) Process isolation, (ii) Efficient memory use, (iii) Ability to run larger programs.

**Step 2 — Explain Paging [2 marks]:**
- **Paging** divides the **logical address space** into fixed-size blocks called **pages**, and the **physical memory** into blocks of the same size called **frames**.
- Eliminates **external fragmentation** (although it may cause minor internal fragmentation on the last page).

**Step 3 — Address Translation & Page Table [1.5 marks]:**
- The **page table** is a data structure maintained by the OS that maps each **virtual page number (VPN)** to a **physical frame number (PFN)**.
- A logical address `<VPN, offset>` is split, the VPN is used to index the page table, the PFN is retrieved, and combined with the offset to form the **physical address**.
- This translation is hardware-accelerated by the **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** inside the CPU.
- A **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** caches recent translations to avoid repeated page-table lookups.

**Step 4 — Numerical Computation [1.5 marks]:**

Given: Logical address = 32 bits, Page size = 4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of pages} &= \dfrac{2^{32}}{2^{12}} = 2^{32-12} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ pages} \\
\text{Page offset bits} &= 12 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Virtual page number bits} &= 32 - 12 = 20 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If we assume physical memory is also addressed by 32 bits and the page/frame size is identical, then the number of **physical frames** is also $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ frames.

> **Generalization:** If physical address has $p$ bits, then number of frames = $2^{p-12}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Gantt Chart Boundary:** Use `\vert` (vertical bar in LaTeX math mode) for the Gantt chart delimiters, **NOT** the markdown table pipe. Wrong delimiter syntax breaks the renderer and the examiner will deduct ½ mark.
> 2. **Arrival Time vs Burst Time confusion:** In scheduling problems, many students compute $WT = TAT - AT$ instead of $WT = TAT - BT$. This is a classic 2-mark loss. Remember: **TAT uses Arrival Time; WT uses Burst Time.**
> 3. **Amdahl's Law Missing Asymptote:** When asked for speedup, always conclude with the **asymptotic limit** $1/(1-f)$. Examiners expect it for full marks.
> 4. **Banker's Algorithm Order:** Always recompute `Work = Work + Allocation` **after** a process is found safe. Students who update Work prematurely produce a wrong safe sequence.
> 5. **Boot Process Confusion:** Don't mix up BIOS and Boot Loader. BIOS is firmware on the motherboard; Boot Loader is loaded **by** the BIOS from the disk.
> 6. **Paging Calculation:** Page offset is determined by the **page size**, not the address space. Always compute $\log_2(\text{page size})$ first to find the offset bits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Board-Exam Quick Recap — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam.**

- **System Software** runs the hardware and provides a platform for application software. Major components: **OS, Device Drivers, Firmware, Utilities, Language Translators, Linkers/Loaders**.
- **Operating System** is the master control program; acts as **Resource Manager + Extended Machine + User Interface**.
- **Five core functions of OS:** Process Management, Memory Management, File Management, I/O Management, Security & Deadlock Handling.
- **Process vs Program:** A *program* is a static file on disk; a *process* is a program in execution (dynamic, with a PCB).
- **Kernel Mode (Ring 0) vs User Mode (Ring 3):** Kernel mode has full hardware access; user mode is restricted. **System calls** bridge the two.
- **Boot Sequence:** POST $\rightarrow$ Boot Loader $\rightarrow$ Kernel Initialization $\rightarrow$ Init System $\rightarrow$ Login.
- **Firmware (BIOS/UEFI)** lives in ROM; the **OS Kernel** is loaded from disk into RAM.
- **FCFS:** Non-preemptive; suffers from the **Convoy Effect** (long process blocks all others).
- **SJF (Non-Preemptive):** Optimal AWT theoretically, but suffers from **starvation** of long processes.
- **Round Robin:** Preemptive; uses a **time quantum** (typically 10–100 ms). Fair but context-switch overhead matters.
- **TAT** $=$ Completion Time $-$ Arrival Time. **WT** $=$ TAT $-$ Burst Time. **Averages are always required** in scheduling problems.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = 1/[(1-f) + f/N]$. Asymptotic limit $= 1/(1-f)$. **Multi-core cannot save a program with a large serial fraction.**
- **Paging:** Logical address is split into **<page_number, offset>**. Page size is typically **4 KB ($2^{12}$ bytes)**. A 32-bit system has $2^{20}$ pages.
- **Virtual Memory** uses **swap space** on disk to extend RAM. Implemented via paging + page replacement algorithms (LRU, FIFO, Optimal).
- **Deadlock — Coffman's 4 Necessary Conditions:** **M**utual Exclusion, **H**old and Wait, **N**o Preemption, **C**ircular Wait. **All 4 must hold simultaneously.**
- **Banker's Algorithm** detects safe states by iteratively checking if `Need ≤ Work`. Output a **safe sequence** if one exists.
- **Types of OS:** Batch, Multiprogramming, Multitasking, Time-Sharing, Real-Time (Hard/Soft), Distributed, Network, Mobile.
- **Hard RTOS** = deadline miss is fatal. **Soft RTOS** = deadline miss is a degradation. Examples: QNX (Hard), Embedded Linux (Soft).
- **CLI** (Command Line Interface) vs **GUI** (Graphical User Interface) vs **TUI** (Touch User Interface) — all are *ways the user interacts with the OS*.
- **File System** examples: FAT32 (legacy), NTFS (Windows), ext4 (Linux), APFS (macOS/iOS), Btrfs (modern Linux).
- **System Call overhead:** approximately **100–1000 nanoseconds** per call — non-trivial in tight loops.
- **Final memory aid:** The OS is the **Hotel Manager**; hardware is the **Hotel**; applications are the **Guests**.
- **Always draw the Gantt Chart** in scheduling problems — examiners award partial marks for the chart even if final answers are wrong.

> [!TIP]
> **End of Module 3 Notes.** You now have a complete KTU 2024-scheme-aligned study pack covering definitions, theory, derivations, diagrams, and 14-mark model solutions. Re-read Sections 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3 the night before the exam — they are the most frequently repeated numerical question types.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Basic Commands in Linux / Windows** refer to the standardized, text-based instructions that a user issues to an Operating System (OS) kernel through a **Command-Line Interface (CLI)** or **Command Prompt / Terminal** to perform file management, directory navigation, system administration, process control, and networking operations without relying on a Graphical User Interface (GUI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Basic Commands in Linux/Windows are the user-issued instructions interpreted by the OS shell (Bash in Linux, CMD/PowerShell in Windows) to manipulate files, directories, processes, and system resources, forming the foundation of computational literacy for engineers."*

**Key Terminology Mapping:**

| Term | Linux (Bash) | Windows (CMD) |
|------|--------------|---------------|
| Shell | Bash / sh / zsh | cmd.exe / PowerShell |
| Prompt Symbol | `$` (user) / `#` (root) | `>` (C:\Users\...) |
| Directory | Folder | Folder |
| Root | `/` | `C:\` |
| Case-Sensitive | **Yes** | **No** |
| Path Separator | `/` (forward slash) | `\` (backslash) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Imagine the **Operating System** is a large professional restaurant kitchen:
- The **GUI (icons, menus, windows)** is like the *dining room* — pleasant, visual, but slow because the waiter (the OS) has to walk back and forth.
- The **CLI (Terminal/Command Prompt)** is the *Head Chef speaking directly to the cooks* — fast, precise, scriptable, and powerful.

When you type a command like `mkdir projects`, it's like telling the cook: *"Make me a new storage rack called 'projects' right here, right now."* The cook (the kernel) does it instantly without any visual decoration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Learn CLI Commands:**
> - **Speed:** CLI is significantly faster than clicking through menus.
> - **Automation:** Commands can be chained into scripts.
> - **Remote Access:** Servers (cloud, web, DB) are administered almost exclusively via CLI over SSH.
> - **Reproducibility:** A text file of commands documents *exactly* what was done.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Default Settings

- **Linux Default Shell:** Bash (Bourne Again SHell) — `bash --version` typically reports version **5.x**.
- **Windows Default Shell:** `cmd.exe` (Windows 10/11 also offers PowerShell **5.1+**).
- **PATH Environment Variable:** A colon-separated (Linux) / semicolon-separated (Windows) list of directories the shell searches for executable files.
- **Hidden File Prefix:** Files beginning with `.` in Linux (e.g., `.bashrc`); files with the **Hidden** attribute in Windows (`attrib +h`).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The `man` command in Linux stands for **manual**, and every standard command on a KTU lab machine can be queried: e.g., `man ls` opens the full reference page.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown — Anatomy of a Command

Every CLI instruction, regardless of OS, follows the same universal grammar:

$$
\underbrace{\text{Command}}_{\text{verb}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{[Options/Flags]}}_{\text{adverbs}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{[Arguments]}}_{\text{nouns}}
$$

**Example Decomposition:**

$$
\texttt{ls} \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{-l}}_{\text{long format}} \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{-a}}_{\text{include hidden}} \;\; \underbrace{\texttt{/home/student}}_{\text{target directory}}
$$

**The Three Pillars of CLI Interaction:**

1. **Standard Input (stdin) — File Descriptor `0`** — Where the command reads data (default: keyboard).
2. **Standard Output (stdout) — File Descriptor `1`** — Where the command writes normal results (default: terminal screen).
3. **Standard Error (stderr) — File Descriptor `2`** — Where error messages are sent (default: terminal screen).

The redirection operators `>`, `>>`, `<`, `|` manipulate these streams — a high-yield KTU topic.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Command Syntax Logic

### 3.1 Worked Example: A Multi-Command Workflow (Linux)

**Scenario:** A KTU student wants to create a project folder, navigate into it, create a file, list contents, and then copy it to a backup directory.

**Step-by-Step Logical Derivation:**

$$
\textbf{Step 1: Confirm where we are}
$$

$$
\texttt{pwd} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \texttt{/home/k2312345}
$$

*Logic:* `pwd` (Print Working Directory) returns the absolute path of the current location. This is essential before any modification to prevent accidental damage.

$$
\textbf{Step 2: Create a new project directory}
$$

$$
\texttt{mkdir MyProject}
$$

*Logic:* `mkdir` (make directory) is the verb. `MyProject` is the argument (the noun — the name of the new entity). No options are used because we want the simplest behavior.

$$
\textbf{Step 3: Move into the new directory}
$$

$$
\texttt{cd MyProject}
$$

*Logic:* `cd` (change directory) is the verb. `MyProject` is a *relative path* (relative to current location `~`). To use an *absolute path*, we would write `cd /home/k2312345/MyProject`.

$$
\textbf{Step 4: Verify the move}
$$

$$
\texttt{pwd} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \texttt{/home/k2312345/MyProject}
$$

*Logic:* The shell updates the working directory after `cd`. This is a **state change** — the shell's CWD variable is now updated.

$$
\textbf{Step 5: Create an empty file}
$$

$$
\texttt{touch notes.txt}
$$

*Logic:* `touch` updates the file's access/modification timestamp; if the file does not exist, it creates an empty one with size 0 bytes.

$$
\textbf{Step 6: List contents (with details)}
$$

$$
\texttt{ls -l}
$$

*Logic:* The `-l` flag invokes the *long format* option. Output structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{total } 0 \\
\text{-rw-r--r-- 1 k2312345 students 0 Oct 14 10:32 notes.txt}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Decoding the output:*
- `-rw-r--r--` → file type (`-`) + permissions (owner: `rw-`, group: `r--`, others: `r--`).
- `1` → number of hard links.
- `k2312345` → owner user.
- `students` → owner group.
- `0` → file size in bytes.
- `Oct 14 10:32` → last modification time.

$$
\textbf{Step 7: Make a backup copy}
$$

$$
\texttt{cp notes.txt notes.bak}
$$

*Logic:* `cp` (copy) reads `notes.txt` and writes an identical byte stream to `notes.bak`. The original remains untouched. This is a **non-destructive** operation.

$$
\textbf{Step 8: List with hidden and detailed view}
$$

$$
\texttt{ls -la}
$$

*Logic:* `-a` includes hidden files (those starting with `.`). On a fresh directory, `.` and `..` will appear.

$$
\textbf{Step 9: Move backup to a sibling directory}
$$

$$
\texttt{mkdir ../backup} \quad\quad \texttt{mv notes.bak ../backup/}
$$

*Logic:* First we create `backup` in the parent directory (`..`). Then `mv` (move) relocates the file. Note: on the **same filesystem**, `mv` is a rename (instant); **across filesystems**, it becomes copy + delete.

$$
\textbf{Step 10: Remove the original}
$$

$$
\texttt{rm notes.txt}
$$

*Logic:* `rm` (remove) deletes the file. In Linux, there is **no recycle bin** by default — deletion is permanent. To force, use `rm -f`; to be interactive (prompt first), use `rm -i`.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Permission Modification (chmod)

The `chmod` command in Linux modifies file permissions using either **symbolic** or **octal (numeric)** notation.

**Octal Mode Derivation:**

$$
\text{Permission} = r (\text{read}) = 4, \quad w (\text{write}) = 2, \quad x (\text{execute}) = 1
$$

For each of the three classes (user/owner `u`, group `g`, others `o`), sum the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{rwx} &= 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 \\
\texttt{rw-} &= 4 + 2 + 0 = 6 \\
\texttt{r-x} &= 4 + 0 + 1 = 5 \\
\texttt{r--} &= 4 + 0 + 0 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Calculation:**

Make a script executable for the owner only, readable for group and others:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } rwx &= 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 \\
\text{Group: } r-- &= 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 \\
\text{Others: } r-- &= 4 + 0 + 0 = 4 \\
\hline
\therefore \texttt{chmod 744 script.sh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbolic Equivalent:**

$$
\texttt{chmod u+x,go=r script.sh}
$$

*Logic:* `u+x` adds execute for user; `go=r` sets group and others to read-only.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — I/O Redirection

| Operator | Syntax | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|
| `>` | `cmd > file` | Redirect stdout to file (overwrite) |
| `>>` | `cmd >> file` | Append stdout to file |
| `<` | `cmd < file` | Read stdin from file |
| `2>` | `cmd 2> err.log` | Redirect stderr to file |
| `&>` | `cmd &> all.log` | Redirect both stdout and stderr |
| `\|` (pipe) | `cmd1 \| cmd2` | stdout of cmd1 → stdin of cmd2 |

**Worked Pipe Example (Linux):**

List all `.txt` files containing the word "KTU":

$$
\texttt{ls *.txt} \;\;\vert\;\; \texttt{grep "KTU"}
$$

*Logic:* 
- `ls *.txt` produces a stream of filenames ending in `.txt` (glob expansion).
- The pipe `|` feeds that stream as stdin to `grep`.
- `grep "KTU"` filters and prints only lines containing "KTU".
- This is the **functional composition** of two processes — a fundamental CS concept.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing `>` (overwrite) with `>>` (append). Writing `> file.txt` on an existing file **destroys** its contents instantly.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Command Execution Flow — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps how a typed command travels through the OS layers to produce a result. This is a high-yield visual for KTU descriptive questions on "How does the shell work?".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Types Command in Terminal] --> B{Shell Parser}
    B --> C[Lexical Analysis: Tokenize into Command Options Arguments]
    C --> D[Path Resolution: Search PATH directories for executable]
    D --> E{Executable Found?}
    E -- No --> F[Throw 'command not found' error to stderr]
    E -- Yes --> G[Fork System Call: Create Child Process]
    G --> H[Exec System Call: Load executable into memory]
    H --> I[Kernel Allocates Resources: CPU Memory File Descriptors]
    I --> J[Process Runs: Reads stdin, Computes, Writes stdout/stderr]
    J --> K{Exit Status 0?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Return Prompt to User]
    K -- No --> M[Print non-zero error code to terminal]
    L --> A
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 File System Hierarchy — Linux vs Windows

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Linux Root: /] --> L2["/home (user files)"]
    L1 --> L3["/etc (config files)"]
    L1 --> L4["/bin (binaries)"]
    L1 --> L5["/var (logs)"]
    L1 --> L6["/tmp (temporary)"]
    L1 --> L7["/usr (user programs)"]
    L1 --> L8["/dev (devices)"]
    L1 --> L9["/proc (process info)"]
    W1[Windows Root: C:\] --> W2["C:\Users (profiles)"]
    W1 --> W3["C:\Program Files (apps)"]
    W1 --> W4["C:\Windows (OS)"]
    W1 --> W5["C:\ProgramData (shared config)"]
    W1 --> W6["C:\Temp (temp files)"]
    W1 --> W7["C:\System32 (system binaries)"]
```

### 4.3 Command Family Mind Map — Grouped by Function

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Basic CLI Commands))
    Navigation
      pwd

    File Operations
      cp / copy

      mkdir / md

      cat

    Search
      grep / findstr

```

### 4.4 Permission Bit Structure (Linux) — Octal Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[File: script.sh] --> B[User Owner: rwx = 7]
    A --> C[Group: r-x = 5]
    A --> D[Others: r-- = 4]
    B --> E["chmod 754 script.sh"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[755 in binary bit-pattern]
    F --> G["rwx r-x r-x"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each triple (`rwx`) represents the three bits for that class. `r-x` means "read + execute, but no write" — a typical setting for shared library files.

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the anatomy of a Linux command with a proper diagram. Describe the `chmod` command in detail, including both the symbolic and octal (numeric) notations, with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain any seven networking-related commands available in both Linux and Windows operating systems, with a note on the equivalent commands in each OS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Command Anatomy:** A Linux command has three logical parts: the **command** (the action verb), **options/flags** (modifiers prefixed by `-` or `--`), and **arguments** (the targets of the action, e.g., filenames).

$$
\texttt{[user@host ~]\$ ls -la /home/student}
$$

- `ls` → command (verb)
- `-la` → options (`-l` for long format, `-a` for hidden files — combined as `-la`)
- `/home/student` → argument (the directory to list)

**chmod — Change Mode:** The `chmod` command modifies the read (`r`), write (`w`), and execute (`x`) permissions of a file for three classes of users: the **owner (u)**, the **group (g)**, and **others (o)**.

**Octal Notation:** Each permission is assigned a numeric value: `r=4`, `w=2`, `x=1`. The values for each class are summed to form a 3-digit octal number.

**Example:** To make a shell script executable for the owner, readable and executable for the group, and unreadable/unwritable/unexecutable for others:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Owner: } rwx &= 4+2+1 = 7 \\
\text{Group: } r-x &= 4+0+1 = 5 \\
\text{Others: } --- &= 0+0+0 = 0 \\
\hline
\texttt{chmod 750 deploy.sh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbolic Notation:** Permissions can also be modified with `+`, `-`, `=`. Example: `chmod u+x,g+x,o-rwx deploy.sh` (equivalent to the octal `750`).

---

|---|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Test network connectivity to a host | `ping google.com` | `ping google.com` |
| 2 | Display IP address and interface configuration | `ip addr` (modern) or `ifconfig` (legacy) | `ipconfig` / `ipconfig /all` |
| 3 | Trace the route packets take to a host | `traceroute google.com` | `tracert google.com` |
| 4 | Query DNS records for a domain | `nslookup google.com` / `dig google.com` | `nslookup google.com` |
| 5 | Display active network connections and listening ports | `netstat -tuln` or `ss -tuln` | `netstat -an` |
| 6 | Download a file from the internet | `wget <URL>` or `curl -O <URL>` | `curl -O <URL>` (PowerShell) |
| 7 | Securely connect to a remote machine | `ssh user@host` | `ssh user@host` (OpenSSH client in Win 10/11) |
| 8 | Display the routing table | `ip route` / `route` | `route print` |
| 9 | Perform a port scan (security) | `nmap host` | — (third-party tools) |
| 10 | Flush the local DNS cache | `sudo systemd-resolve --flush-caches` | `ipconfig /flushdns` |
