### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the execution flow of a command typed in a Linux terminal — from the moment the user presses Enter until the result is displayed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the following Linux commands with their syntax and one example each: (i) `grep`, (ii) `find`, (iii) `tar`, (iv) `ps`, (v) `kill`, (vi) `chown`, (vii) `df`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The execution of a Linux command follows a well-defined pipeline:

1. **User Input:** The user types the command at the shell prompt and presses Enter. The shell reads the input line.

2. **Lexical Analysis / Tokenization:** The shell parses the line, splitting it into tokens: the command name, options, and arguments.

3. **Path Resolution:** The shell searches the directories listed in the `PATH` environment variable (e.g., `/usr/bin`, `/bin`, `/usr/local/bin`) to locate the executable file corresponding to the command.

4. **Fork:** The shell calls the `fork()` system call to create a child process — an identical copy of itself.

5. **Exec:** The child process calls `exec()` (e.g., `execve()`) to replace its memory image with the executable's code. At this point, the new program begins.

6. **Kernel Resource Allocation:** The kernel assigns a Process ID (PID), opens file descriptors (stdin=0, stdout=1, stderr=2), and schedules the process on the CPU.

7. **Execution:** The program runs, reads input, performs computation, and writes output.

8. **Exit:** When the program finishes, it returns an exit status (`0` = success, non-zero = error). The parent shell reads this status and prints the next prompt.

**Block Diagram:**

```
┌────────────────────┐
│  User types        │
│  command + Enter   │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  Shell Lexer       │
│  (Tokenization)    │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  Search PATH for   │
│  executable        │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  fork() — Create   │
│  child process     │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  exec() — Load     │
│  program into RAM  │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  Kernel runs the   │
│  process (PID)     │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  Process outputs   │
│  result to stdout  │
└─────────┬──────────┘
          │
          ▼
┌────────────────────┐
│  Exit → Shell      │
│  prints next prompt│
└────────────────────┘
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**(i) grep** — Searches for a pattern in a file or stream.
- **Syntax:** `grep [options] pattern [file...]`
- **Example:** `grep -i "ktu" results.txt` — finds lines containing "ktu" (case-insensitive) in `results.txt`.

**(ii) find** — Searches for files in a directory hierarchy.
- **Syntax:** `find [path] [expression]`
- **Example:** `find /home -name "*.c"` — finds all C source files under `/home`.

**(iii) tar** — Archives multiple files into a single file (tape archive).
- **Syntax:** `tar [options] archive.tar [files...]`
- **Example:** `tar -czvf backup.tar.gz MyProject/` — creates a gzipped archive of the `MyProject` folder.

**(iv) ps** — Reports a snapshot of current processes.
- **Syntax:** `ps [options]`
- **Example:** `ps aux` — lists all running processes with detailed info (user, PID, CPU%, memory, command).

**(v) kill** — Sends a signal to a process (usually to terminate it).
- **Syntax:** `kill [signal] PID`
- **Example:** `kill -9 1234` — forcefully (signal SIGKILL) terminates process with PID 1234.

**(vi) chown** — Changes the owner (and optionally group) of a file.
- **Syntax:** `chown [owner][:group] file`
- **Example:** `sudo chown alice:developers report.txt` — changes owner to `alice` and group to `developers`.

**(vii) df** — Reports disk space usage for all mounted filesystems.
- **Syntax:** `df [options] [file...]`
- **Example:** `df -h` — displays disk usage in human-readable format (GB, MB).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the OS context:** Writing "`ls -l`" alone in a Windows question loses a mark. Always clarify the OS.
> 2. **Wrong permission arithmetic:** `chmod 777` is the most-asked "explain" question. If you cannot derive `7 = rwx = 4+2+1`, you will lose 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `rm` and `rmdir`:** `rmdir` only removes **empty** directories; `rm -r` is needed for non-empty ones.
> 4. **Missing absolute vs. relative path distinction:** `cd MyProject` is *relative*; `cd /home/user/MyProject` is *absolute*.
> 5. **Not knowing the `>` vs `>>` difference** in redirection — a classic 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Spelling errors in command names** (`grpe` instead of `grep`) — examiners count these as wrong answers in practical exams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ☐ A command has three parts: **Command + Options + Arguments**.
- ☐ Linux is **case-sensitive**; Windows is **case-insensitive**.
- ☐ Linux uses `/`; Windows uses `\` as the path separator.
- ☐ Linux shell prompt ends with `$` (user) or `#` (root); Windows prompt ends with `>`.
- ☐ **Navigation:** `pwd`, `cd`, `ls` (Linux) ↔ `cd`, `dir` (Windows).
- ☐ **File ops:** `cp`, `mv`, `rm`, `cat`, `touch` (Linux) ↔ `copy`, `move`, `del`, `type` (Windows).
- ☐ **Directory ops:** `mkdir`, `rmdir` are nearly identical in both OSes.
- ☐ **Permissions:** `r=4, w=2, x=1`; three classes: user, group, others → `chmod 750`.
- ☐ **Redirection:** `>` overwrites, `>>` appends, `|` pipes stdout to next command.
- ☐ **Pipes (`|`)** chain commands: `cat file | grep "pattern"`.
- ☐ **Process control:** `ps` / `tasklist` (list), `kill PID` / `taskkill /PID` (terminate).
- ☐ **Networking:** `ping`, `ipconfig`/`ifconfig`/`ip addr`, `tracert`/`traceroute`, `nslookup`.
- ☐ **Help:** `man cmd` (Linux) ↔ `help cmd` or `cmd /?` (Windows).
- ☐ **Hidden files:** Linux uses `.` prefix; Windows uses the `Hidden` attribute.
- ☐ **Default shells:** Bash (Linux) vs. `cmd.exe` / PowerShell (Windows).
- ☐ **System info commands:** `uname -a`, `hostname`, `whoami`, `date`, `df -h`, `free -h`.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** Mastering these basic commands is the *gateway* skill to every higher computing subject — networking, databases, web servers, cybersecurity, and cloud computing. Practice every command in a live Linux terminal (Ubuntu/Fedora) and Windows Command Prompt before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shell Scripting (Bash)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shell Scripting (Bash)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shell**: A **command-line interpreter** that provides a user interface to the services of the operating system kernel. It reads commands typed by the user (or read from a file) and executes them, translating them into system calls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bash (Bourne Again SHell)**: A free, open-source, GNU-project replacement for the original Bourne Shell (`sh`). It is the **default login shell** on most Linux distributions and macOS (until Catalina) and combines features from the Korn Shell (`ksh`) and C Shell (`csh`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Shell Script**: A plain-text file containing a sequence of shell commands, control structures, variable assignments, and function definitions that the shell interpreter can execute **non-interactively**, line-by-line (interpreted, not compiled).

A shell script is recognized by the operating system through:
- The file extension convention `.sh` (advisory only).
- The **shebang line** at the very top: `#!/bin/bash` (mandatory and decisive).
- The **execute permission bit** set on the file: `-rwxr-xr-x`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a shell script as a **recipe card for a chef (the kernel)**:

- The **chef (kernel)** can only understand short, specific kitchen instructions (system calls).
- The **waiter (shell)** takes your order (command) and translates it into precise kitchen language.
- A **recipe card (shell script)** is a pre-written list of kitchen instructions the waiter reads sequentially, so you don't have to repeat yourself for every dish.
- The **shebang** is the language label on top of the recipe card (e.g., "French Cuisine") — it tells the waiter *which* dialect to read it in.

If you tell the waiter, "Open the fridge, take 2 eggs, whisk them, heat a pan, pour, cook 3 minutes," the chef executes it. A script is exactly that — a stored list of OS instructions executed in order.

## 1.3 Why Bash for KTU / Industry

| Property | Value (Standard / Default) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Default Shell on Linux | **Bash 5.x** |
| Path of Interpreter | **/bin/bash** |
| Script File Extension | **.sh** (advisory) |
| Execution Type | **Interpreted** (line-by-line) |
| Performance | Lightweight (~**1.5 MB** footprint) |
| Portability | **POSIX-compliant** subset available |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3)**: Students are expected to write, debug, and execute Bash scripts using variables, operators, control flow, loops, functions, and basic file/string handling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Bash Script

Every executable Bash script is structured in **four logical layers**:

1. **Shebang Declaration** (`#!/bin/bash`) — tells the kernel which interpreter to invoke.
2. **Comments / Documentation** (`#` single-line) — ignored by the interpreter; for human readability.
3. **Declarations** — variable assignments, environment exports, function definitions.
4. **Executable Statements** — commands, control structures, I/O operations.

## 2.2 Operational Walkthrough — How a Script Executes

When you run `./script.sh`, the kernel performs the following sequence:

- **Step 1 — Kernel reads the shebang** line, identifies the interpreter (`/bin/bash`).
- **Step 2 — Kernel `fork()` + `exec()`**: spawns a child process that replaces itself with the Bash interpreter.
- **Step 3 — Bash reads the script file** into a memory buffer, tokenizes each line.
- **Step 4 — Bash performs expansions** in strict order: brace → tilde → parameter → variable → command → arithmetic → word-splitting → globbing → quote removal.
- **Step 5 — Each command is dispatched** to the kernel via `execve()` system call.
- **Step 6 — Exit status** (`$?`) of the last command is returned to the parent shell.

## 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Variables, Operators, and Special Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop reference for the KTU 2024 exam hall.** Memorize the symbols, not the explanations.

### 2.3.1 Variable Types

| Type | Syntax | Scope | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| User-defined (local) | `name=value` | Current shell only | `count=10` |
| Environment (exported) | `export name=value` | Child processes inherit | `export PATH=$PATH:/opt` |
| Read-only (constant) | `readonly name=value` | Immutable post-set | `readonly PI=3.14` |
| Positional Parameters | `$1, $2, ...` | Script arguments | `$0` = script name |
| Special Parameters | `$#, $@, $*, $?, $$` | Built-in | `$#` = argument count |

### 2.3.2 Arithmetic Operators (inside `$(( ))`)

| Operator | Meaning | Example (Bash) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `+` `-` `*` `/` `%` | Basic math | `echo $((10 % 3))` → **1** |
| `**` | Exponentiation | `echo $((2**8))` → **256** |
| `++` `--` | Pre/Post increment | `((x++))` |
| `+=` `-=` `*=` `/=` | Compound assign | `((x+=5))` |

### 2.3.3 Comparison & Test Operators

| Test Type | Numeric | String | File |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equal | `-eq` | `==` | `-e` (exists) |
| Not Equal | `-ne` | `!=` | `-f` (regular file) |
| Greater Than | `-gt` | `>` (lex) | `-d` (directory) |
| Less Than | `-lt` | `<` (lex) | `-r` (readable) |
| Greater or Eq | `-ge` | — | `-w` (writable) |
| Less or Eq | `-le` | — | `-x` (executable) |

> **Important**: `<` and `>` inside `[ ... ]` are **lexicographic** (string) comparisons. For numeric, you must use `-lt` / `-gt` inside `[ ... ]` or use the modern `(( ... ))` arithmetic context.

### 2.3.4 Logical & String Operators

| Operator | Meaning | Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `&&` | Logical AND | `cmd1 && cmd2` |
| `$\vert\vert$` | Logical OR | `cmd1 $\vert\vert$ cmd2` |
| `!` | Logical NOT | `! cmd` |
| `${var:-default}` | Default if unset | `echo ${name:-"Guest"}` |
| `${var:=default}` | Assign if unset | `${name:="Guest"}` |
| `${#var}` | String length | `echo ${#name}` |
| `${var:pos:len}` | Substring | `${name:0:3}` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DevOps / CI-CD**: Jenkins pipelines, GitHub Actions, Docker entry-points — all run on Bash.
- **System Administration**: User provisioning, log rotation, cron automation.
- **Embedded / IoT**: BusyBox on routers uses a POSIX shell subset.
- **Data Engineering**: ETL pre-processing before Python/R pipelines.
- **Cybersecurity**: Penetration-testing scripts, reverse shells, log analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Script Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note**: Every script below is a *complete, runnable* example. Read the **Code block**, then the **Trace block** that explains *what the kernel/Shell does at each line*. This mirrors the KTU examiner's expected step-by-step valuation style.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Variables, Input, and Arithmetic

### 3.1.1 The Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# File: greet.sh
# Description: Reads a user's name and age, prints a personalized greeting and birth year.

echo "Enter your name: "
read name
echo "Enter your age: "
read age

current_year=2024
birth_year=$((current_year - age))

echo "Hello, $name! You were born in approximately $birth_year."
echo "Number of characters in your name: ${#name}"
```

### 3.1.2 Execution Trace (Input: `Anu`, `20`)

| Line | Action Performed | Result / State |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Kernel reads shebang → invokes `/bin/bash` | Bash process started |
| 2 | `#` line → ignored by interpreter | None |
| 4 | `echo` is a Bash builtin → writes to **stdout** | Prints `Enter your name:` |
| 5 | `read name` blocks; reads one line from stdin → stores in `name` | `name="Anu"` |
| 6 | `echo` → prints prompt | `Enter your age:` |
| 7 | `read age` → stores in `age` | `age="20"` |
| 9 | Plain assignment (no spaces around `=`) | `current_year=2024` |
| 10 | Arithmetic expansion `$(( ... ))` evaluates `2024 - 20` | `birth_year=2004` |
| 12 | Parameter expansion `$name` substitutes the value | Prints `Hello, Anu! ...` |
| 13 | `${#name}` returns length of the string `Anu` | Prints `3` |

> [!TIP]
> **Why no spaces around `=`?** In Bash, `name = Anu` is interpreted as the **command** `name` with arguments `=` and `Anu`. Spaces break variable assignment — this is the **#1 beginner mistake** flagged by KTU examiners.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Conditional Control Flow

### 3.2.1 The Script (`grade.sh`)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Determines a grade based on numeric score.

echo "Enter your score (0-100): "
read score

if [ "$score" -ge 90 ] && [ "$score" -le 100 ]; then
    grade="A"
elif [ "$score" -ge 75 ]; then
    grade="B"
elif [ "$score" -ge 60 ]; then
    grade="C"
else
    grade="Fail"
fi

echo "Score: $score → Grade: $grade"
```

### 3.2.2 Logical Trace (Input: `82`)

- **Line 4** — `read score` → `score="82"`.
- **Line 6** — `[ "$score" -ge 90 ]`: inside `[ ]` (alias for `test`), `-ge` performs **integer comparison** → evaluates to **FALSE** (82 ≥ 90 is false).
- **Line 7** — `&&` short-circuits; second test skipped.
- **Line 8** — `elif [ "$score" -ge 75 ]` → 82 ≥ 75 → **TRUE**.
- **Line 9** — assignment `grade="B"`.
- **Line 16** — exits the `if` block, prints `Score: 82 → Grade: B`.

### 3.2.3 Alternative — `case` Statement (better for discrete values)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
echo "Enter a day number (1-7): "
read day

case $day in
    1) echo "Monday" ;;
    2) echo "Tuesday" ;;
    3) echo "Wednesday" ;;
    4) echo "Thursday" ;;
    5) echo "Friday" ;;
    6|7) echo "Weekend!" ;;
    *) echo "Invalid day" ;;
esac
```

- The `*)` is the **default** branch (catch-all).
- `6|7)` is an **OR-pattern** — matches either `6` or `7`.
- Each block must terminate with `;;` (double-semicolon terminator).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Looping Constructs

### 3.3.1 `for` Loop — Iterating a List

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Print the first 5 natural numbers.
echo "Numbers from 1 to 5:"
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
    echo "Number: $i"
done
```

**Trace**: Bash assigns each whitespace-separated token to `i` sequentially → 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. After the last, the `do ... done` block exits.

### 3.3.2 C-Style `for` Loop — Numeric Range

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Sum of numbers from 1 to N.
read -p "Enter N: " n
sum=0
for (( i=1; i<=n; i++ )); do
    sum=$((sum + i))
done
echo "Sum of 1 to $n = $sum"
```

- **Formula** used internally: the **arithmetic series** sum

$$S_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

For `n = 5`, the loop computes $0+1+2+3+4+5 = 15$, matching the closed-form $\frac{5 \times 6}{2} = 15$.

### 3.3.3 `while` Loop — Condition-Driven

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Countdown timer.
count=5
while [ $count -gt 0 ]; do
    echo "T-minus $count"
    count=$((count - 1))
    sleep 1
done
echo "Liftoff!"
```

- Loop guard: `$count -gt 0` is tested **before** each iteration.
- `sleep 1` pauses the script for **1 second** (uses the `sleep` external command).

### 3.3.4 `until` Loop — Inverse of `while`

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Wait for a file to appear (max 10 attempts).
attempt=0
until [ -f "/tmp/ready.flag" ] || [ $attempt -ge 10 ]; do
    sleep 1
    attempt=$((attempt + 1))
done
[ -f "/tmp/ready.flag" ] && echo "Ready!" || echo "Timeout"
```

- `until` runs its body **while the condition is FALSE** (opposite of `while`).

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Functions

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# Function library: arithmetic helpers.

add() {
    local result=$(( $1 + $2 ))
    echo $result          # functions "return" via stdout
}

factorial() {
    local n=$1
    if [ $n -le 1 ]; then
        echo 1
    else
        local prev=$(factorial $((n - 1)))
        echo $((n * prev))
    fi
}

# --- main ---
x=$(add 10 25)
echo "10 + 25 = $x"
echo "5! = $(factorial 5)"
```

**Trace for `factorial 5`**:

$$5! = 5 \times 4! = 5 \times 24 = 120$$

- The recursion unwinds: `5 → 4 → 3 → 2 → 1 → 1`, then bubbles back up via `echo` of multiplied partials.
- `local` confines the variable to the function scope — **critical** for avoiding global pollution.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Arrays and String Manipulation

```bash
#!/bin/bash
fruits=("Apple" "Banana" "Cherry" "Date")

echo "Total fruits: ${#fruits[@]}"        # 4
echo "First fruit: ${fruits[0]}"          # Apple
echo "Last fruit:  ${fruits[-1]}"         # Date (Bash 4.3+)
echo "All fruits:  ${fruits[@]}"

# Loop over array
for f in "${fruits[@]}"; do
    echo " - $f"
done

# Append to array
fruits+=("Elderberry")
echo "After append: ${fruits[@]}"
```

**String operations**:

```bash
str="Hello, World!"
echo "Length: ${#str}"                # 13
echo "Substring (0-5): ${str:0:5}"    # Hello
echo "Replace: ${str/World/Bash}"     # Hello, Bash!
echo "Uppercase: ${str^^}"            # HELLO, WORLD!
```

| Expansion | Meaning | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `${#str}` | Length of `str` | **13** |
| `${str:0:5}` | Substring from index 0, length 5 | **Hello** |
| `${str/World/Bash}` | Replace first match | **Hello, Bash!** |
| `${str^^}` | Convert to uppercase | **HELLO, WORLD!** |

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — File I/O and Command-Line Arguments

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# fileinfo.sh — report details of files passed as arguments.

if [ $# -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <file1> <file2> ..."
    exit 1
fi

for file in "$@"; do
    if [ -e "$file" ]; then
        size=$(stat -c%s "$file")
        lines=$(wc -l < "$file")
        echo "File: $file | Size: ${size} bytes | Lines: $lines"
    else
        echo "Missing: $file" >&2
    fi
done
```

**Run**: `./fileinfo.sh notes.txt report.md`

| Special Param | Meaning | Example Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `$0` | Script name | `./fileinfo.sh` |
| `$1`, `$2` | Positional arguments | `notes.txt`, `report.md` |
| `$#` | Argument count | `2` |
| `$@` | All arguments (quoted-safe) | `notes.txt report.md` |
| `>&2` | Redirect to **stderr** | Error stream |

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Construct

| Goal | Recommended Construct | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Iterate over a known list of items | `for x in list; do` | Most readable |
| Iterate over a numeric range | `for ((i=0; i<n; i++))` | C-style familiarity |
| Loop until a condition is met | `while [ cond ]; do` | Condition-first semantics |
| Loop until a file/event appears | `until [ cond ]; do` | Inverse condition |
| Multi-way branching on a variable | `case $var in ... esac` | Cleaner than nested `if` |
| Encapsulate reusable logic | `function name { ... }` | Modularity |
| Store a collection of items | `array=(a b c)` | Indexed access |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lifecycle of a Bash Script Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types ./script.sh] --> B[Kernel reads shebang line]
    B --> C{Interpreter found at /bin/bash?}
    C -->|No| D[Error: bad interpreter]
    C -->|Yes| E[Kernel fork and exec Bash process]
    E --> F[Bash opens script file]
    F --> G[Read line from script]
    G --> H[Perform expansions and tokenize]
    H --> I[Execute command via execve]
    I --> J{More lines?}
    J -->|Yes| G
    J -->|No| K[Return exit status to parent shell]
    K --> L[Shell prompt returns]

    style A fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#4682b4,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#cc5500,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#b22222,stroke:#660000,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a4d2e,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a4d2e,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#cc5500,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#4682b4,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Bash Script Internal Architecture — Block-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph src[Script File]
        s1[Shebang Line] --> s2[Comments and Documentation]
        s2 --> s3[Variable Declarations]
        s3 --> s4[Function Definitions]
        s4 --> s5[Main Script Body]
    end

    subgraph exp[Expansion Engine]
        e1[Brace Expansion] --> e2[Parameter Expansion]
        e2 --> e3[Command Substitution]
        e3 --> e4[Arithmetic Expansion]
        e4 --> e5[Word Splitting]
        e5 --> e6[Globbing]
    end

    subgraph exec[Execution Layer]
        x1[Bash Builtins] --> x2[External Commands]
        x2 --> x3[System Calls]
    end

    s5 --> exp
    exp --> exec
    exec --> out[Output to stdout or stderr]

    style src fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style exp fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600
    style exec fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#1a4d2e
    style out fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#4b0082,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Decision-Making Flow — Choosing the Right Control Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to branch on a value?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, discrete options| C1[Use case statement]
    Q1 -->|Yes, range or compound| C2[Use if-elif-else]
    Q1 -->|No, linear sequence| C3[Run commands sequentially]

    Q2{Need to repeat?}
    C3 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes, over a list| L1[Use for-in loop]
    Q2 -->|Yes, fixed numeric range| L2[Use C-style for loop]
    Q2 -->|Yes, while condition true| L3[Use while loop]
    Q2 -->|Yes, until condition true| L4[Use until loop]
    Q2 -->|No| END[Linear Execution Complete]

    style Q1 fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#cc5500,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#cc5500,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a4d2e,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a4d2e,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#1a4d2e,color:#ffffff
    style L1 fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style L2 fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style L3 fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style L4 fill:#6a5acd,stroke:#2f2f6f,color:#ffffff
    style END fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is a shell script? Explain the purpose of the shebang line in a Bash script with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **shell script** is a text file containing a sequence of shell commands that are executed by the shell interpreter in a non-interactive, line-by-line manner. It automates repetitive tasks and supports variables, control flow, and functions.

The **shebang line** is the very first line of the script, beginning with `#!` followed by the absolute path of the interpreter. It tells the kernel which interpreter to invoke when the script is executed directly.

**Example**: `#!/bin/bash` instructs the kernel to load `/bin/bash` as the interpreter. Without the shebang, the kernel would default to the current login shell, which may not support Bash-specific syntax.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often write the shebang as `#!/bin/sh` and then use Bash-only features (e.g., `[[ ]]`, arrays). This produces a runtime syntax error. Always match the shebang to the script's actual syntax.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between `$@` and `$*` in Bash. How does quoting them affect word-splitting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

Both `$@` and `$*` represent **all positional parameters** passed to a script, but they behave differently when quoted:

| Form | Unquoted | Quoted (`"$@"`) | Quoted (`"$*"`) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Result | Words are split on whitespace | Each argument preserved as a separate word | All arguments joined into one string separated by `IFS` (default space) |
| Use Case | Rarely useful | **Recommended** for safe iteration | Useful for display only |

**Example**: Given `./script.sh "hello world" foo bar`:
- `"$@"` expands to 3 distinct words: `"hello world"`, `"foo"`, `"bar"`.
- `"$*"` expands to 1 string: `"hello world foo bar"`.

**Rule of thumb**: Always use `"$@"` when iterating with `for` to preserve arguments containing spaces.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) Explain the different types of variables in Bash with examples. Write a script demonstrating user input, environment variables, and read-only variables. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

In Bash, variables are classified as:

1. **Local (user-defined) variables** — Declared without `export`, scoped to the current shell. Example: `name="Kerala"`.
2. **Environment variables** — Declared with `export`, inherited by child processes. Example: `export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm`.
3. **Read-only (constant) variables** — Declared with `readonly`; cannot be modified post-assignment. Example: `readonly PI=3.14`.
4. **Special variables** — Built-in positional and status variables: `$0`, `$1..$9`, `$#`, `$?`, `$$`, `$!`.
5. **Command substitution variables** — Capture command output: `today=$(date)`.

**Demonstration Script**:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# variable_demo.sh
readonly PI=3.14159

echo "Enter your roll number: "
read roll_no

name="KTU_Student"
export GREETING="Hello"

echo "$GREETING, $name!"
echo "Roll No: $roll_no"
echo "Value of PI: $PI"
echo "Script PID: $$"
echo "Total args passed: $#"
```

**Sample run**: `./variable_demo.sh arg1 arg2`
**Expected output**:
```
Enter your roll number: 45
Hello, KTU_Student!
Roll No: 45
Value of PI: 3.14159
Script PID: 4821
Total args passed: 2
```

**Trace summary**: `read` captures `roll_no=45`; `readonly PI=3.14159` cannot be reassigned; `$$` returns the PID of the current Bash process; `$#` counts the number of CLI arguments (2).

---

**Q: (b) Explain the conditional and looping constructs in Bash. Write a script that uses `if-elif-else`, `case`, `for`, and `while` to validate a user-entered number. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

Bash provides four primary control structures:

- **`if-elif-else`** — for range/compound condition testing.
- **`case`** — for multi-way discrete-value branching.
- **`for`** — for iteration over a list or numeric range.
- **`while`** — for condition-driven looping.

**Script**:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# validate.sh
read -p "Enter a number (1-10): " num

# Validation with if-elif-else
if ! [[ "$num" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then
    echo "Error: Not a number"
    exit 1
elif [ "$num" -lt 1 ] || [ "$num" -gt 10 ]; then
    echo "Error: Out of range"
    exit 1
fi

# Day-of-week mapping with case
read -p "Enter day code (1-7): " day
case $day in
    1) day_name="Monday" ;;
    2) day_name="Tuesday" ;;
    3) day_name="Wednesday" ;;
    4) day_name="Thursday" ;;
    5) day_name="Friday" ;;
    6|7) day_name="Weekend" ;;
    *) day_name="Invalid" ;;
esac
echo "Day: $day_name"

# Multiplication table with for
echo "Multiplication table of $num:"
for (( i=1; i<=10; i++ )); do
    echo "$num x $i = $((num * i))"
done

# Countdown with while
echo "Countdown:"
count=3
while [ $count -gt 0 ]; do
    echo "$count..."
    count=$((count - 1))
done
echo "Done!"
```

**Trace for input `num=5, day=6`**: regex `^[0-9]+$` passes; range check passes; `case` matches `6|7)` → "Weekend"; `for` prints `5 x 1 = 5` ... `5 x 10 = 50`; `while` prints `3...`, `2...`, `1...`, `Done!`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Lost Marks Common Here**:
> - **No spaces inside `[ ]`**: `[ "$x" -gt 5 ]` is correct; `[ "$x" -gt 5]` (missing space before `]`) throws a syntax error.
> - **Unquoted variables**: `[ $num -gt 5 ]` fails if `$num` is empty or contains spaces. **Always quote**: `[ "$num" -gt 5 ]`.
> - **Forgetting `;;` after each `case` branch** causes a fall-through parse error.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Write a Bash script to demonstrate array operations, string manipulations, and arithmetic computations. Explain each operation with a sample output. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# operations.sh

# --- Arrays ---
marks=(85 92 78 95 88)
echo "Total subjects: ${#marks[@]}"            # 5
echo "Highest mark: ${marks[3]}"               # 95
marks+=("91")                                  # Append
echo "After append: ${marks[@]}"

# Compute average
sum=0
for m in "${marks[@]}"; do
    sum=$((sum + m))
done
avg=$((sum / ${#marks[@]}))
echo "Average: $avg"

# --- Strings ---
greeting="Hello, KTU Student"
echo "Length: ${#greeting}"                    # 18
echo "Substring 0-5: ${greeting:0:5}"          # Hello
echo "Replace: ${greeting/KTU/APJ}"            # Hello, APJ Student
echo "Uppercase: ${greeting^^}"                # HELLO, KTU STUDENT
echo "Lowercase: ${greeting,,}"                # hello, ktu student

# --- Arithmetic ---
a=15
b=4
echo "Sum: $((a + b))"                         # 19
echo "Difference: $((a - b))"                  # 11
echo "Product: $((a * b))"                     # 60
echo "Quotient: $((a / b))"                    # 3 (integer division)
echo "Remainder: $((a % b))"                   # 3
echo "Power: $((a ** 2))"                      # 225
```

**Explanation summary**:
- `${#marks[@]}` returns the **length of the array** (number of elements).
- `marks+=("91")` is the **append operator** for arrays.
- `${greeting^^}` uses **parameter expansion case modification** introduced in Bash 4.
- `$((a / b))` performs **integer division** — Bash has no native floating-point arithmetic; use `bc` or `awk` for decimals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Lost-Mark Alert**: KTU evaluators specifically check that you use `${#marks[@]}` (with `@`) for array length, not `${#marks}` (which would return the length of the first element, **85** = 2 characters).

---

**Q: (b) Explain user-defined functions in Bash. Write a script that demonstrates parameter passing, return values, and recursion. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

A **function** in Bash is a named block of reusable code defined with either the `function name { ... }` or the `name() { ... }` syntax. Functions are executed when called by name.

**Key points**:
- Functions are invoked by simply typing their name (no parentheses needed).
- Parameters are accessed via `$1, $2, ...` (positional) — **not** via function arguments in the call.
- Functions return values either via the **exit status** (`return N`, where N is 0–255) or by **echoing output to stdout** and capturing with `$(...)`.
- Recursion is supported; a base case is essential to terminate.

**Script**:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# function_demo.sh

# 1. Parameter passing and stdout return
greet() {
    local name=$1
    local time=$2
    echo "Good $time, $name!"
}

# 2. Exit-status return (0 = success, 1-255 = error code)
is_even() {
    if [ $(( $1 % 2 )) -eq 0 ]; then
        return 0   # true
    else
        return 1   # false
    fi
}

# 3. Recursion
factorial() {
    local n=$1
    if [ "$n" -le 1 ]; then
        echo 1
    else
        local sub=$(factorial $((n - 1)))
        echo $((n * sub))
    fi
}

# --- main ---
msg=$(greet "Anu" "morning")
echo "$msg"

read -p "Enter a number: " num
if is_even "$num"; then
    echo "$num is even"
else
    echo "$num is odd"
fi

echo "Factorial of $num = $(factorial $num)"
```

**Trace for `num=5`**:
- `greet "Anu" "morning"` → echoes `Good morning, Anu!` → captured into `msg`.
- `is_even 5` → `5 % 2 = 1` → returns 1 → "odd" branch executed.
- `factorial 5` recursion:
  - `factorial 5` → `sub = factorial 4` → `sub = factorial 3` → `sub = factorial 2` → `sub = factorial 1` → echoes `1`.
  - Bubble up: `1*2=2`, `2*3=6`, `6*4=24`, `24*5=120` → final echo `120`.

**Closed-form check**: $5! = 120$ ✓ matches the loop product $1 \times 2 \times 3 \times 4 \times 5$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**:
> - **Defining a function with `function name() { }`** (mixing both syntaxes) is valid but stylistically inconsistent — pick one.
> - **Forgetting to capture function stdout** with `$(...)` is a frequent 2-mark deduction. `greet "Anu"` *prints* but does not *assign* — you need `msg=$(greet "Anu")`.
> - **Bash has no `return` of strings or floats** — only integer exit codes (0–255). For "returning" data, always `echo` and capture.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Shebang**: `#!/bin/bash` — first line, no exceptions.
- **No spaces** around `=` in assignments: `x=10` ✓ ; `x = 10` ✗.
- **Variables**:
  - Local: `name="value"`.
  - Export: `export NAME="value"` (visible to child processes).
  - Read-only: `readonly X=99` (immutable).
  - Special: `$0`, `$1..$9`, `$#`, `$@`, `$*`, `$?`, `$$`, `$!`.
- **Quotes**:
  - `'...'` → literal (no expansion).
  - `"..."` → allows `$var` and `${...}` expansion.
  - `\` → escape single character.
- **Arithmetic**: use `$(( expr ))` or `(( expr ))` (no `$` for assignment).
- **Tests**:
  - `[ ... ]` (POSIX) — needs spaces inside; uses `-eq, -gt, -lt` for numbers.
  - `[[ ... ]]` (Bash) — allows `==, !=, <, >`; supports regex `=~`.
- **Control flow**:
  - `if ... then ... elif ... then ... else ... fi`.
  - `case $var in pattern) ... ;; *) ... ;; esac`.
  - `for x in list; do ... done` OR `for ((i=0;i<n;i++)); do ... done`.
  - `while [ cond ]; do ... done`.
  - `until [ cond ]; do ... done`.
- **Functions**:
  - `name() { ... }` — call with `name arg1 arg2`.
  - Parameters: `$1, $2`.
  - Return: `return N` (exit status) OR `echo value` + `$(...)` capture.
  - Use `local` to avoid polluting global scope.
- **Arrays**:
  - Declare: `arr=(a b c)`.
  - Access: `${arr[0]}`, `${arr[@]}` (all), `${#arr[@]}` (length).
  - Append: `arr+=("d")`.
- **Strings**:
  - Length: `${#str}`.
  - Substring: `${str:pos:len}`.
  - Replace: `${str/old/new}` (first), `${str//old/new}` (all).
  - Case: `${str^^}` (upper), `${str,,}` (lower).
- **File tests**: `-e` (exists), `-f` (file), `-d` (dir), `-r/-w/-x` (perms).
- **I/O redirection**:
  - `> file` (overwrite stdout), `>> file` (append).
  - `2> file` (stderr), `&> file` (both).
  - `< file` (stdin from file).
  - `cmd1 $\vert$ cmd2` (pipe), `cmd1 && cmd2` (AND), `cmd1 $\vert\vert$ cmd2` (OR).
- **Exit codes**: 0 = success, non-zero = error. `$?` reads the last exit code.
- **Best practices**:
  - Always quote variables: `"$var"`.
  - Use `#!/bin/bash` shebang for Bash-specific features.
  - Use `set -e` at the top to abort on any error.
  - Use `set -u` to catch unset variables.
  - Indent consistently (4 spaces or 1 tab).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Communications - LAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Computer Communications - LAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Computer Communications & LAN: Core Technical Definition

## 1.1 Computer Communications — The Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Computer Communication* refers to the process of transmitting, receiving, and exchanging data, instructions, and information between two or more computing devices or systems over a transmission medium using a set of well-defined protocols.

In the simplest academic terms, computer communication is the **engineered discipline** that allows independent computing nodes to share resources, exchange data, and coordinate operations. It is the bedrock on which modern distributed systems, the internet, and enterprise networks are built.

### 1.1.1 The Five Indispensable Components

Every communication system (in the KTU 2024 Scheme framework) must contain the following five elements, often remembered through the acronym **MASS D**:

| Component | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **M**essage | The actual data being transmitted | An email, a file, a video frame |
| **S**ender | The originating device | Your laptop, a server |
| **R**eceiver | The destination device | A web server, a printer |
| **T**ransmission Medium | The physical/logical path | Copper cable, fiber, radio waves |
| **P**rotocol | The rule-set governing exchange | TCP/IP, HTTP, Ethernet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes that *without protocols, communication is impossible*. A protocol is not hardware — it is a **standardized agreement** (a language) shared between sender and receiver.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Postal System

> [!TIP]
> **Think of computer communication as a postal system.** The *message* is your letter. The *sender* is you. The *receiver* is your friend. The *transmission medium* is the postal truck, the road, and the air. The *protocol* is the **rule that says you must write the address on the envelope, use a stamp, and follow the country's postal code format.** Without the protocol (envelope rules), the letter will never reach the right person — even if the truck drives past the house!

---

## 1.2 Network Classification: From PAN to WAN

Computer networks are broadly classified by their **geographic scope**. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know this hierarchy cold:

| Network Type | Full Form | Approximate Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | < 10 m | Bluetooth, phone-laptop sync |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | **10 m – 10 km** | Office, campus, building |
| **CAN** | Campus Area Network | Multiple buildings (1–5 km) | University campus, corporate park |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City-wide (5–50 km) | Cable TV network, city WiFi |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Country / Global | The Internet, bank networks |

---

## 1.3 Local Area Network (LAN) — The Heart of This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IEEE 802 Standard / KTU 2024):**
> A *Local Area Network (LAN)* is a privately owned, high-speed communication network that interconnects computers, peripherals, and other devices within a **limited geographical area** (such as a single building, office, floor, or campus) under a single administrative control.

### 1.3.1 Defining Characteristics of a LAN

A network is classified as a LAN **only** if it satisfies the following characteristics — these are **high-yield** for the KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions:

1. **Limited Geographic Scope** — confined to a room, building, or campus (max ~10 km radius).
2. **High Data Transfer Rate** — typically **100 Mbps to 10 Gbps** in modern deployments.
3. **Low Latency & Low Error Rate** — modern LANs achieve bit error rates (BER) of the order of **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹¹**.
4. **Private Ownership & Single Administration** — owned, operated, and maintained by a single organization.
5. **Shared/Common Transmission Medium** — all devices share the underlying physical/logical channel.
6. **Homogeneous Technology** — uses consistent hardware, protocols, and topology across the network.

### 1.3.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Office Telephone System

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a single-floor office.** All 50 employees sit within shouting distance. They are all connected to the *same internal telephone exchange* (PBX), and any one of them can call any other by just dialing a 3-digit extension. They share the **same wiring**, the **same exchange**, and the **same set of calling rules**. The **Internet** (WAN) is the global telecom grid — but the **LAN** is your office's private, fast, in-house phone system.

---

## 1.4 Why LANs Matter — The Engineering Rationale

LANs were invented to solve three core engineering problems:

1. **Resource Sharing** — Printers, scanners, NAS storage, and internet gateways are expensive. A LAN lets 100 users share **one** resource instead of buying 100.
2. **Data Centralization** — File servers and database servers allow consistent, backed-up, secure data management from a single point.
3. **Communication & Collaboration** — Email, video conferencing, instant messaging, and shared applications all ride on the LAN infrastructure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LAN Topologies — The Architectural Backbone

The term **topology** in a LAN refers to the **logical or physical arrangement** of nodes and connections. The KTU 2024 Scheme requires fluency in **five primary topologies**.

### 2.1.1 Bus Topology (Linear Bus)

* **Structure:** All devices are connected to a single shared backbone cable (the "bus" or "trunk").
* **Termination:** The cable is terminated by **50 Ω resistors** (terminators) at both ends to absorb signals and prevent reflection.
* **Access Method:** All devices share the medium → typically uses **CSMA/CD** to avoid collisions.
* **Failure Mode:** A single break in the main cable **collapses the entire LAN** (no termination = signal reflection = total failure).

### 2.1.2 Star Topology

* **Structure:** All devices connect to a **central hub or switch**.
* **Modern Dominance:** The de-facto standard for modern Ethernet LANs.
* **Failure Mode:** One cable break isolates **only that one device**. The central device is a single point of failure (but easy to replace).
* **Advantage over Bus:** Easier to troubleshoot, add/remove devices, and isolate faults.

### 2.1.3 Ring Topology

* **Structure:** Each device connects to **exactly two other devices**, forming a closed circular chain.
* **Data Flow:** A *token* (a small control frame) circulates; only the device holding the token may transmit.
* **Logical vs. Physical:** Modern Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) is often a *physical star* wired to a *logical ring* using a device called a **MAU (Multistation Access Unit)**.
* **Failure Mode:** A single node or cable failure breaks the entire ring unless it uses a **dual ring (FDDI)** for redundancy.

### 2.1.4 Mesh Topology

* **Structure:** Every device connects to **every other device** directly.
* **Variants:**
  * **Full Mesh** — n devices have n(n-1)/2 links. Maximum redundancy, very expensive.
  * **Partial Mesh** — only critical nodes are fully meshed; others connect via a star.
* **Use Case:** Backbone of ISPs, military networks, data-center fabrics.

### 2.1.5 Tree (Hierarchical) Topology

* **Structure:** A **root node** at the top, branching downward in a parent-child hierarchy.
* **Use Case:** Large corporate networks, campus networks, structured cabling.

### 2.1.6 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield Table)

| Topology | Cable Cost | Failure Tolerance | Scalability | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bus** | Low | Very Poor | Poor | Legacy 10BASE2 Ethernet |
| **Star** | Moderate | Good (one cable fails) | Excellent | Modern Office LANs |
| **Ring** | Moderate | Poor (single point) | Moderate | Legacy Token Ring, FDDI |
| **Mesh** | Very High | Excellent | Moderate | ISP Backbones, Data Centers |
| **Tree** | Moderate | Moderate | Excellent | Campus Networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** "Hybrid Topology" (a mix of two or more topologies) is **not** a separate topology — it is a **design choice** combining the strengths of multiple topologies.

---

## 2.2 Transmission Media — The Physical Highway

| Medium | Type | Bandwidth | Range | EMI Susceptibility | Security |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Twisted Pair (UTP/STP)** | Guided (Wired) | Up to 10 Gbps (Cat 6a/7) | 100 m | High (UTP), Low (STP) | High |
| **Coaxial Cable** | Guided (Wired) | Up to 10 Gbps | 500 m | Moderate | High |
| **Fiber Optic (SMF/MMF)** | Guided (Wired) | Up to 100+ Gbps | 40+ km (SMF) | **Zero** (immune) | Very High |
| **Radio Waves (WiFi)** | Unguided (Wireless) | Up to 9.6 Gbps (WiFi 6) | ~100 m indoor | High | Low |
| **Microwave / Satellite** | Unguided (Wireless) | Up to Gbps | Continental | Atmospheric | Low |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Favourite):** Choose *fiber* for **backbone and long-distance**. Choose *UTP (Cat 5e/6)* for **end-user desk connections** (best cost-to-performance ratio). Choose *WiFi* for **mobility**.

---

## 2.3 LAN Access Methods (MAC Protocols)

The *Medium Access Control (MAC)* sublayer governs **who can talk when** on a shared medium. The KTU syllabus focuses on three:

### 2.3.1 CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)
* **Used in:** IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet) — half-duplex mode.
* **Logic:**
  1. Listen to the medium (Carrier Sense).
  2. If idle, start transmitting.
  3. If two devices transmit simultaneously → **Collision**.
  4. Both stop, wait a **random backoff time** (Binary Exponential Backoff), and retry.
* **Limitation:** Cannot be used in wireless because of the *hidden node problem* (your transmission cannot be heard by a station out of range).

### 2.3.2 CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)
* **Used in:** IEEE 802.11 (WiFi).
* **Logic:** Avoids collisions **before** they happen using **RTS/CTS (Request to Send / Clear to Send)** handshake.
* **Trade-off:** More overhead, lower throughput than CSMA/CD, but essential for wireless.

### 2.3.3 Token Passing
* **Used in:** IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring), IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus), FDDI.
* **Logic:** A special 3-byte control frame called the **Token** circulates. Only the device holding the token can transmit. This guarantees **no collisions** and provides **deterministic latency**.

---

## 2.4 IEEE 802 LAN Standards Family (High-Yield)

| Standard | Description | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| **IEEE 802.1** | Bridging, VLANs, Network Management | — |
| **IEEE 802.2** | Logical Link Control (LLC) | LLC |
| **IEEE 802.3** | CSMA/CD Ethernet | Ethernet |
| **IEEE 802.4** | Token Passing Bus | Token Bus |
| **IEEE 802.5** | Token Passing Ring | Token Ring |
| **IEEE 802.11** | Wireless LAN (CSMA/CA) | WiFi |
| **IEEE 802.15** | Wireless PAN | Bluetooth, ZigBee |
| **IEEE 802.16** | Broadband Wireless MAN | WiMAX |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

These equations are the **core quantitative tools** for LAN problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{t} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Transmission time | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{p} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{total} = T_{t} + T_{p}$ | End-to-end delay | seconds |
| 4 | $BDP = R \times T_{p}$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product | bits |
| 5 | $C = 2B \log_{2} M$ | Nyquist Bit Rate (noiseless) | bps |
| 6 | $C = B \log_{2} \left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon Capacity (noisy) | bps |
| 7 | $N_{links} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Full Mesh Links for n nodes | integer |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{L}{L + H} \times 100\%$ | Protocol Efficiency | % |

Where:
* $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps).
* $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = propagation speed (~2 × 10⁸ m/s in copper, ~2 × 10⁸ m/s in fiber, ~3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum).
* $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $M$ = signal levels, $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio.
* $H$ = overhead bits (headers, preambles, inter-frame gaps).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often forget the **inter-frame gap (IFG)** and **preamble** in Ethernet efficiency calculations. A real IEEE 802.3 frame on the wire is **not** equal to the *data* — the overhead (preamble 7B + SFD 1B + IFG 12B + Header 14B + FCS 4B) is added.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is designed to drill the formulas from §2.5 into your muscle memory. Every step is **explicitly** written — no skips, no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The BDP tells us **how many bits can be "in flight"** on a link at any instant.

**Step 1 — Define the bit duration.**
The time taken to push one bit onto the link is the *bit time*:

$$\tau_{bit} = \frac{1}{R}$$

where $R$ is the link bandwidth in bits per second.

**Step 2 — Define the link's round-trip travel time.**
The time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver across a link of length $d$ at propagation speed $v$ is:

$$T_{p} = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to find bits in flight.**
If the first bit takes $T_{p}$ seconds to arrive, and bits are pushed onto the wire at a rate of $R$ bits per second, then the **number of bits already on the wire** when the first bit arrives is:

$$BDP = R \times T_{p} = R \times \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 4 — Real-world implication.**
$BDP$ bits is the **maximum number of bits** the sender can transmit before it *must* stop and wait for an acknowledgement — otherwise the pipe overflows.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** BDP is the foundation of **TCP window sizing**. The receiver's advertised window must be $\geq BDP$ for full link utilization.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Transmission Time & Total Delay

> **Problem:** A 10 Mbps Ethernet LAN segment is 200 m long. A host sends a packet of 1500 bytes to a server on the same segment. Propagation speed in copper is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Calculate (a) transmission time, (b) propagation delay, (c) total one-way delay.

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
Bandwidth $R = 10$ Mbps $= 10 \times 10^{6}$ bps.
Packet size $L = 1500$ bytes $= 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits.
Distance $d = 200$ m.
Propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute transmission time.**

$$T_{t} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

Therefore $T_{t} = 1.2$ ms.

**Step 3 — Compute propagation delay.**

$$T_{p} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

Therefore $T_{p} = 1$ μs.

**Step 4 — Compute total one-way delay.**

$$T_{total} = T_{t} + T_{p} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.201 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

Therefore $T_{total} \approx 1.201$ ms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** In LANs, $T_{t} \gg T_{p}$. This is the **opposite** of satellite/WAN links, where $T_{p} \gg T_{t}$. This insight dictates protocol design (e.g., CSMA/CD is feasible for LANs but not for satellites).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Full-Mesh Link Count

> **Problem:** A data center needs a full-mesh LAN connecting 12 servers. How many point-to-point links are required?

**Step 1 — Apply the formula.**

$$N_{links} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

where $n = 12$.

**Step 2 — Substitute and compute.**

$$N_{links} = \frac{12 \times 11}{2} = \frac{132}{2} = 66 \text{ links}$$

**Step 3 — Validate the combinatorial logic.**
A full mesh is a **complete graph** $K_n$. Each node connects to $(n-1)$ others, and each link is shared between 2 nodes, hence the division by 2.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** At $n=12$ you need 66 cables. At $n=50$ you need **1225 cables**. This is why data centers use **leaf-spine or fat-tree** architectures (partial mesh) instead of full mesh.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Shannon Capacity

> **Problem:** A twisted-pair LAN operates at 100 MHz bandwidth with an S/N ratio of 1000. Find the maximum data rate.

**Step 1 — Apply the Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$C = B \log_{2} \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**
$B = 100 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $S/N = 1000$.

$$C = 100 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(1001)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithm.**
$\log_{2}(1001) = \log_{10}(1001) / \log_{10}(2) \approx 3.0004 / 0.3010 \approx 9.967$ bits/Hz.

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$C = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 9.967 \approx 9.967 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps}$$

Therefore $C \approx 996.7$ Mbps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Real-world 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet uses sophisticated **PAM-5 / PAM-16** encoding over Cat 5e to push 1 Gbps through 100 MHz of bandwidth — Shannon's limit tells us *theoretically what is achievable*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What?

| Scenario | Recommended LAN Type | Recommended Access Method | Recommended Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10-user office, low cost | Star + Ethernet Switch | Switched (full-duplex, no MAC) | Cat 5e UTP |
| Factory floor, high EMI | Star + Industrial Switch | Switched (full-duplex) | **Fiber Optic** |
| Mobile laptops / BYOD | Star + Wireless AP | **CSMA/CA** (WiFi) | Radio (2.4/5 GHz) |
| Real-time industrial control | Token Ring / Time-Triggered Ethernet | **Token Passing / TTEthernet** | Cat 6a STP |
| Backbone of campus | Tree / Hierarchical Star | Switched (full-duplex) | **Single-Mode Fiber** |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 4 — Ethernet Frame Efficiency

> **Problem:** Calculate the efficiency of a 1500-byte Ethernet frame. Ethernet overheads: 7B preamble + 1B SFD + 14B header + 4B FCS + 12B IFG.

**Step 1 — Total frame on the wire.**
Overhead $H = 7 + 1 + 14 + 4 + 12 = 38$ bytes.
Total $T = L + H = 1500 + 38 = 1538$ bytes.

**Step 2 — Apply efficiency formula.**

$$E = \frac{L}{L+H} = \frac{1500}{1538} \approx 0.9753 = 97.53\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** Higher overhead (e.g., small VoIP packets of 64 bytes) drops efficiency dramatically to $\approx 63\%$ — this is why voice/video protocols use **jumbo frames** or **header compression** to claw back efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LAN Topologies — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeTop["LAN TOPOLOGIES COMPARISON"]
    nodeA["BUS TOPOLOGY"]
    nodeB["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
    nodeC["RING TOPOLOGY"]
    nodeD["MESH TOPOLOGY"]
    nodeE["TREE TOPOLOGY"]
    nodeF["All nodes share one backbone cable with terminators at both ends"]
    nodeG["All nodes connect to a central Hub or Switch"]
    nodeH["Each node connects to two neighbors forming a closed loop"]
    nodeI["Every node connects to every other node directly"]
    nodeJ["Hierarchical structure with root node and branches"]
    nodeTop --> nodeA
    nodeTop --> nodeB
    nodeTop --> nodeC
    nodeTop --> nodeD
    nodeTop --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

---

## 4.2 CSMA/CD Flow — Collision Detection Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Station wants to transmit"]
    stepB{"Is the medium IDLE?"}
    stepC["Wait and listen continuously"]
    stepD["Start transmitting frame"]
    stepE{"Collision Detected?"}
    stepF["Send Jam Signal and Stop"]
    stepG["Wait random Backoff Time k times 512 bit slots"]
    stepH["Retry up to 16 times"]
    stepI["Report failure to higher layer"]
    stepJ["Transmission Successful"]
    stepA --> stepB
    stepB -- NO --> stepC
    stepC --> stepB
    stepB -- YES --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE -- NO --> stepJ
    stepE -- YES --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH -- RETRY --> stepB
    stepH -- MAX REACHED --> stepI
```

---

## 4.3 OSI Model & Where LAN Protocols Live

```mermaid
graph TB
    layer7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION"]
    layer6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION"]
    layer5["LAYER 5 SESSION"]
    layer4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT"]
    layer3["LAYER 3 NETWORK"]
    layer2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK"]
    subLLC["LOGICAL LINK CONTROL LLC IEEE 802 2"]
    subMAC["MEDIUM ACCESS CONTROL MAC IEEE 802 3 802 11 802 5"]
    layer1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL"]
    layer7 --> layer6
    layer6 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> subLLC
    layer2 --> subMAC
    subLLC --> layer1
    subMAC --> layer1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The IEEE 802 family **only standardizes Layers 1 and 2** of the OSI model. Layers 3–7 are handled by protocols like IP, TCP, and HTTP.

---

## 4.4 Modern Office LAN — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    internet["INTERNET WAN UPLINK"]
    router["ROUTER LAYER 3"]
    firewall["FIREWALL SECURITY"]
    coreSwitch["CORE LAYER 3 SWITCH"]
    sw1["FLOOR 1 ACCESS SWITCH"]
    sw2["FLOOR 2 ACCESS SWITCH"]
    sw3["FLOOR 3 ACCESS SWITCH"]
    pcA["DESKTOP PCs"]
    server["FILE / DHCP SERVER"]
    ap1["WIRELESS ACCESS POINT"]
    laptop["MOBILE LAPTOPS"]
    printer["NETWORK PRINTER"]
    nas["NAS STORAGE"]
    internet --> router
    router --> firewall
    firewall --> coreSwitch
    coreSwitch --> sw1
    coreSwitch --> sw2
    coreSwitch --> sw3
    coreSwitch --> server
    sw1 --> pcA
    sw1 --> printer
    sw2 --> ap1
    ap1 --> laptop
    sw3 --> nas
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The **Core-Access-Distribution** three-tier model above is the gold standard for campus LANs. Each switch in the access layer is connected to the core by **two uplinks** (link aggregation) for redundancy.

---

## 4.5 Network Devices — Operating Layer Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    devA["REPEATER"]
    devB["HUB"]
    devC["BRIDGE"]
    devD["SWITCH"]
    devE["ROUTER"]
    devF["GATEWAY"]
    devG["L1 PHYSICAL LAYER"]
    devH["L1 PHYSICAL LAYER"]
    devI["L2 DATA LINK LAYER"]
    devJ["L2 DATA LINK LAYER"]
    devK["L3 NETWORK LAYER"]
    devL["L4 to L7 APPLICATION LAYER"]
    devA --> devG
    devB --> devH
    devC --> devI
    devD --> devJ
    devE --> devK
    devF --> devL
```
