- Basic deployment terminology used in industry
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of a Web Server

A web server's operation can be broken down into a structured sequence of **seven logical steps**, which is a frequent KTU 14-mark question pattern:

### Step 1 — Listening (Bind & Listen)
The web server process initializes, binds to its configured **TCP port** (default 80 or 443), and enters an infinite listening state, waiting for incoming client connections using the `socket()` system call.

### Step 2 — Connection Acceptance
When a client (browser) opens a TCP connection via the **three-way handshake** (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`), the server `accept()`s the connection, spawning a temporary socket for the transaction.

### Step 3 — Request Parsing
The server reads the incoming HTTP request, which typically contains:
- **Request Line** — `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1`
- **Request Headers** — `Host`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `Cookie`
- **Empty Line** — separator
- **Optional Body** — for POST/PUT requests

### Step 4 — URL Mapping (Resolution)
The server translates the requested URL path to a physical file in the **Document Root** using rules defined in its configuration (e.g., `DocumentRoot "/var/www/html"` in Apache).

### Step 5 — Content Generation
- For **static** requests, the server reads the file from disk.
- For **dynamic** requests (e.g., `.php`, `.py`, `.jsp`), the server passes the file to the appropriate interpreter/middleware and returns the generated output.

### Step 6 — Response Construction
The server builds the HTTP response:
- **Status Line** — `HTTP/1.1 200 OK`
- **Response Headers** — `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Date`, `Server`
- **Empty Line**
- **Response Body** — the actual HTML/CSS/JS/image bytes

### Step 7 — Logging & Connection Termination
The transaction is logged to **access logs** and **error logs**, and the connection is either closed (HTTP/1.0 style) or kept alive for reuse (HTTP/1.1 persistent connections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Explain how a web server works," always explicitly mention the **TCP port binding**, **HTTP methods (GET/POST)**, **status codes (200, 404, 500)**, and the **client-server paradigm**. These are the four evaluation anchors.

## 2.2 Types of Web Servers (Industry & KTU-Relevant)

### 2.2.1 Apache HTTP Server
- Released: **1995**
- License: Open-source (Apache License 2.0)
- Architecture: **Process-based** (one process/thread per connection, configurable via MPMs)
- Strength: Highly modular via `.so` modules (`mod_php`, `mod_rewrite`, `mod_ssl`)
- Configuration file: `httpd.conf` and `.htaccess`

### 2.2.2 Nginx (Engine-X)
- Released: **2004** by Igor Sysoev
- Architecture: **Event-driven, asynchronous, non-blocking** — handles thousands of concurrent connections with minimal memory
- Use case: Reverse proxy, load balancer, HTTP cache, and static content server
- Configuration file: `nginx.conf`

### 2.2.3 Microsoft IIS (Internet Information Services)
- Bundled with Windows Server editions
- Architecture: **Thread-pool based**
- Configuration: GUI-based IIS Manager plus `applicationHost.config`
- Native support for ASP.NET

### 2.2.4 Lighttpd, Caddy, Tomcat
- **Lighttpd** — Lightweight, security-focused
- **Caddy** — Automatic HTTPS by default
- **Tomcat** — Java Servlet container (specialized for Java EE applications)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions often include a **comparison table** between Apache and Nginx. Memorize the architectural difference: **Apache = process/thread-per-connection** vs **Nginx = event-driven asynchronous**.

## 2.3 Static vs Dynamic Content — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Static Content | Dynamic Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Pre-existing files on disk (HTML, CSS, images) | Generated on-the-fly by server-side scripts |
| **Response Time** | Very fast (file I/O only) | Slower (script execution + database query) |
| **Personalization** | Same for every user | Can vary per user/session |
| **Technologies** | Plain HTML, CSS, JS files | PHP, Python (Django/Flask), Node.js, JSP, ASP.NET |
| **Database Access** | None | Often involves DBMS queries |
| **Example URL** | `https://site.com/about.html` | `https://site.com/user?id=42` |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> Web Servers is a **conceptual topic**, but the following standardized parameters and formulas are tested:

| Symbol / Constant | Meaning | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{HTTP}$ | Default HTTP port | 80 | TCP port number |
| $P_{HTTPS}$ | Default HTTPS port | 443 | TCP port number |
| $N_{concurrent}$ | Max concurrent connections (Apache default) | 256 | connections |
| $T_{3WH}$ | TCP three-way handshake time | $\frac{2 \times RTT + S_{syn}}{B}$ | seconds |
| $B$ | Bandwidth of server link | Variable | bits per second (bps) |
| $R_{throughput}$ | Request throughput | $\frac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$ | requests/second |
| $L_{p95}$ | 95th percentile response latency | Variable | milliseconds (ms) |
| $U_{cpu}$ | CPU utilization under load | $0 \le U_{cpu} \le 1$ | dimensionless ratio |
| $S_{uptime}$ | Server uptime percentage | $\frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} \times 100$ | percent |

### 2.4.1 Derivation of the Throughput Equation

The fundamental relationship between **request count** and **service time** is given by Little's Law, applied to web servers:

$$R_{throughput} = \frac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$$

where $N_{req}$ is the number of completed requests and $T_{total}$ is the total observation window. Under steady-state load, the average response time $\bar{L}$ relates to concurrency $C$ by:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{C}{R_{throughput}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is the foundation of **capacity planning** for any web-facing service. AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure SLA documents report metrics using these exact derived quantities.

## 2.5 Common HTTP Status Codes (High-Yield for KTU)

| Code | Phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 200 | OK | Request succeeded |
| 301 | Moved Permanently | Resource relocated permanently |
| 302 | Found | Temporary redirect |
| 400 | Bad Request | Malformed request syntax |
| 401 | Unauthorized | Authentication required |
| 403 | Forbidden | Server refuses to authorize |
| **404** | **Not Found** | **Most frequently tested — resource missing** |
| 500 | Internal Server Error | Generic server-side failure |
| 503 | Service Unavailable | Server overloaded or under maintenance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full HTTP Transaction Trace

> **Problem:** A user types `http://www.example.com/index.html` into a browser. Trace the complete sequence of events until the page is rendered, identifying every web server interaction.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — URL Parsing**
The browser parses the URL into its components:
- **Scheme:** `http`
- **Host:** `www.example.com`
- **Path:** `/index.html`
- **Port:** 80 (default for HTTP)

**Step 2 — DNS Resolution**
The browser queries a DNS resolver to obtain the IP address:
$$IP_{resolved} = DNS\_Lookup(\text{"www.example.com"})$$
Suppose the returned IP is `93.184.216.34`.

**Step 3 — TCP Connection Establishment**
The browser opens a TCP connection to `93.184.216.34:80` using the three-way handshake:
$$SYN \rightarrow SYN\text{-}ACK \rightarrow ACK$$

**Step 4 — HTTP Request Transmission**
The browser sends the following raw HTTP request:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{GET /index.html HTTP/1.1} \\
\texttt{Host: www.example.com} \\
\texttt{User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0} \\
\texttt{Accept: text/html} \\
\texttt{Connection: keep-alive}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Server-Side Processing**
The web server (e.g., Apache) performs:
- Reads the `GET` request line.
- Maps the path `/index.html` to the file system:
$$File_{path} = DocumentRoot \oplus \text{"/index.html"}$$
$$File_{path} = \text{"/var/www/html/index.html"}$$
- Checks file existence and permissions.

**Step 6 — HTTP Response Construction**
The server transmits back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{HTTP/1.1 200 OK} \\
\texttt{Date: Mon, 15 Jan 2024 12:00:00 GMT} \\
\texttt{Server: Apache/2.4.52} \\
\texttt{Content-Type: text/html} \\
\texttt{Content-Length: 1234}
\end{aligned}
$$

Followed by the HTML body.

**Step 7 — Browser Rendering**
The browser parses the HTML, fetches subordinate resources (CSS, JS, images via parallel connections), and renders the page. The TCP connection is held open for further requests (keep-alive).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Server Throughput Calculation

> **Problem:** A web server handled 45,000 requests in a 1-hour window. Calculate the request throughput and estimate the average response time if 600 requests were being served concurrently on average.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Throughput**

$$R_{throughput} = \frac{N_{req}}{T_{total}}$$

$$R_{throughput} = \frac{45000}{3600 \text{ seconds}}$$

$$R_{throughput} = 12.5 \text{ requests/second}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Response Time using Little's Law**

Rearranging $\bar{L} = \frac{C}{R_{throughput}}$:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{600 \text{ concurrent}}{12.5 \text{ req/s}}$$

$$\bar{L} = 48 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** A 48-second response time is unrealistically high. In practice, web servers aim for $\bar{L} < 200$ ms. The numbers here are for **formula practice only** — for the KTU exam, focus on the **algebraic substitution** and **unit consistency**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Uptime Calculation

> **Problem:** A web server was operational for 720 hours in a 30-day month (720 hours total). It experienced 2 outages of 15 minutes each. Compute the uptime percentage and determine if it meets a **99.9% SLA**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Total Downtime**

$$T_{down} = 2 \times 15 \text{ min} = 30 \text{ min} = 0.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Uptime**

$$S_{uptime} = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} \times 100$$

$$S_{uptime} = \frac{719.5}{720} \times 100$$

$$S_{uptime} = 99.9305\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare to SLA**

The required SLA is $99.9\%$. Since $99.9305\% > 99.9\%$, the server **meets** the SLA.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Web Server Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | Apache | Nginx | IIS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OS Support** | Linux, Windows, macOS | Linux, Windows, BSD, macOS | Windows only |
| **Architecture** | Process/Thread per request | Event-driven async | Thread-pool |
| **Static Content** | Good | Excellent (fastest) | Good |
| **Dynamic Content** | Excellent (mod_php) | Requires FastCGI/Passenger | Excellent (ASP.NET) |
| **Configuration** | `.htaccess` supported | Centralized only | GUI-based |
| **Market Share (2024)** | ~30% | ~34% | ~12% |
| **Best For** | Shared hosting, PHP apps | High-traffic, reverse proxy | Microsoft-centric stacks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Client-Server Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["User types URL in Browser"]
    node2["Browser parses URL and extracts Host and Path"]
    node3["DNS Resolver translates domain to IP Address"]
    node4["Browser initiates TCP connection to Port 80 or 443"]
    node5["Three-Way Handshake completes"]
    node6["Browser sends HTTP GET or POST Request"]
    node7["Web Server receives and parses request"]
    node8{"Is the resource static or dynamic?"}
    node9["Reads file from Document Root on Disk"]

```

## 4.3 HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant B as Browser Client
    participant D as DNS Resolver
    participant S as Web Server
    participant F as File System
    participant DB as Database for Dynamic Content

    B->>D: Resolve www dot example dot com
    D-->>B: Return IP 93.184.216.34
    B->>S: TCP SYN on Port 80
    S-->>B: TCP SYN ACK

    S->>F: Read file DocumentRoot index dot html
    F-->>S: Return file content

    B->>S: HTTP GET slash app dot js
    S-->>B: HTTP 1.1 200 OK with JS Body

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Web Server. Differentiate between hardware and software web servers. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Web Server** is a system that stores, processes, and delivers web pages to clients over HTTP/HTTPS.

- **Hardware Web Server:** A physical computer with a fast CPU, large RAM, high-capacity storage, and a high-bandwidth network interface, dedicated to hosting websites 24/7 in a data center.
- **Software Web Server:** A program (daemon) such as Apache, Nginx, or IIS that handles HTTP requests, maps URLs to files, and returns responses.

**Example:** An Amazon EC2 instance is the *hardware*; the Apache process running on it is the *software* web server.

*(Self-Assessment: 3/3 marks for definition + distinction + example)*

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between static and dynamic web content? Provide one example URL for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Static Content:** Pre-written files served verbatim from disk; identical for every user. Example: `https://example.com/about.html`
- **Dynamic Content:** Generated at request time by server-side scripts; can vary per user. Example: `https://example.com/profile?user=rahul`

*(Self-Assessment: 3/3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Explain the working of a web server with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of HTTP protocol and describe the Apache and Nginx web servers. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Working of a Web Server (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The working of a web server follows a strict request-response cycle. The step-by-step operation is:

1. **Binding & Listening:** The server process binds to TCP port 80 (HTTP) or 443 (HTTPS) and listens for incoming connections.
2. **Connection Acceptance:** When a browser initiates a TCP connection, the server completes the three-way handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`).
3. **Request Reception:** The server reads the HTTP request, which includes a request line (`GET /path HTTP/1.1`), headers (`Host`, `User-Agent`), and optional body.
4. **URL Resolution:** The URL path is mapped to a physical file in the document root, e.g., `/var/www/html/index.html`.
5. **Content Generation:** For static files, the server reads them from disk. For dynamic requests, the server delegates to a script interpreter.
6. **Response Transmission:** The server constructs an HTTP response with a status code, headers, and body, and transmits it to the client.
7. **Logging:** The transaction is recorded in access and error logs for monitoring and debugging.

A block diagram was provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Part (b) — HTTP Protocol, Apache, and Nginx (7 Marks)

**HTTP Protocol Role:**

HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the **application-layer protocol** governing the format and timing of requests/responses between client and server. Key methods include:
- `GET` — Retrieve a resource
- `POST` — Submit data
- `PUT` — Update a resource
- `DELETE` — Remove a resource

**Apache HTTP Server:**
- Open-source, process/thread-based architecture
- Uses `.htaccess` for per-directory configuration
- Highly modular: `mod_php`, `mod_ssl`, `mod_rewrite`
- Best suited for shared hosting and PHP applications

**Nginx:**
- Event-driven, asynchronous, non-blocking architecture
- Handles thousands of concurrent connections with minimal memory
- Excels at serving static content and acting as a reverse proxy/load balancer
- Centralized configuration in `nginx.conf`; does not support `.htaccess`

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Apache | Nginx |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Process/Thread | Event-driven |
| Static Content | Good | Excellent |
| `.htaccess` | Supported | Not supported |
| Memory Footprint | Higher | Lower |
| Reverse Proxy | Via module | Native |

*(Self-Assessment: 7+7 = 14/14 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks Alternative): What is a web server? Explain HTTP request and response message formats with examples. Discuss web server security considerations. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Web Server & HTTP Message Formats (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **Web Server** is a software/hardware system that serves web content over HTTP/HTTPS.

**HTTP Request Format:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Request Line: } \texttt{GET /index.html HTTP/1.1} \\
&\text{Headers: } \texttt{Host: example.com} \\
&\text{Headers: } \texttt{User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0} \\
&\text{Empty Line: } \\
&\text{Body: (optional, for POST)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**HTTP Response Format:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Status Line: } \texttt{HTTP/1.1 200 OK} \\
&\text{Headers: } \texttt{Content-Type: text/html} \\
&\text{Headers: } \texttt{Content-Length: 1234} \\
&\text{Empty Line: } \\
&\text{Body: } \texttt{<html>...</html>}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Web Server Security (7 Marks)

Common security considerations include:

1. **HTTPS/TLS Encryption** — Prevents eavesdropping on port 443.
2. **Authentication & Authorization** — `.htpasswd`, OAuth, JWT tokens.
3. **Input Validation** — Prevents SQL injection and XSS attacks.
4. **Firewall Rules** — Restrict access by IP and port.
5. **Rate Limiting** — Prevents DDoS attacks.
6. **Regular Patching** — Mitigates known CVEs.
7. **Disable Directory Listing** — Avoid exposing file structure.
8. **Security Headers** — `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Frame-Options`, `Strict-Transport-Security`.

*(Self-Assessment: 7+7 = 14/14 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Web Server Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Web Server" with "Application Server":** Tomcat is an application server (servlet container), not a pure web server. Apache and Nginx are web servers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Port Numbers:** Always state **port 80 for HTTP** and **port 443 for HTTPS** in definitions.
> 3. **Mixing up 401 vs 403:** 401 = "You must log in"; 403 = "You are logged in but not allowed." This is a classic trick question.
> 4. **Omitting the Three-Way Handshake:** A trace question that skips `SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK` loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Static vs Dynamic Confusion:** Dynamic content does **not** mean "JavaScript animations" — it means **server-side generation**. JavaScript is client-side.
> 6. **Apache vs Nginx architecture:** Saying "Nginx is faster because it is newer" earns 0 marks. The correct reason is **event-driven asynchronous I/O vs process-per-connection**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A web server serves web content over HTTP/HTTPS on **ports 80/443**.
- **Two senses:** Hardware (physical machine) and Software (Apache, Nginx, IIS).
- **Process cycle:** Bind → Listen → Accept → Parse → Map → Generate → Respond → Log.
- **Static content** = pre-stored files; **Dynamic content** = generated by server scripts.
- **HTTP methods:** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE` (memorize all four).
- **Status codes:** 200 (OK), 301 (Moved), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Server Error), 503 (Unavailable).
- **Apache:** Process-based, modular, supports `.htaccess`.
- **Nginx:** Event-driven, low memory, excellent for static content & reverse proxy.
- **IIS:** Windows-only, integrated with ASP.NET.
- **Security essentials:** HTTPS, authentication, input validation, rate limiting, patches.
- **Key formulas:** Throughput $R = N/T$; Response time $\bar{L} = C/R$; Uptime $S = T_{up}/(T_{up}+T_{down})$.
- **Architectural distinction:** Process-per-request (Apache) vs Event-driven async (Nginx).
- **Application vs Web Server:** Tomcat = application server; Apache/Nginx = web server.
- **First browser hit (Easter egg for memory):** Tim Berners-Lee's `httpd` (1990) was the world's first web server, running on a NeXT computer.
- **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Working mechanism, HTTP messages, Apache vs Nginx comparison, security.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Web Design (Basics of HTML and JavaScript) - Understanding the Web Content Delivery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Web Design (Basics of HTML and JavaScript) - Understanding the Web Content Delivery # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Web Content Delivery** refers to the end-to-end process of transmitting, rendering, and executing digital content (text, images, multimedia, scripts) from a **Web Server** to a **Client** (typically a web browser) over the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP/HTTPS)**. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, this encompasses the foundational triad of web technologies: **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** for structural content, **CSS (Cascading Style Sheets)** for presentation, and **JavaScript** for client-side behavioral logic. The **World Wide Web (WWW)** operates on a **Client-Server Architecture** where: - The **Client** sends an HTTP Request (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) via a Uniform Resource Locator (URL). - The **Server** processes the request and returns an HTTP Response containing a **Status Code**, **Headers**, and a **Body** (typically HTML/CSS/JS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly focuses on *Basic HTML structure* (tags, elements, attributes, forms) and *JavaScript fundamentals* (variables, data types, control flow, functions, DOM manipulation). Students must master the syntax, semantic meaning, and execution model of both languages. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine a **restaurant**: - The **Customer (Client/Browser)** is sitting at a table and wants food (web content). - The **Menu (URL)** lists all available dishes (web pages). - The **Waiter (HTTP Protocol)** takes the order (Request) to the kitchen. - The **Kitchen (Web Server)** prepares the dish (HTML page) and hands it to the waiter. - The **Dish (HTML Document)** is the final served content. But the dish is just the main course — **CSS** is the *garnish and plating* (how it looks), and **JavaScript** is the *chef's special interactive elements* (like a sizzler plate that reacts when served). > [!NOTE] > **Core Definitions Box:** > - **HTML:** A declarative, tag-based markup language used to structure web documents. It uses `` enclosed in angle brackets. > - **JavaScript (JS):** A high-level, interpreted, dynamically-typed, multi-paradigm scripting language that conforms to the **ECMAScript (ES)** specification. It enables interactivity in browsers. > - **DOM (Document Object Model):** A tree-structured, in-memory representation of the HTML document that JavaScript can read and modify. ## 1.3 Standard Web Metrics & Units The following are the standard units and metrics used in web content delivery: - **File Size Measurement:** **Bytes (B)**, **Kilobytes (KB)**, **Megabytes (MB)** where 1 KB = **1024 Bytes** (binary) or **1000 Bytes** (SI). - **Response Time:** Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. Industry standard for a good user experience is **< 200 ms**. - **HTTP Status Code Ranges:** - **1xx** → Informational - **2xx** → Success (e.g., **200 OK**) - **3xx** → Redirection (e.g., **301 Moved Permanently**) - **4xx** → Client Error (e.g., **404 Not Found**) - **5xx** → Server Error (e.g., **500 Internal Server Error**) - **Port Number for HTTP:** **80** | **Port for HTTPS:** **443** - **Default Page Name:** `index.html` or `default.html` # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Web Content Delivery Pipeline (Operational Breakdown) The complete journey of a web request involves six structured stages: 1. **URL Resolution & DNS Lookup:** The browser parses the URL (e.g., `https://www.ktu.edu.in/index.html`) and queries the **Domain Name System (DNS)** to translate the domain name into an **IP address** (e.g., `192.168.1.1`). 2. **TCP/IP Connection Establishment:** A **3-Way Handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) is performed to establish a reliable connection between client and server. 3. **HTTP Request Dispatch:** The browser sends an HTTP request message containing: - **Request Line:** Method + URI + HTTP Version - **Headers:** Metadata (User-Agent, Accept, Host, Cookie) - **Body:** (Optional, for POST/PUT methods) 4. **Server-Side Processing:** The web server (Apache, Nginx, IIS) locates the resource, executes server-side scripts if needed, and constructs the response. 5. **HTTP Response Transmission:** The server returns the response with a **Status Code**, **Response Headers**, and the **HTML Body**. 6. **Browser Rendering Engine Execution:** The browser parses HTML → constructs the **DOM Tree**, parses CSS → constructs the **CSSOM Tree**, executes JavaScript → manipulates the DOM. Finally, a **Render Tree** is generated and pixels are painted on the screen. ## 2.2 HTML Structural Hierarchy Every valid HTML5 document follows a strict nested structure. The root element is ``, which contains two mandatory children: `` (metadata container) and `` (visible content container). ```text ← Declares HTML5 standard ← Root element ├── ← Metadata (not displayed) │ ├── │ ├── <meta> │ └── <link> └── <body> ← Visible content ├── <h1>-<h6> ← Headings ├── <p> ← Paragraphs ├── <a> ← Hyperlinks ├── <img> ← Images ├── <ul>/<ol> ← Lists └── <form> ← Interactive forms ``` ## 2.3 JavaScript Execution Model JavaScript is executed by the browser's **JavaScript Engine** (e.g., V8 in Chrome, SpiderMonkey in Firefox). It operates on a **single-threaded event loop** with a **call stack**, **heap**, and **callback queue**. **Key Data Types:** - **Primitive Types:** `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`, `symbol`, `bigint` - **Reference Type:** `object` (including `Array`, `Function`, `Date`, etc.) ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Web Technologies Quick Reference | **Component** | **Syntax / Pattern** | **Purpose** | **Example** | |---|---|---|---| | HTML Tag | `<tagname>content</tagname>` | Defines an element | `<p>Hello</p>` | | Self-Closing Tag | `<tagname />` | Void elements | `<br />`, `<img />` | | HTML Attribute | `attribute="value"` | Provides element metadata | `<a href="url">` | | JS Variable (Modern) | `let` / `const` | Block-scoped declaration | `let x = 10;` | | JS Variable (Legacy) | `var` | Function-scoped declaration | `var y = 20;` | | JS Function | `function name(){}` | Reusable code block | `function greet(){}` | | Arrow Function | `() => {}` | ES6+ concise function | `const add = (a,b) => a+b;` | | DOM Selector | `document.getElementById()` | Access HTML element | `document.getElementById("demo")` | | Event Handler | `onclick="function()"` | Trigger JS on user action | `<button onclick="fn()">` | | Form Input | `<input type="text">` | User data entry | `<input name="email" />` | | HTTP Method | `GET`, `POST` | Request type | Form submission | | Status Code | `200`, `404`, `500` | Response status | Success / Not Found / Error | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Web content delivery underpins every modern digital service — e-commerce, banking, healthcare portals, IoT dashboards, and government e-governance platforms. Understanding HTTP request-response cycles is critical for cybersecurity, performance optimization (CDNs, caching), and full-stack development. <!-- SECTION_2_END --> <!-- SECTION_3_START --> # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Example: HTML Page Request-Response Cycle Let us trace a complete web content delivery scenario with a worked numerical/analytical example. **Problem Statement:** A user types `https://university.edu/course.html` into their browser. Analyze the request-response cycle, expected status codes, and a complete HTML+JS code structure. ### Step 1: URL Decomposition A URL is parsed into five components: - **Scheme:** `https` (HyperText Transfer Protocol Secure) - **Subdomain:** `www` (optional) - **Domain:** `university.edu` - **Path:** `/course.html` - **Port (implicit):** 443 (for HTTPS) ### Step 2: DNS Resolution Time Calculation The total time to fetch a webpage is the sum of multiple latencies. Let us derive a simplified model: $$ T_{total} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} + T_{Request} + T_{Server} + T_{Transfer} + T_{Render} $$ Where: - $T_{DNS}$ = Domain Name System lookup time (typically 20–120 ms) - $T_{TCP}$ = TCP handshake time (1 RTT ≈ 0.5 ms on LAN) - $T_{TLS}$ = TLS/SSL negotiation time (1–2 RTT for HTTPS) - $T_{Request}$ = Time to send HTTP request - $T_{Server}$ = Server processing time - $T_{Transfer}$ = Data transfer time = $\frac{\text{File Size (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth (bps)}}$ - $T_{Render}$ = Browser rendering time **Numerical Substitution:** Given: - File size = **250 KB** = $250 \times 1024 \times 8 = 2,048,000$ bits - Bandwidth = **4 Mbps** = $4 \times 10^6$ bps $$ T_{Transfer} = \frac{2{,}048{,}000 \text{ bits}}{4 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.512 \text{ seconds} = 512 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_{total} \approx 50 + 5 + 20 + 5 + 100 + 512 + 200 = 892 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 3: Complete HTML+JavaScript Code Construction The following is a complete, valid HTML5 document demonstrating core concepts from the KTU syllabus: ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>KTU Student Portal

Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme

Click the button to display your details.





``` ### Step 4: Status Code Decision Matrix The following analytical matrix maps user actions to expected HTTP response codes: | **Scenario** | **Client Action** | **Server Action** | **Status Code** | |---|---|---|---| | Page exists | GET /course.html | Returns HTML file | **200 OK** | | Page moved | GET /oldpage.html | Redirects to new URL | **301 Moved Permanently** | | Page missing | GET /missing.html | Returns error page | **404 Not Found** | | Form submitted | POST /register | Server processes data | **200 OK** or **201 Created** | | Server crash | Any request | Internal error | **500 Internal Server Error** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Question papers frequently test the *reasoning* behind HTTP methods (GET is idempotent and cacheable; POST is not), the *semantic meaning* of tags (semantic HTML5 like `
`, `
`, `
` vs. non-semantic `
`), and the *execution order* of script parsing in the browser. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Client-Server Web Content Delivery Flow The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete web content delivery architecture from request to rendering: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[User enters URL in Browser] --> B[Browser parses URL] B --> C[DNS Lookup] C --> D[Establish TCP Connection] D --> E[Send HTTP GET Request] E --> F[Web Server receives request] F --> G{Resource exists?} G -->|Yes| H[Server sends HTTP 200 + HTML file] G -->|No| I[Server sends HTTP 404 Error] H --> J[Browser receives response] I --> J J --> K[HTML Parser builds DOM Tree] K --> L[CSS Parser builds CSSOM Tree] L --> M[JavaScript Engine executes scripts] M --> N[DOM manipulation if required] N --> O[Render Tree constructed] O --> P[Layout calculation] P --> Q[Paint pixels to screen] Q --> R[User sees final webpage] ``` ## 4.2 HTML Document Object Model (DOM) Tree Structure The following diagram shows how an HTML document is represented as a hierarchical tree by the browser: ```mermaid flowchart TD docNode[Document Root] --> htmlNode[html element] htmlNode --> headNode[head element] htmlNode --> bodyNode[body element] headNode --> titleNode[title element] headNode --> metaNode[meta element] bodyNode --> h1Node[h1 element - Welcome] bodyNode --> formNode[form element] bodyNode --> buttonNode[button element] bodyNode --> scriptNode[script element] formNode --> inputNode1[input type=text] formNode --> inputNode2[input type=submit] scriptNode --> funcNode1[function showMessage] scriptNode --> funcNode2[function validateForm] ``` ## 4.3 JavaScript Event Loop and Execution Model The following Mermaid diagram depicts how JavaScript handles asynchronous operations in the browser: ```mermaid flowchart LR mainThread[Call Stack - Main Thread] --> exec1[Execute synchronous code] exec1 --> checkQueue{Is call stack empty?} checkQueue -->|Yes| processQueue[Process Callback Queue] checkQueue -->|No| exec1 processQueue --> async1[Event: Click handler] processQueue --> async2[Event: Timer callback] processQueue --> async3[Event: AJAX response] async1 --> render[Browser Repaints UI] async2 --> render async3 --> render render --> checkQueue ``` ## 4.4 Form Data Submission Workflow The following block-level functional architecture shows how form data flows from client to server: ```mermaid flowchart TD userInput[User fills form fields] --> validateJS[JavaScript client-side validation] validateJS -->|Invalid| showError[Display error message] validateJS -->|Valid| submitForm[Submit form to server] submitForm --> httpPost[HTTP POST request with form data] httpPost --> serverProcess[Server-side script processes data] serverProcess --> storeData[Store in database] serverProcess --> sendResponse[Send HTTP 200 with success page] sendResponse --> browserRender[Browser displays confirmation] showError --> userInput ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `mainThread`, `httpPost`) to avoid conflicts with reserved Mermaid keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. Labels are kept as raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define HTML. List any four commonly used HTML tags with their purpose.** **Model Answer:** **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** is the standard markup language used to create and structure the content of web pages. It uses a system of *tags* enclosed in angle brackets to define elements like headings, paragraphs, links, and images. | **Tag** | **Purpose** | |---|---| | `

` to `

` | Define headings of varying levels | | `

` | Defines a paragraph of text | | `` | Creates a hyperlink to another resource | | `` | Embeds an image in the page | | `` | Creates a tabular data structure | --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the difference between `let`, `const`, and `var` in JavaScript with examples.** **Model Answer:** | **Keyword** | **Scope** | **Reassignable?** | **Redeclarable?** | **Hoisted?** | |---|---|---|---|---| | `var` | Function-scoped | Yes | Yes | Yes (initialized as `undefined`) | | `let` | Block-scoped (`{}`) | Yes | No | Yes (but in Temporal Dead Zone) | | `const` | Block-scoped (`{}`) | No (immutable binding) | No | Yes (but in Temporal Dead Zone) | **Example Code:** ```javascript var a = 10; // Function-scoped, can be redeclared let b = 20; // Block-scoped, cannot be redeclared in same block const c = 30; // Block-scoped, cannot be reassigned if (true) { let b = 50; // Different b, block-scoped to this if-block console.log(b); // Output: 50 } console.log(b); // Output: 20 ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of web content delivery. Describe the role of HTTP protocol, DNS, and the browser rendering engine in delivering a webpage to the user. Include a labeled diagram of the request-response cycle. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete HTML5 document that contains: - A heading "KTU Student Registration" - A form with fields: Name (text), Email (email), Branch (dropdown), and a Submit button - JavaScript validation that ensures all fields are filled before submission - A `

KTU Student Registration








``` **Code Explanation:** - The `validateForm()` function checks if all fields are non-empty using the logical OR (`||`) operator. - The `showGreeting()` function accesses form values using `document.getElementById()` and updates the DOM using `.innerHTML`. - The `onsubmit="return validateForm()"` pattern ensures the form is not submitted if validation fails. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between HTML and JavaScript. Explain the role of the **Document Object Model (DOM)** in enabling JavaScript to interact with HTML elements. Provide an example demonstrating dynamic content modification. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write JavaScript code to: - Declare an array of 5 student names - Use a `for` loop to iterate and display all names in an HTML `
    ` element with `id="studentList"` - Calculate and display the total number of students - Create a function that searches for a specific student name entered by the user and displays whether they are found **Model Solution:** **(a) HTML vs JavaScript & DOM Role:** | **Feature** | **HTML** | **JavaScript** | |---|---|---| | Type | Markup Language | Scripting Language | | Purpose | Structure & content | Behavior & interactivity | | Execution | Parsed by browser | Executed by JS engine | | Syntax | Tag-based `` | Statement-based | | Dynamic? | Static (without JS) | Dynamic | | File Extension | `.html` | `.js` | **Role of the Document Object Model (DOM):** The **DOM** is a programming interface (API) provided by the browser that represents the HTML document as a **tree of objects**. Each HTML element becomes a **node** in this tree. JavaScript can: - **Read** element content (`element.innerHTML`) - **Modify** element attributes (`element.setAttribute()`) - **Change** styles (`element.style.color`) - **Add/Remove** elements (`document.createElement()`, `element.remove()`) - **Respond to events** (`element.addEventListener()`) **Example of Dynamic Content Modification:** ```html DOM Demo

    Original Title

    ``` When the user clicks the button, the `changeTitle()` function accesses the `

    ` element via the DOM and dynamically changes its text content, color, and font size. **(b) JavaScript Array Operations and Search:** ```html Student List

    Student List

      Search Student

      ``` **Code Explanation:** - The `displayStudents()` function uses `document.createElement("li")` to dynamically create list items and `appendChild()` to add them to the `
        `. - The `searchStudent()` function performs a case-insensitive linear search using a `for` loop and a `break` statement for early termination. - The `onload="displayStudents()"` event in the `` tag triggers the display function when the page finishes loading. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Missing DOCTYPE Declaration:** Always include `` at the top — omitting it triggers *quirks mode* rendering and costs marks. > 2. **Unclosed Tags:** Forgetting to close tags like `

        `, ``, or `` breaks the DOM tree and causes layout errors. > 3. **Using `var` in Modern JS:** Prefer `let` and `const` in 2024 Scheme answers. Using only `var` suggests outdated knowledge. > 4. **Confusing `innerHTML` with `innerText`:** `innerHTML` parses HTML tags; `innerText` treats everything as plain text. Examiners test this distinction. > 5. **Missing `return false` in Form Validation:** Without `return false`, the form submits even when validation fails, defeating the purpose of client-side validation. > 6. **Forgetting to Explain the 'Why':** Don't just write code — explain *what each line does* and *why it's used*. KTU awards marks for conceptual clarity, not just working code. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **HTML = Structure** (markup), **CSS = Style** (presentation), **JavaScript = Behavior** (interactivity) - ✅ Every HTML5 document starts with `` declaration - ✅ The `` contains **metadata**; the `` contains **visible content** - ✅ HTML tags can be **container tags** (`

        ...

        `) or **void/self-closing tags** (`
        `, ``, `
        `, ``) - ✅ Attributes provide **additional information** about elements (e.g., `id`, `class`, `href`, `src`, `type`) - ✅ **Semantic HTML5 tags** like `
        `, `
        `, `
        `, `
        `, `
        ` improve accessibility and SEO - ✅ JavaScript uses `let` (block-scoped, reassignable) and `const` (block-scoped, immutable binding) — avoid `var` in modern code - ✅ **Data types:** `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, `undefined`, `object`, `array` - ✅ **DOM manipulation** uses methods like `getElementById()`, `querySelector()`, `createElement()`, `appendChild()` - ✅ **Event handling** can be inline (`onclick="fn()"`) or via `addEventListener()` - ✅ **Form validation** can be **client-side** (JavaScript) or **server-side** (PHP, Python, Node.js) - ✅ **HTTP Status Codes to memorize:** 200 (OK), 301 (Redirect), 404 (Not Found), 500 (Server Error) - ✅ **HTTP Methods:** GET (retrieve, idempotent, cacheable) vs POST (submit data, not idempotent) - ✅ **Default ports:** HTTP → 80, HTTPS → 443 - ✅ The **3-way TCP handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) establishes every web connection - ✅ JavaScript is **single-threaded** with an **event loop** for asynchronous operations - ✅ The **critical rendering path:** HTML Parse → DOM → CSS Parse → CSSOM → Render Tree → Layout → Paint → Composite > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering theory questions, always include **diagrams** (use Mermaid or labeled sketches) and **code examples** in your answer. KTU examiners award significant marks for visual representations and practical demonstrations. For 14-mark questions, aim for a balanced 60% theory + 40% code explanation split.

topics.title: Understanding HTML and XHTML Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Understanding HTML and XHTML Connections # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is HTML? **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** is the standard markup language used to create and structure sections, paragraphs, and links in web pages. It is the foundational layer of the World Wide Web and is interpreted by web browsers to render visual content. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)** > HTML is a markup language that uses a system of *tags* enclosed in angle brackets (`< >`) to describe the structure and presentation of web documents. The latest version is **HTML5** (W3C Recommendation, 2014). ### Historical Versions of HTML | Version | Year | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | HTML 1.0 | 1993 | Basic tags, headings, paragraphs | | HTML 2.0 | 1995 | Forms, tables introduced | | HTML 3.2 | 1997 | Scripts, applets, tables enhanced | | HTML 4.01 | 1999 | Strict, Transitional, Frameset DTDs | | **XHTML 1.0** | 2000 | HTML reformulated in XML syntax | | **XHTML 1.1** | 2001 | Modular XHTML | | **HTML5** | 2014 | Native audio/video, canvas, semantic tags | ## 1.2 What is XHTML? **XHTML (eXtensible HyperText Markup Language)** is a family of XML-based markup languages that mirror and extend HTML 4.01. It was developed by the **W3C (World Wide Web Consortium)** to bring the strictness and extensibility of XML to web documents. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Insight:** XHTML is essentially **HTML 4.01 rewritten as a strict XML application**. Every XHTML document is also a valid XML document, but not every HTML document is valid XHTML. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Two Languages" Imagine you have a **letter** that you want to send via postal mail: - **HTML** is like a **handwritten letter** — flexible, forgiving of small mistakes, smudged ink is okay, you can skip the date, and the reader will still understand most of it. - **XHTML** is like a **formal business email** — strict formatting rules, every field required, every line must end properly, no errors tolerated, or the system rejects the entire message. Both convey the same information, but **XHTML demands discipline**, while **HTML allows artistic freedom**. ## 1.4 The Connection Between HTML and XHTML The connection can be summarized in three core principles: 1. **Same Purpose**: Both are designed to display content on web browsers. 2. **Same Tags**: XHTML uses the same tag set as HTML 4.01. 3. **Different Syntax Rules**: XHTML applies strict XML parsing rules. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Statement:** "XHTML 1.0 is a reformulation of HTML 4.01 as an XML 1.0 application." — This single sentence is the bridge between the two technologies. ### Visual Summary of the Relationship ``` SGML (Standard Generalized Markup Language) | +-------+--------+ | | HTML 4.01 XML 1.0 | | +-------+--------+ | XHTML 1.0 | +-------+--------+ | | XHTML 1.1 XHTML5 | HTML5 (the reunion) ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Rules Sheet ## 2.1 Why Was XHTML Created? The W3C created XHTML to solve four major problems of HTML 4.01: 1. **Interoperability Across Devices**: Mobile phones, PDAs, and embedded devices required strict markup to parse correctly. HTML's lenient parsing caused inconsistencies. 2. **Extensibility**: XML allows developers to create new tags and namespaces. HTML did not. 3. **Data Exchange**: XML is the universal format for data interchange (SOAP, RSS, SVG). XHTML bridged web pages with this XML ecosystem. 4. **Cleaner Code**: Strict rules forced developers to write well-structured, error-free markup. ## 2.2 The XHTML Syntax Rules (High-Yield for KTU) These are the **Seven Golden Rules of XHTML** that examiners love to ask: | # | Rule | HTML Behavior | XHTML Requirement | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nesting | Tags can be improperly nested | Tags must be **properly nested** | | 2 | Case Sensitivity | Tags are case-insensitive (`

` = `

`) | Tags must be **lowercase** | | 3 | Closing Tags | Some tags may be unclosed (e.g., `
`) | **Every tag must be closed** | | 4 | Attribute Values | Quotes optional (`width=100`) | **Quotes mandatory** (`width="100"`) | | 5 | Attribute Minimization | `checked` allowed | **Must be** `checked="checked"` | | 6 | Root Element | `` optional in some cases | `` is **mandatory** with xmlns | | 7 | DOCTYPE | Optional but recommended | **Mandatory** for every document | ## 2.3 Document Type Definitions (DTDs) A **DTD** tells the browser which version of (X)HTML the document follows and what syntax rules apply. > [!NOTE] > **The Three XHTML 1.0 DTDs:** > - **XHTML 1.0 Strict**: No deprecated tags, no frames, cleanest markup. > - **XHTML 1.0 Transitional**: Allows deprecated tags (for migration). > - **XHTML 1.0 Frameset**: Allows frame-based layouts. ### Code Structure of an XHTML 1.0 Document ```xml My XHTML Page

Hello, XHTML World!

``` ## 2.4 Comparison Table: HTML vs XHTML (Most Asked in KTU) | Feature | HTML 4.01 | XHTML 1.0 | |---|---|---| | Based On | SGML | XML | | Parsing | Lenient (forgiving) | Strict (well-formed required) | | Case Sensitive | No | Yes | | Closing Tags | Optional for some | Mandatory for all | | Attribute Quotes | Optional | Mandatory | | Custom Tags | Not allowed | Allowed (with XML namespaces) | | DOCTYPE | Optional | Mandatory | | Error Tolerance | Browser guesses | Document rejected on error | | File Extension | `.html` or `.htm` | `.xhtml`, `.xml`, `.html` | | MIME Type | `text/html` | `application/xhtml+xml` | ## 2.5 Similarities Between HTML and XHTML - Both use the **same tag set** (h1–h6, p, div, span, a, img, table, etc.). - Both are **rendered by web browsers**. - Both support **forms, images, links, and multimedia** (with extensions). - Both have a **head section** and a **body section**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility XHTML's strict syntax laid the groundwork for: - **HTML5 parsing algorithms** - **Mobile web (WML → XHTML MP)** - **Web services (XML-based)** - **Modern Single Page Applications (React, Angular use XHTML-style syntax)** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern Context (2024):** HTML5 effectively merged the best of both worlds — the **leniency of HTML** with the **discipline of XHTML**. Modern web developers write XHTML-compliant HTML5 by default. # 3. Step-by-Step Conversions & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Conversion: HTML → XHTML Below is the **complete step-by-step transformation** of a sloppy HTML document into a valid XHTML document. This is a frequently asked **14-mark question** in KTU examinations. ### Step 1 — Original (Invalid) HTML Document ```html My Page

Welcome

This is a paragraph
``` ### Step 2 — Add XML Declaration The very first line of an XHTML document must declare the XML version and character encoding. This is mandatory because XHTML is an XML application. ```xml ``` ### Step 3 — Add the DOCTYPE Declaration The DOCTYPE must reference the DTD. For Strict XHTML 1.0: ```xml ``` ### Step 4 — Convert `` to Lowercase with xmlns Rule: All tags must be lowercase. The `` element must declare the XHTML namespace. ```xml ``` ### Step 5 — Lowercase All Other Tags `` → ``, `` → ``, `` → `<title>`, `<BODY>` → `<body>`, `<H1>` → `<h1>`, `<P>` → `<p>`, `<IMG>` → `<img>`, `<INPUT>` → `<input>`. ### Step 6 — Close All Unclosed Tags Rule: Every element must have a closing tag. For empty elements, use the self-closing syntax `<tag />`. - `<BR>` becomes `<br />` - `<IMG>` becomes `<img />` ### Step 7 — Quote All Attribute Values Rule: All attribute values must be enclosed in double quotes. - `SRC=logo.jpg` becomes `src="logo.jpg"` - `TYPE=checkbox` becomes `type="checkbox"` ### Step 8 — Fully Expand Attribute Names (No Minimization) Rule: Attributes cannot be minimized. Standalone attributes must have explicit values. - `CHECKED` becomes `checked="checked"` ### Step 9 — Final Valid XHTML Document ```xml <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD XHTML 1.0 Strict//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-strict.dtd"> <html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml"> <head> <title>My Page

Welcome

This is a paragraph

``` > [!NOTE] > **Note on `

` nesting:** In the final version, the `
`, ``, and `` were wrapped inside `

...

`. This is because in Strict XHTML, block-level elements like `

` cannot contain certain inline elements loosely — proper nesting is enforced. ## 3.2 Analytical Verification: XML Well-Formedness Check For an XHTML document to be valid, it must pass these XML well-formedness tests: | Test | Formula/Condition | Result | |---|---|---| | Single Root Element | Count of root elements = **1** | Pass | | Proper Nesting | For every ``, must close `` | Pass | | Case Consistency | Opening and closing tags match in case | Pass | | Quoted Attributes | `attr="value"` always | Pass | | Unique IDs | No duplicate `id="..."` values | Pass | | Entity Escaping | `<` → `<`, `>` → `>`, `&` → `&` | Pass | If any test fails, the XML parser will **reject the entire document** — there is no partial rendering. This is fundamentally different from HTML, where browsers render what they can and ignore errors. ## 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Strictness We can quantify the strictness difference: Let $E$ = number of errors in the source code. Let $D$ = resulting display behavior. $$ D_{HTML} = \begin{cases} \text{Render with fallback} & \text{if } E \geq 0 \end{cases} $$ $$ D_{XHTML} = \begin{cases} \text{Full Render} & \text{if } E = 0 \\ \text{Parse Error (no display)} & \text{if } E \geq 1 \end{cases} $$ $$ \therefore \quad \text{Strictness}_{XHTML} \gg \text{Strictness}_{HTML} $$ This shows that **HTML is error-tolerant (lenient parsing)**, while **XHTML is error-intolerant (strict parsing)**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution Flow: From SGML to HTML5 ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SGML - 1986] --> B[HTML 1.0 - 1993] A --> C[XML 1.0 - 1998] B --> D[HTML 2.0 - 1995] D --> E[HTML 3.2 - 1997] E --> F[HTML 4.01 - 1999] F --> G[XHTML 1.0 - 2000] C --> G G --> H[XHTML 1.1 - 2001] G --> I[XHTML 2.0 - Abandoned 2009] H --> J[XHTML5 - 2014] F --> K[HTML5 - 2014] J --> K K --> L[HTML Living Standard - Today] ``` ## 4.2 Architecture: HTML vs XHTML Document Parsing ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HTML["HTML 4.01 Parsing Path"] H1[HTML Document] --> H2{TagSoup Parser} H2 --> H3[Lenient Tree Builder] H3 --> H4[Browser Display] H2 -->|Error Found| H5[Skip Error and Continue] H5 --> H3 end subgraph XHTML["XHTML 1.0 Parsing Path"] X1[XHTML Document] --> X2{XML Strict Parser} X2 -->|Well-Formed| X3[DOM Tree] X3 --> X4[Browser Display] X2 -->|Any Error| X5[Parser Stops] X5 --> X6[Display Error Message] end ``` ## 4.3 The Three XHTML 1.0 DTDs Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD Root[XHTML 1.0 Document] --> DTD{Choose DOCTYPE} DTD --> S[Strict DTD] DTD --> T[Transitional DTD] DTD --> F[Frameset DTD] S --> S1[No Deprecated Tags] S --> S2[No Frames] S --> S3[CSS for Styling] T --> T1[Deprecated Tags Allowed] T --> T2[For HTML Migration] T --> T3[Inline Styling Permitted] F --> F1[Frame Tags Allowed] F --> F2[Framesets Permitted] F --> F3[Legacy Layout Support] ``` ## 4.4 Conversion Workflow: HTML to XHTML ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: HTML Document] --> P1[Step 1: Add XML Declaration] P1 --> P2[Step 2: Add DOCTYPE] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Lowercase All Tags] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Close All Tags] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Quote All Attributes] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Expand Minimized Attributes] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Add xmlns to html] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Escape Special Characters] P8 --> Validate{XML Validator} Validate -->|Pass| Success[Valid XHTML Document] Validate -->|Fail| Fix[Fix Errors] Fix --> P3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Q: Define XHTML. How is it different from HTML?** **Model Answer:** **XHTML (eXtensible HyperText Markup Language)** is a markup language that combines the flexibility of HTML with the strictness of XML. It is essentially HTML 4.01 reformulated as an XML 1.0 application. **Key Differences:** | Aspect | HTML | XHTML | |---|---|---| | Parsing | Lenient | Strict XML | | Closing Tags | Optional | Mandatory | | Case | Insensitive | Lowercase only | | MIME Type | `text/html` | `application/xhtml+xml` | --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **Q: List any four syntax rules that must be followed while writing an XHTML document.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Lowercase tags and attributes**: `

` is invalid; use `

`. 2. **Close all tags**: Even empty tags like `
` must be `
`. 3. **Quote attribute values**: Use `width="100"` not `width=100`. 4. **No attribute minimization**: Use `checked="checked"` not `checked`. 5. **Proper nesting**: Tags must close in reverse order of opening. 6. **Mandatory DOCTYPE** and `` declaration. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Model Paper KTU 2024]** **(a)** Explain the relationship between HTML, XHTML, and XML with a neat diagram. Discuss why XHTML was developed. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Convert the following HTML document into a valid XHTML 1.0 Strict document. Show all conversion steps. **(7 Marks)** ```html College Page

Welcome to KTU

Engineering Education
Next Page ``` --- ### Model Answer for Question A #### Part (a) — Relationship & Motivation (7 Marks) **Relationship:** XHTML is the result of combining HTML 4.01 (a web markup language) with XML 1.0 (a strict data format). It bridges the gap between the **human-readable web** (HTML) and the **machine-parseable data interchange** (XML). ``` HTML 4.01 XML 1.0 (Web Pages) + (Data Rules) \ / \ / XHTML 1.0 (Reformulated) ``` **Why XHTML was developed:** 1. To enforce **strict document structure** for consistent browser parsing. 2. To enable **mobile and embedded device compatibility**, which required predictable markup. 3. To allow **extensibility through XML namespaces** — developers can define new tags. 4. To integrate web content with **XML-based technologies** (SOAP, SVG, MathML). 5. To eliminate the **tag soup** problems of HTML 4.01. #### Part (b) — Step-by-Step Conversion (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Original HTML** (already shown in question). **Step 2 — Add XML Declaration and DOCTYPE:** ```xml ``` **Step 3 — Lowercase all tags** (`` → ``, `` → ``, etc.). **Step 4 — Close all tags**: Add closing ``, ``, ``. Convert `
` to `
` and `` to ``. **Step 5 — Quote all attributes**: `SRC=college.jpg` → `src="college.jpg"`. **Step 6 — Final XHTML Document:** ```xml College Page

Welcome to KTU

Engineering Education
Next Page

``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Sample Paper]** **(a)** Write short notes on: (i) Document Type Definition (DTD) in XHTML (ii) Well-formedness rules of XML **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare HTML 4.01 and XHTML 1.0 in a tabular format. List the three XHTML 1.0 DTDs and explain their differences. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B #### Part (a) (i) — DTD in XHTML (3.5 Marks) A **Document Type Definition (DTD)** specifies the rules for the structure of an XHTML document. It defines which tags are allowed, their nesting rules, and which attributes are permitted. The DTD is referenced through the `` declaration at the top of the document. For example, the XHTML 1.0 Strict DTD URL is `http://www.w3.org/TR/xhtml1/DTD/xhtml1-strict.dtd`. #### Part (a) (ii) — XML Well-Formedness (3.5 Marks) An XML document is **well-formed** if it satisfies these rules: 1. It contains exactly **one root element**. 2. Every start tag has a matching end tag. 3. Tags are properly nested (no overlapping). 4. Tag names are case-sensitive and consistent. 5. Attribute values are enclosed in quotes. 6. Special characters (`<`, `>`, `&`) are escaped as entities. If any rule is violated, the XML parser reports a fatal error and **does not render the document**. #### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks) | Feature | HTML 4.01 | XHTML 1.0 | |---|---|---| | Parent Standard | SGML | XML | | Tag Case | Insensitive | Lowercase mandatory | | Closing Tags | Optional | Mandatory | | MIME Type | text/html | application/xhtml+xml | | Error Handling | Lenient | Strict (fatal) | | Extensibility | None | XML namespaces | | Custom Tags | Not allowed | Allowed | | DOCTYPE | Optional | Mandatory | **The Three XHTML 1.0 DTDs:** 1. **Strict**: No deprecated elements, no frames, requires CSS. 2. **Transitional**: Allows deprecated tags for migration. 3. **Frameset**: Permits `` elements for legacy multi-frame layouts. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the XML declaration** at the top — costs 1 mark. > 2. **Not adding `xmlns`** to the `` tag — costs 1 mark. > 3. **Leaving unquoted attributes** like `width=100` — costs 1–2 marks. > 4. **Using uppercase tags** like `

` in XHTML — costs 1 mark per instance. > 5. **Forgetting to close empty tags** like `
` instead of `
` — costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **Using deprecated tags** (like ``, `

`) in Strict DTD — costs 2 marks. > 7. **Not mentioning the DOCTYPE URL** correctly — costs 1 mark. > 8. **Confusing XHTML 1.0 with XHTML 1.1** (the latter has no Transitional DTD). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **XHTML = HTML 4.01 + XML 1.0 strictness**. - The **seven golden rules**: lowercase, closed, nested, quoted, expanded, namespaced, DOCTYPE. - **XHTML has three DTDs**: Strict, Transitional, Frameset. - **Every XHTML document is a valid XML document**, but not vice versa. - The MIME type for XHTML is **`application/xhtml+xml`** (not `text/html`). - **HTML5 (2014)** is the modern successor that merged XHTML's discipline with HTML's forgiveness. - The `` element **must** carry `xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml"`. - Empty elements (br, img, hr, input, meta, link) use the **self-closing** form: ``. - **W3C** is the governing body that standardized both HTML and XHTML. - For KTU exams: always show **complete conversion steps** when transforming HTML to XHTML.

topics.title: Understanding Cascading Style Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Understanding Cascading Style Sheets # Module 4 — Understanding Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cascading Style Sheets (CSS)** is a declarative, rule-based **style sheet language** used to describe the presentation, formatting, and visual layout of documents written in a structured markup language (most commonly **HTML** and **XML**). It is the official W3C-recommended standard for separating **content/structure** (handled by HTML) from **presentation/aesthetics** (handled by CSS). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GXEST203 — Module 4):** The module expects students to understand (a) CSS syntax & selectors, (b) three inclusion methods (inline, internal, external), (c) the **Box Model**, (d) text/font/color properties, and (e) the **Cascade, Specificity, and Inheritance** rules that govern which style wins when multiple rules target the same element. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House and the Interior Designer" Imagine you have built the **skeleton of a house** — the walls, doors, windows, rooms, and plumbing. That skeleton is your **HTML document** (the *structure*). The house is functional, but it is plain concrete, bare brick, and uncoloured. Now you hire an **interior designer and painter**. The designer does NOT rebuild the house. Instead, the designer: - Decides the **wall colours** (background, text colour) - Chooses the **furniture arrangement** (positioning: relative, absolute, flex) - Sets the **room sizes and spacing** (margin, padding, width) - Picks the **fonts on signs** (typography) - Applies a **theme** consistently across all rooms (cascading rules) That designer is **CSS**. It does not change the *what* of your content — it controls the *how it looks*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-line essence for the exam:** *HTML = Structure & Content, CSS = Presentation & Layout.* Together with JavaScript (Behaviour), they form the three pillars of front-end web development. ### 1.3 Why CSS Exists — The Separation Principle Before CSS (in the HTML 3.2 era), styling attributes like `` and `
` were embedded directly inside HTML. This caused three major problems: 1. **Code Bloat** — styling mixed with content bloated file size. 2. **Inconsistency** — the same logical "warning message" had to be re-styled on every page. 3. **No Reusability** — changing a brand colour meant editing thousands of files. CSS solves all three by allowing **one external `.css` file** to style **thousands of HTML pages** centrally — a concept called **Single Source of Truth (SSOT)** in software engineering. ### 1.4 The Three Layers of a Styled Web Page | Layer | Language | Responsibility | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Structure | HTML | What the content *is* | `

Hello

` | | Presentation | CSS | How the content *looks* | `h1 { color: blue; }` | | Behaviour | JavaScript | How the content *responds* | `button.onclick = ...` | ### 1.5 Key Vocabulary (High-Frequency KTU Terms) - **Selector** — the part that *chooses* which HTML element(s) to style. - **Declaration Block** — the `{ ... }` block containing one or more declarations. - **Property** — the styling attribute (e.g., `color`, `font-size`). - **Value** — the setting applied to the property (e.g., `red`, `16px`). - **Rule** — one Selector + one Declaration Block = one CSS Rule. - **Cascade** — the algorithm that resolves conflicts when multiple rules apply. - **Specificity** — a numeric weight score deciding which rule wins. - **Inheritance** — some properties (e.g., `color`, `font-family`) pass from parent to child automatically. > [!TIP] > KTU frequently asks: *"List the three methods of inserting CSS in an HTML document."* Memorise them in this exact order: **(1) Inline CSS, (2) Internal/Embedded CSS, (3) External CSS.** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — CSS Syntax, Selectors & Inclusion Methods ### 2.1 Anatomy of a CSS Rule A complete CSS rule is composed of two main parts: $$ \text{CSS Rule} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Selector}}_{\text{Who}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Declaration Block}}_{\text{What to apply}} $$ Each declaration inside the block is a `property: value;` pair. **Generic Syntax (in pseudocode form):** ``` selector { property-1: value-1; property-2: value-2; /* this is a comment */ } ``` **Concrete Example:** ```css h1 { color: #2c3e50; font-size: 32px; text-align: center; } ``` Here, `h1` is the **element selector**, and the three lines inside `{ }` are three **declarations**. ### 2.2 CSS Selectors — The Complete Family Selectors are the targeting mechanism of CSS. KTU's Module 4 expects familiarity with at least the following classes: #### 2.2.1 Basic Selectors 1. **Universal Selector (`*`)** — selects *every* element on the page. Used for CSS resets. 2. **Type / Element Selector (`h1`, `p`, `div`)** — selects all elements of a given tag. 3. **Class Selector (`.classname`)** — selects every element that has `class="classname"`. **Reusable** across elements. 4. **ID Selector (`#idname`)** — selects the **single** element with that unique ID. **Not reusable.** 5. **Grouping Selector (`h1, h2, p`)** — applies the same rule to multiple selectors separated by commas. #### 2.2.2 Combinator Selectors 1. **Descendant Selector (`A B`)** — selects every `B` *inside* an `A` (at any depth). 2. **Child Selector (`A > B`)** — selects only the *direct* child `B` of `A`. 3. **Adjacent Sibling (`A + B`)** — selects `B` that *immediately* follows `A`. 4. **General Sibling (`A ~ B`)** — selects every `B` that follows `A` (at the same level). #### 2.2.3 Attribute Selectors - `[attr]` — has the attribute. - `[attr="value"]` — exact match. - `[attr^="value"]` — starts with. - `[attr$="value"]` — ends with. - `[attr*="value"]` — contains. #### 2.2.4 Pseudo-Classes & Pseudo-Elements (Frequently tested) - `:hover`, `:focus`, `:active`, `:visited`, `:first-child`, `:nth-child(n)`. - `::before`, `::after`, `::first-letter`, `::first-line`. ### 2.3 The Three Methods of CSS Inclusion > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Explain the three ways of applying CSS to an HTML document with examples."* — Master this section thoroughly. #### Method 1 — Inline CSS - **Where:** Inside the HTML `style` attribute of an element. - **Specificity:** Highest (overrides internal & external). - **Reusability:** **Zero** — must be repeated for every element. - **Use case:** Quick testing, dynamic JS-injected styles. ```html

Hello KTU!

``` #### Method 2 — Internal / Embedded CSS - **Where:** Inside a ` ``` #### Method 3 — External CSS - **Where:** A separate file with the `.css` extension, linked via ``. - **Specificity:** Lowest (but most reusable). - **Reusability:** **Maximum** — one file styles thousands of pages. - **Use case:** Production websites, maintainable codebases. ```html ``` ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — CSS Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Total Element Width (Box Model) | $\text{Total} = W + 2(P + B) + 2M$ | $W$=width, $P$=padding, $B$=border, $M$=margin | | Box-Sizing: content-box (default) | declared width = content width | padding & border add *outside* the width | | Box-Sizing: border-box | declared width = content + padding + border | easier responsive layouts | | Specificity Tuple | $(a, b, c, d)$ | inline $\rightarrow$ ID $\rightarrow$ class/attr/pseudo-class $\rightarrow$ element/pseudo-element | | Inheritance | `color`, `font-family`, `font-size`, `line-height`, `visibility` are inherited by default | `margin`, `padding`, `border`, `background` are **not** | | Default `1em` size | $1\,\text{em} = 16\,\text{px}$ | based on browser default font-size | | `rem` unit | relative to root `` font-size | predictable; preferred over `em` | | RGB to Hex | $\text{Hex} = R_{(16)} \, G_{(16)} \, B_{(16)}$ | e.g., `rgb(255,0,0)` $\rightarrow$ `#FF0000` | | Viewport units | $1\,\text{vw} = 1\%$ of viewport width | used for responsive typography | | Cascade order (tie-breaker) | `!important` $\rightarrow$ Inline $\rightarrow$ Specificity $\rightarrow$ Source order | last rule with equal specificity wins | > [!WARNING] > In the **Formula Sheet table above**, never use the vertical pipe `|` character for absolute value. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser safe. The author has done this for you — do not modify it during revision. ### 2.5 The CSS Cascade — The "C" in CSS The word **Cascading** refers to the **priority algorithm** that decides which rule applies when multiple rules target the same element. The cascade uses the following priority hierarchy (from **highest** to **lowest**): 1. **Origin & Importance** — User-agent styles < User styles < Author styles. `!important` reverses the order. 2. **Specificity** — More specific selectors win. 3. **Source Order** — When specificity is equal, the *later* rule in the source wins. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility of CSS - **Brand Consistency** — corporate colour palettes enforced through one CSS file. - **Accessibility** — proper contrast ratios, scalable `rem` units, focus states. - **Performance** — external CSS is cached by the browser, reducing bandwidth. - **Responsive Design** — media queries adapt layouts to mobile, tablet, and desktop. - **Theming** — CSS variables (`--primary-color`) enable dark-mode and white-label themes without JavaScript. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Specificity Calculation Specificity is the *most-tested* theoretical concept in CSS for KTU. It is computed as a **4-tuple (a, b, c, d)** read left-to-right, where a higher number in a leftward position wins, and a tie moves to the next position rightward. **Step 1 — Identify the four components of the tuple:** $$ (a,\; b,\; c,\; d) \;=\; (\text{inline},\; \text{ID},\; \text{class/attr/pseudo-class},\; \text{element/pseudo-element}) $$ **Step 2 — Count occurrences in the selector.** **Worked Example A:** Consider the selector `div#header > ul.nav li.active:hover`. Let us count each category from right to left of the tuple: - **a (inline):** There is no `style="..."` attribute, so $a = 0$. - **b (ID):** The selector contains `#header`. So $b = 1$. - **c (class/attr/pseudo-class):** We count `.nav`, `.active`, and `:hover`. So $c = 3$. - **d (element/pseudo-element):** We count `div`, `ul`, and `li`. So $d = 3$. **Step 3 — Form the tuple:** $$ \text{Specificity} = (0,\; 1,\; 3,\; 3) $$ **Step 4 — Compare against another rule.** Suppose another rule `ul li` is also in play. Its tuple is $(0,\,0,\,0,\,2)$. Comparing tuples left-to-right: - Position a: $0 = 0$ (tie). - Position b: $1 > 0$ (decisive). **Conclusion:** `div#header > ul.nav li.active:hover` wins, regardless of source order. **Worked Example B:** The selector `#main #content p.intro` evaluates as: - $a = 0$ (no inline) - $b = 2$ (two IDs: `#main` and `#content`) - $c = 1$ (one class: `.intro`) - $d = 1$ (one element: `p`) $$ \text{Specificity} = (0,\; 2,\; 1,\; 1) $$ ### 3.2 Derivation — CSS Box Model Total Width The **Box Model** is the rectangular layout blueprint of every HTML element. It consists of four concentric layers (from innermost to outermost): **Content → Padding → Border → Margin**. **Step 1 — State the formula.** $$ \text{Total Width} = W_{\text{content}} + 2 \cdot P + 2 \cdot B + 2 \cdot M $$ The factor of 2 accounts for both the left and right sides of the element. **Step 2 — Plug in numerical values.** Suppose we have: - `width: 200px` - `padding: 10px` - `border: 5px solid black` - `margin: 20px` **Step 3 — Substitute into the formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Width} &= W_{\text{content}} + 2(P + B + M) \\ &= 200 + 2(10 + 5 + 20) \\ &= 200 + 2(35) \\ &= 200 + 70 \\ &= 270 \text{ px} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Out of the total $270\,\text{px}$ horizontal footprint, only $200\,\text{px}$ is *visible* to the user's content; the remaining $70\,\text{px}$ is consumed by spacing and outlines. This is why modern developers prefer `box-sizing: border-box`, which makes `width: 200px` mean the *total* $200\,\text{px}$ allocation. **Verification with border-box:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{\text{total}} &= 200\,\text{px (explicit)} \\ W_{\text{content}} &= 200 - 2(10 + 5) = 200 - 30 = 170\,\text{px} \\ \text{Outer space} &= 2 \cdot M = 40\,\text{px (separate from total)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation — RGB to Hexadecimal Color Conversion CSS accepts colours in three notations: named (`red`), `rgb(255, 0, 0)`, or `#FF0000`. Converting between them is a frequent lab question. **Step 1 — Take the RGB triplet.** Example: `rgb(46, 139, 87)` (Sea Green). **Step 2 — Convert each decimal component to a 2-digit hexadecimal number.** - $46 \div 16 = 2$ remainder $14 \rightarrow 2E$ - $139 \div 16 = 8$ remainder $11 \rightarrow 8B$ - $87 \div 16 = 5$ remainder $7 \rightarrow 57$ **Step 3 — Concatenate the three 2-digit hex codes.** $$ \text{rgb}(46,\, 139,\, 87) \;\longrightarrow\; \#2E8B57 $$ **Step 4 — Verify using reverse conversion.** - $2E_{16} = 2 \cdot 16 + 14 = 32 + 14 = 46$ ✓ - $8B_{16} = 8 \cdot 16 + 11 = 128 + 11 = 139$ ✓ - $57_{16} = 5 \cdot 16 + 7 = 80 + 7 = 87$ ✓ **Shorthand hex rule:** If each pair is a doubled digit (e.g., `#FF0000` $\rightarrow$ `F0` is doubled), it can be shortened to 3 characters: `#F00`. Hence `#FF0000` $\equiv$ `#F00`. ### 3.4 Derivation — Relative Unit Conversion Given `html { font-size: 18px; }` and a rule `p { font-size: 1.5rem; }`, what is the rendered `p` size in pixels? **Step 1 — Recognise that `rem` is rooted at the `` element.** **Step 2 — Apply the formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} p_{\text{size}} &= 1.5 \cdot \text{html}_{\text{size}} \\ &= 1.5 \cdot 18 \\ &= 27\,\text{px} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with `em`.** If `1.5em` were used inside a `
` whose own font-size is $24\,\text{px}$, the result would be $1.5 \cdot 24 = 36\,\text{px}$ — **compounding effects**, which is why `rem` is preferred. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Selector | Scenario | Best Selector | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Style all paragraphs | `p` | Simplest, most efficient | | Style unique header | `#main-header` | Highest specificity, semantically unique | | Style multiple buttons with same look | `.btn` | Reusable across many elements | | Style the first item in a list | `li:first-child` | Pseudo-class avoids extra HTML hooks | | Reset all default margins | `*` | Universal selector, applied once | | Style a link's hover state | `a:hover` | Pseudo-class for interaction | | Style all `input` with `type="text"` | `input[type="text"]` | Attribute selector, semantic and explicit | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The CSS Box Model The following Mermaid block visualises the four concentric layers of the CSS Box Model, with margin, border, padding, and content areas clearly labelled. ```mermaid graph TD A["OUTER MARGIN
Transparent surrounding space"] --> B["BORDER ZONE
Outline around padding"] B --> C["PADDING ZONE
Inner transparent spacing"] C --> D["CONTENT BOX
Text and images live here"] D --> E["ACTUAL TEXT / IMAGE
Rendered output"] F["width: 200px"] --> D G["padding: 10px"] --> C H["border: 5px"] --> B I["margin: 20px"] --> A ``` **Interpretation:** Reading from the outside in, the *outermost* layer is the **margin** (the gap between this element and its neighbours). The **border** wraps the padding. The **padding** separates the border from the actual content. The **content box** holds the text or image. In `box-sizing: content-box` (the default), the declared `width` applies only to the innermost layer. ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CSS Cascade & Conflict Resolution Flow This block diagram represents the decision algorithm the browser uses to choose which CSS rule wins when multiple rules apply to the same element. ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Multiple CSS rules target the same element"] --> q1{"Is any rule marked
with !important?"} q1 -- YES --> win1["Apply the !important rule
Highest priority"] q1 -- NO --> q2{"Is any rule Inline
style attribute?"} q2 -- YES --> win2["Apply Inline style
Second highest priority"] q2 -- NO --> q3{"Compare Specificity tuples
(a, b, c, d)"] q3 -- "One is higher" --> win3["Apply the higher specificity rule"] q3 -- "Equal" --> q4{"Compare source order
in stylesheet"} q4 -- "Last rule" --> win4["Apply the LAST declared rule"] q4 -- "First rule" --> lose["Earlier rule is overridden"] ``` **Interpretation:** A student should memorise this exact 4-step decision tree — it is a guaranteed short-answer question in KTU. ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CSS Inclusion Methods Comparison ```mermaid graph LR A["HTML Document"] --> B{"How is CSS applied?"} B -- "1. Inline" --> C["style attribute on element
Highest specificity
Not reusable"] B -- "2. Internal" --> D["style tag in head
Page-scoped
Medium specificity"] B -- "3. External" --> E["Separate .css file
linked via link tag
Most reusable"] C --> F["Rendered Page"] D --> F E --> F ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — CSS Selector Specificity Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD p1["INLINE STYLES
style attribute"] --> p2["ID SELECTORS
#myId"] p2 --> p3["CLASS / ATTR / PSEUDO-CLASS
.myClass [type=text] :hover"] p3 --> p4["ELEMENT / PSEUDO-ELEMENT
p div ::before"] p4 --> p5["UNIVERSAL & INHERITED
* and inherited values"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading this diagram:** Moving *downward* decreases specificity. The browser resolves a tie at any level by looking at the next level *down*. This is the same logic embedded in the tuple $(a, b, c, d)$. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Define CSS. List and briefly explain the three methods of including CSS in an HTML document.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) is a stylesheet language used to describe the presentation of HTML/XML documents. It separates content (HTML) from presentation (CSS), enabling consistent, reusable, and maintainable styling across web pages. **Three Methods (2 marks):** 1. **Inline CSS** — Written inside the `style` attribute of an HTML element. Highest specificity; not reusable. Example: `

`. 2. **Internal (Embedded) CSS** — Written inside a `` | `` | **Advantages and Disadvantages (2 marks):** - **Inline:** Advantage — quick and highly specific. Disadvantage — cannot be reused, hard to maintain, bloats markup. - **Internal:** Advantage — keeps styles with the page, no extra HTTP request. Disadvantage — cannot be shared across pages, increases initial page size. - **External:** Advantage — clean separation of concerns, cached for performance, single point of update. Disadvantage — extra HTTP request (mitigated by caching/CDN). **When to choose External CSS (1 mark):** Use **External CSS** for any project with **more than one page**, or any project expected to grow. It enforces the *separation of concerns* principle, dramatically reduces duplication, enables team collaboration (one developer per concern), supports browser caching for faster page loads, and allows **theming** via CSS variables to be changed in one place. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering *"differentiate"* questions, students often write a one-line answer for each type.
