The examiner expects a **tabular comparison** plus a concluding statement of *when to use which*. Missing the conclusion typically costs 1–2 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — CSS):** - **Definition:** CSS = Cascading Style Sheets; separates presentation from structure. - **Three inclusion methods:** Inline, Internal, External — in increasing order of reusability. - **CSS Rule syntax:** `selector { property: value; }`. - **Selector types:** Universal (`*`), Element (`p`), Class (`.x`), ID (`#x`), Descendant (`A B`), Child (`A > B`), Adjacent Sibling (`A + B`), Attribute (`[attr=val]`), Pseudo-class (`:hover`), Pseudo-element (`::before`). - **Specificity tuple $(a, b, c, d)$:** inline $\rightarrow$ ID $\rightarrow$ class/attr/pseudo-class $\rightarrow$ element/pseudo-element. Compare left-to-right. - **Cascade priority (descending):** `!important` $\rightarrow$ Inline $\rightarrow$ Specificity $\rightarrow$ Source order. - **Box Model layers (inner to outer):** Content $\rightarrow$ Padding $\rightarrow$ Border $\rightarrow$ Margin. - **Total width formula:** $\text{Total} = W + 2(P + B + M)$ in `content-box` mode. - **`box-sizing: border-box`:** declared width includes content + padding + border. - **Default `1em` = `16px`**; `rem` is relative to root ``. - **Inherited properties by default:** `color`, `font-family`, `font-size`, `line-height`, `visibility`. - **Non-inherited properties:** `margin`, `padding`, `border`, `background`, `width`, `height`. - **RGB $\rightarrow$ Hex:** Convert each decimal channel to 2-digit hex; concatenate. `#FF0000` $\equiv$ `#F00`. - **Margin collapse:** Vertical adjacent margins collapse; the larger of the two wins. > [!TIP] > For the **ESE (End Semester Exam)**, always pair the conceptual definition with a **code example** and a **real-world analogy** — KTU's outcome-based evaluation (OBE) rewards answers that demonstrate application, not just memorised definitions.

topics.title: Understanding JavaScript
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GXEST203 - FOUNDATIONS OF COMPUTING: FROM HARDWARE ESSENTIALS TO WEB DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Understanding JavaScript # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **JavaScript (JS)** is a high-level, interpreted, just-in-time (JIT) compiled, multi-paradigm programming language that conforms to the **ECMAScript (ES) specification**. It is the de-facto scripting language of the **World Wide Web (WWW)** and is primarily used to create dynamic, interactive behavior in web browsers (client-side) and, with environments such as **Node.js**, on servers (server-side). In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Foundations of Computing*, JavaScript is positioned as the **third layer** of the classic front-end web stack: | Layer | Language | Responsibility | KTU Keyword | |-------|----------|----------------|-------------| | Structure | **HTML** | Content / Skeleton | *What is on the page* | | Presentation | **CSS** | Styling / Appearance | *How it looks* | | **Behavior** | **JavaScript** | **Logic / Interaction** | **How it behaves** | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant" Model Imagine a webpage is a **restaurant**: - **HTML** is the **building, tables, and menu** (static structure). - **CSS** is the **interior decoration, lighting, and uniform** (presentation). - **JavaScript** is the **chef, waiter, and cashier** (behavior). It takes orders (events), cooks food (computations), updates the menu (DOM manipulation), and gives change (responses) — *all while the customer is seated*. Without JS, the restaurant looks beautiful but is **completely static**; the moment a customer walks in, nothing happens. ### 1.3 Key Properties of JavaScript (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > - **Interpreted + JIT Compiled**: Modern engines (V8, SpiderMonkey) compile JS to machine code at runtime. > - **Dynamically Typed**: Variable types are decided at runtime, not declared by the programmer. > - **Single-Threaded with an Event Loop**: Executes one task at a time but handles async via the event loop. > - **First-Class Functions**: Functions can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned. > - **Prototype-Based OOP**: Inheritance is achieved through prototypes, not classical classes (though ES6 added `class` syntax as syntactic sugar). > - **Case-Sensitive**: `myVar` and `myvar` are two different identifiers. > - **Unicode-based**: Identifiers may contain Unicode characters. ### 1.4 Where JavaScript Runs (Execution Environments) ```text ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ JavaScript Runtime Environments │ ├────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┤ │ Client-Side (Browser) │ Server-Side / Other │ ├────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┤ │ • Google Chrome (V8) │ • Node.js (V8) │ │ • Mozilla Firefox (SpMky) │ • Deno (V8 + TS) │ │ • Safari (JSC) │ • Bun (Zig+JS) │ │ • Microsoft Edge (V8) │ • Embedded (IoT,ReactN) │ └────────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 1.5 Embedding JavaScript in HTML There are three standard ways to include JS in an HTML document (KTU frequently tests this): 1. **Inline** — using event attributes (e.g., `onclick="..."`). ❌ Discouraged. 2. **Internal** — using a ``. ✅ Best practice. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 JavaScript Syntax Building Blocks (Anatomy of a JS Program) A JavaScript program is a sequence of **statements**, terminated by semicolons (`;`) — semicolons are optional due to **Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI)** but are **strongly recommended** for clarity and to avoid ASI pitfalls. ### 2.1.1 Variables — The Three Declarators > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU **favourite question**: *Differentiate between `var`, `let`, and `const`.* | Feature | `var` (ES5) | `let` (ES6+) | `const` (ES6+) | |---|---|---|---| | Scope | Function-scoped | **Block-scoped** `{ }` | **Block-scoped** `{ }` | | Hoisting | Hoisted, initialized to `undefined` | Hoisted, in **Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)** | Hoisted, in **TDZ** | | Re-declaration | ✅ Allowed in same scope | ❌ Error | ❌ Error | | Re-assignment | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Error (binding immutable) | | Global object property (in browser) | ✅ `window.x` | ❌ | ❌ | | Recommended Use | ❌ Legacy | ✅ When value changes | ✅ Default choice | ### 2.1.2 Data Types (8 Total) JavaScript has **8 data types** — 7 primitive + 1 non-primitive (object). | # | Type | `typeof` result | Example | Mutability | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `number` | `"number"` | `42`, `3.14`, `NaN`, `Infinity` | Immutable | | 2 | `bigint` | `"bigint"` | `123n` | Immutable | | 3 | `string` | `"string"` | `"Kerala"`, `'KTU'` | Immutable | | 4 | `boolean` | `"boolean"` | `true`, `false` | Immutable | | 5 | `undefined` | `"undefined"` | `let x;` | Immutable | | 6 | `null` | `"object"` ⚠️ | `let x = null;` | Immutable | | 7 | `symbol` | `"symbol"` | `Symbol("id")` | Immutable | | 8 | `object` | `"object"` | `{a:1}`, `[1,2]`, `function(){…}` | **Mutable** | > [!WARNING] > **`typeof null === "object"`** is a famous 25-year-old bug in JavaScript preserved for backward compatibility. Examiners love this! ### 2.1.3 Operators — The Reference Table KTU frequently asks for **operator precedence** and **type coercion** rules. | Category | Operators | Notes | |---|---|---| | Arithmetic | `+ - * / % **` | `**` is exponent (ES2016) | | Increment / Decrement | `++ --` | Pre vs Post matters | | Comparison | `== != === !== < > <= >=` | **`===` strict** vs `==` loose (coerces) | | Logical | `&& \|\| ?? !` | `??` is nullish-coalescing (ES2020) | | Assignment | `= += -= *= /= %= **=` | Right-associative | | Ternary | `cond ? a : b` | Only ternary operator | | Type | `typeof instanceof` | | | Bitwise | `& \| ^ ~ << >> >>>` | Operates on 32-bit signed integers | ### 2.1.4 Type Coercion — Truthy vs Falsy > [!IMPORTANT] > JavaScript performs **implicit type conversion** in boolean contexts. The following **8 values** are **falsy** — *everything else is truthy*: ```javascript false, 0, -0, 0n, "", null, undefined, NaN ``` Quick demo: ```javascript Boolean("KTU") // true (non-empty string) Boolean("") // false Boolean(0) // false Boolean([]) // true (!) Empty array is truthy Boolean({}) // true Empty object is truthy ``` ### 2.1.5 Control Flow Structures | Construct | Syntax Shape | KTU Note | |---|---|---| | `if / else if / else` | `if (cond) {…} else if {…} else {…}` | Most common | | `switch` | `switch(expr){ case x: … break; }` | Uses **strict** `===` | | `for` | `for(init; cond; update){…}` | C-style | | `while` | `while(cond){…}` | Pre-test | | `do…while` | `do{…}while(cond)` | Post-test (runs at least once) | | `for…of` | `for (const v of arr)` | Iterates **values** | | `for…in` | `for (const k in obj)` | Iterates **keys** | ### 2.1.6 Functions — Three Ways to Define 1. **Function Declaration** (hoisted): ```javascript function greet(name) { return "Hello, " + name; } ``` 2. **Function Expression** (not hoisted): ```javascript const greet = function(name) { return "Hello, " + name; }; ``` 3. **Arrow Function** (ES6+, no own `this`): ```javascript const greet = (name) => `Hello, ${name}`; ``` ### 2.1.7 The Document Object Model (DOM) — Why JS Matters > [!NOTE] > The **DOM** is a tree-structured, in-memory representation of the HTML document. JavaScript reads and mutates this tree via the global `document` object to create **dynamic, interactive web pages**. Common DOM APIs (high-yield for KTU): - `document.getElementById("id")` - `document.querySelector(".class")` - `document.querySelectorAll("p")` - `element.innerHTML`, `element.textContent` - `element.style.property = "value"` - `element.addEventListener("event", handler)` ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield "Cheat Sheet" — Operator Precedence (Top → Bottom) | Precedence | Operator | Description | Associativity | |---|---|---|---| | 21 | `()` `[]` `.` | Grouping / Member access | Left | | 20 | `++` `--` `!` `typeof` | Unary | Right | | 13 | `**` | Exponentiation | Right | | 12 | `*` `/` `%` | Multiplicative | Left | | 11 | `+` `-` | Additive / String concat | Left | | 10 | `<<` `>>` `>>>` | Bitwise shift | Left | | 9 | `<` `<=` `>` `>=` `in` `instanceof` | Relational | Left | | 8 | `==` `!=` `===` `!==` | Equality | Left | | 4 | `&&` | Logical AND | Left | | 3 | `\|\|` | Logical OR | Left | | 2 | `??` | Nullish coalescing | Left | | 1 | `? :` | Conditional (ternary) | Right | | 0 | `=` `+=` `-=` … | Assignment | Right | > Real-world utility: incorrect precedence is the source of ~40% of beginner JS bugs. Exam questions often test: `2 + 3 * 4 ** 2` = `2 + 3*16` = `2 + 48` = **50** (not 80, not 98). ## 2.3 Event-Driven Programming Model JavaScript in a browser reacts to **events** (user actions, network responses, timers). The browser maintains an **Event Queue**, and the **Event Loop** dispatches them to the **Call Stack** one at a time. ```text ┌─────────────┐ push ┌─────────────┐ │ Event Queue│ ────────► │ Call Stack │ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ ▲ │ │ Event Loop ▼ └──────────────────── JS Engine ``` This is why JavaScript can be **non-blocking** even though it is single-threaded — long operations (e.g., `fetch`, `setTimeout`) are delegated, and their callbacks re-enter the queue when complete. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Truth Tables & Analytical Matrix > Since JavaScript is a CS/Programming topic, "derivations" here take the form of **exhaustive code walkthroughs, execution traces, and analytical matrices**. No mathematical physics derivations are present in this topic. ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — `var` vs `let` vs `const` Execution Trace The following program is **executed line-by-line**. We trace the state of memory and the output to the console. ```javascript console.log(a); // Line 1 var a = 10; // Line 2 let b = 20; // Line 3 const c = 30; // Line 4 b = 25; // Line 5 // c = 35; // Line 6 ← TypeError at runtime ``` ### Step-by-Step Trace | Line | Action | Internal State | Output / Effect | |------|--------|----------------|-----------------| | 0 (Hoisting Phase) | Engine scans whole script. `var a` is hoisted and initialized to `undefined`. `let b` and `const c` are hoisted into the **TDZ**. | `a = undefined`, `b = TDZ`, `c = TDZ` | — | | 1 | `console.log(a)` reads `a` (which is `undefined`). | `a = undefined` | **`undefined`** | | 2 | `a = 10` assigns value. | `a = 10` | — | | 3 | `b` exits TDZ; `b = 20`. | `b = 20` | — | | 4 | `c` exits TDZ; `c = 30`. | `c = 30` | — | | 5 | `b = 25` re-assigns (allowed for `let`). | `b = 25` | — | | 6 (if uncommented) | Attempt to assign to `const` binding. | — | **TypeError: Assignment to constant variable.** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Take-away:** `var` is hoisted with initialization, `let`/`const` are hoisted without initialization (TDZ). This is the single most-tested JS concept. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Type Coercion Trace (`==` vs `===`) The loose equality operator `==` performs implicit type conversion. The strict operator `===` does **not**. The following trace is from the **ECMAScript Abstract Equality Comparison Algorithm**. | Expression | `==` Result | `===` Result | Coercion Logic | |---|---|---|---| | `5 == "5"` | `true` | `false` | String `"5"` → Number `5` | | `0 == false` | `true` | `false` | `false` → Number `0` | | `null == undefined` | `true` | `false` | Special rule: only equal to each other | | `null == 0` | `false` | `false` | Special rule overrides numeric | | `"" == false` | `true` | `false` | `""` → `0` → `false` | | `NaN == NaN` | `false` | `false` | `NaN` is never equal to anything, not even itself! | | `[] == 0` | `true` | `false` | `[]` → `""` → `0` | | `[1] == 1` | `true` | `false` | `[1]` → `"1"` → `1` | | `[] == []` | `false` | `false` | Objects compared by **reference**, not value | > [!WARNING] > KTU Best-Practice Rule: **Always use `===` and `!==`** in production code. The loose operator produces bugs that are nearly impossible to debug. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table for Logical Operators For values `A` and `B`, the result of `A && B` and `A || B`: | A | B | `A && B` | `A \|\| B` | `!A` | |---|---|---|---|---| | `false` | `false` | `false` | `false` | `true` | | `false` | `true` | `false` | `true` | `true` | | `true` | `false` | `false` | `true` | `false` | | `true` | `true` | `true` | `true` | `false` | > [!NOTE] > Logical operators in JavaScript do **not** always return booleans! They return the **actual operand** that decided the result — a feature called **short-circuit evaluation with value-return**. ```javascript "KTU" && "Kerala" // returns "Kerala" (last truthy) "" && "Kerala" // returns "" (first falsy, short-circuits) "KTU" || "Kerala" // returns "KTU" (first truthy) "" || "Kerala" // returns "Kerala" (first truthy) ``` ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Walkthrough — DOM Manipulation The HTML: ```html

Hello

``` The JavaScript (`app.js`): ```javascript // Step 1 — Select DOM elements const titleEl = document.getElementById("title"); const btnEl = document.getElementById("btn"); // Step 2 — Define a callback function function handleClick() { titleEl.textContent = "Welcome to KTU!"; titleEl.style.color = "blue"; console.log("Button was clicked at " + new Date().toLocaleTimeString()); } // Step 3 — Register an event listener btnEl.addEventListener("click", handleClick); ``` ### Execution Trace | User Action | Event Fired | Listener Invoked? | DOM State After | |---|---|---|---| | Page loads | `DOMContentLoaded` | ❌ (no listener) | `

Hello

` (black) | | User clicks button (1st time) | `click` | ✅ `handleClick()` | `

Welcome to KTU!

` | | User clicks button (2nd time) | `click` | ✅ `handleClick()` | Same text, new timestamp logged | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — `==` Coercion Order (The Algorithm) The ECMAScript spec defines a precise order for `==`: ```text A == B → 1. If Type(A) === Type(B), return StrictEqual(A,B) 2. If A is null and B is undefined → true (and vice-versa) 3. If Type(A) is number and Type(B) is string → compare A == ToNumber(B) 4. If Type(A) is boolean → compare ToNumber(A) == B 5. If Type(B) is boolean → compare A == ToNumber(B) 6. If A is string/number/symbol and B is object → compare A == ToPrimitive(B) 7. … symmetrical cases 8. Otherwise → false ``` **Worked example:** Trace `"" == false` - Step 1: Types differ (`string` vs `boolean`). Continue. - Step 2: Neither is `null`/`undefined`. - Step 3: A is not number, skip. - Step 4: A is not boolean, skip. - Step 5: B **is** boolean → evaluate `"" == ToNumber(false)` → `"" == 0`. - Step 3 (re-applied): A is string, B is number → `ToNumber("") == 0` → `0 == 0` → **`true`**. ✅ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — JavaScript Execution Pipeline in a Browser ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HTML Document Loaded] --> B[Browser Parses HTML] B --> C{Encounter script tag} C -- External src= --> D[Fetch .js file via Network] C -- Inline script --> E[Read embedded code] D --> F[JavaScript Engine: V8 / SpiderMonkey] E --> F F --> G[Parse to AST] G --> H[Interpreter: Ignition / Baseline] H --> I[Profiler watches hot functions] I -- Hot function detected --> J[JIT Compiler: TurboFan / Baseline JIT] J --> K[Optimized Machine Code] K --> L[Execute on CPU] H --> L L --> M[Mutate DOM / Trigger Events] M --> N[Browser Repaints / Reflows] N --> O[User Sees Updated Page] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Event Loop & Call Stack Interaction ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CallStack[Call Stack] S1[Executing main script] S2[Executing function A] S3[Executing function B] end subgraph WebAPIs[Browser Web APIs] W1[setTimeout] W2[fetch / XHR] W3[DOM Event Listener] end subgraph MicroQueue[Microtask Queue] M1[Promise.then callbacks] M2[queueMicrotask] end subgraph MacroQueue[Macrotask / Callback Queue] Q1[setTimeout callback] Q2[setInterval callback] Q3[DOM event callback] end S1 -- calls --> S2 S2 -- calls --> S3 S3 -- async fetch --> W2 S3 -- setTimeout 1s --> W1 S3 -- addEventListener --> W3 W1 -- timer expires --> MacroQueue W2 -- response received --> MicroQueue W3 -- user clicks --> MacroQueue MicroQueue -- higher priority --> CallStack MacroQueue --> CallStack ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — The DOM Tree as a Hierarchical Object Graph ```mermaid flowchart TD DOC[document] --> HTML[html] HTML --> HEAD[head] HTML --> BODY[body] HEAD --> TITLE[title: My KTU Page] HEAD --> META[meta charset UTF-8] BODY --> H1[h1 id=title: Hello] BODY --> P[p: Welcome] BODY --> BUTTON[button id=btn: Click Me] BODY --> SCRIPT[script src=app.js] SCRIPT --> JS[JavaScript Engine Access] JS -- getElementById --> H1 JS -- addEventListener --> BUTTON JS -- createElement --> SPAN[new span element] BODY --> SPAN ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Client-Server Architecture with JavaScript ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Client[Client Browser] UI1[HTML Skeleton] UI2[CSS Styling] UI3[JavaScript Behavior] end subgraph Server[Web Server] REQ1[GET /index.html] REQ2[GET /app.js] REQ3[POST /api/data] end subgraph JS_Runtime[JavaScript Runtime] ENG[Engine: V8] DOM[DOM API] FETCH[Fetch API] end UI3 --> ENG ENG --> DOM ENG --> FETCH FETCH -- HTTPS --> REQ3 REQ3 -- JSON response --> FETCH FETCH --> ENG ENG -- update DOM --> DOM DOM -- repaint --> Client ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]` **Define JavaScript. List any four features of JavaScript.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **JavaScript** is a high-level, interpreted, JIT-compiled, multi-paradigm scripting language standardized as **ECMAScript**, primarily used to add dynamic and interactive behavior to web pages. (1 mark) > > **Features:** (½ mark each, any four) > 1. **Lightweight and interpreted** — runs without explicit compilation. > 2. **Object-oriented** — supports prototypes and (ES6) classes. > 3. **First-class functions** — functions are values. > 4. **Platform-independent** — runs in any modern browser. > 5. **Event-driven and asynchronous** — reacts to user actions via event loop. > 6. **Case-sensitive** — `Name` ≠ `name`. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between `var`, `let`, and `const` in JavaScript with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | `var` | `let` | `const` | |---|---|---|---| | Scope | Function | Block | Block | | Hoisting | Hoisted & initialized | Hoisted, in TDZ | Hoisted, in TDZ | | Re-declaration | Allowed | Not allowed | Not allowed | | Re-assignment | Allowed | Allowed | **Not allowed** | | Default to window | Yes | No | No | Example: ```javascript { var a = 1; let b = 2; const c = 3; } console.log(a); // 1 — var leaks out console.log(b); // ReferenceError — block-scoped console.log(c); // ReferenceError — block-scoped ``` (2 marks for the table, 1 mark for example) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > *Per KTU ESE 2024 regulations: each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two alternatives.* --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024, Model]` **(a) Explain the different data types available in JavaScript with examples. How does JavaScript differentiate between primitive and non-primitive (reference) types? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** JavaScript has **eight data types** — seven primitive and one non-primitive (object). (½ mark) **Primitive Types (immutable, stored by value):** (3 marks) | Type | Example | Description | |---|---|---| | `number` | `let n = 42;` | Integers and floats (64-bit IEEE-754) | | `bigint` | `let b = 9007199254740993n;` | Arbitrary precision integers | | `string` | `let s = "KTU";` | Sequence of UTF-16 chars | | `boolean` | `let t = true;` | `true` or `false` | | `undefined` | `let x;` | Variable declared, no value assigned | | `null` | `let y = null;` | Intentional absence of value | | `symbol` | `let s = Symbol("id");` | Unique, immutable identifier (ES6) | **Non-Primitive Type (mutable, stored by reference):** (1 mark) - `object` — includes plain objects `{}`, arrays `[]`, functions, dates, regex, etc. **Differentiation (Primitive vs Reference):** (2 marks) | Aspect | Primitive | Reference | |---|---|---| | Storage | Stored **directly** in the variable (stack) | Stored as a **pointer** to a heap object | | Copy behaviour | Copy creates **independent** value | Copy creates a **shared** reference | | Mutability | Immutable | Mutable | | Comparison | Compared by **value** (`===`) | Compared by **reference** | Example: ```javascript let a = 10, b = a; // b is a copy of value 10 a = 20; // b is still 10 let x = {n:1}, y = x; // y points to same object x.n = 99; // y.n is also 99 console.log(y.n); // 99 ``` **(b) Describe the JavaScript Event Loop. With a neat diagram, explain how asynchronous operations like `setTimeout` and `fetch` are handled without blocking the main thread. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** JavaScript is **single-threaded** — it has one Call Stack and can execute only one piece of code at a time. To remain responsive, it uses the **Event Loop** architecture, which comprises the **Call Stack**, the **Web/Host APIs**, the **Microtask Queue**, and the **Macrotask (Callback) Queue**. (2 marks) **Components:** (2 marks) 1. **Call Stack** — LIFO structure where the current function executes. 2. **Web APIs** — Browser-provided (e.g., `setTimeout`, `DOM events`, `fetch`). 3. **Microtask Queue** — Higher priority; holds `Promise.then`, `queueMicrotask`. 4. **Macrotask Queue** — Holds `setTimeout` callbacks, I/O completion, UI events. 5. **Event Loop** — Constantly checks: *Is the stack empty? If yes, drain microtasks first, then take one macrotask.* **Walkthrough of `setTimeout` and `fetch`:** (2 marks) ```javascript console.log("1"); setTimeout(() => console.log("2 — timeout"), 0); fetch("/api").then(r => console.log("3 — fetch done")); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("4 — microtask")); console.log("5"); ``` **Output:** `1, 5, 4, 3, 2` **Why this order:** 1. `console.log("1")` executes synchronously → prints `1`. (Stack) 2. `setTimeout(..., 0)` is handed to the **Web API timer**, which will queue the callback as a **macrotask** after 0 ms. (Stack continues) 3. `fetch()` initiates an HTTP request via the Web API; its `.then` callback is registered as a **microtask**. (Stack continues) 4. `Promise.resolve().then(...)` enqueues a **microtask** immediately. (Stack continues) 5. `console.log("5")` prints `5`. **(Stack empty!)** 6. **Event Loop** drains **microtasks first** → prints `4` (queued in step 4), then `3` (network response might have arrived). 7. Finally, takes a **macrotask** → prints `2`. **Diagram:** (1 mark) — *Refer to the Event-Loop Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.* --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]` **(a) What is the DOM? Explain with a suitable example how JavaScript can be used to (i) change the content of an HTML element, (ii) change its style, and (iii) respond to a user click event. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** > The **Document Object Model (DOM)** is a **W3C-standard, tree-structured, platform- and language-neutral interface** that represents an HTML or XML document as a hierarchical tree of objects. JavaScript uses the DOM API (exposed via the global `document` object) to read, traverse, and modify the structure, content, and styling of a web page **at runtime**. **HTML Setup (1 mark):** ```html

Hello

``` **JavaScript demonstrating all three operations (4 marks):** ```javascript // (i) Select and change content const titleEl = document.getElementById("title"); titleEl.textContent = "Welcome to KTU 2024!"; // (ii) Change style dynamically titleEl.style.color = "darkblue"; titleEl.style.fontSize = "32px"; titleEl.style.textAlign = "center"; // (iii) Register a click-event listener const btnEl = document.getElementById("btn"); btnEl.addEventListener("click", function () { alert("Button clicked! Title is: " + titleEl.textContent); }); ``` **Explanation of execution (1 mark):** 1. `document.getElementById("title")` traverses the DOM tree and returns a reference to the `

` element node. 2. Setting `.textContent` replaces the text node inside `

` → content changes. 3. Setting `.style.color` modifies the **inline style attribute**, which has higher CSS specificity → style changes. 4. `addEventListener("click", handler)` registers a callback in the **DOM Event subsystem**; when the user clicks, the event is dispatched up the DOM, the listener fires, and the alert appears. **(b) Explain the concept of operators and operator precedence in JavaScript. Evaluate the following expressions step by step and state the final output: (7 marks)** - `let x = 4 + 3 * 2 ** 2;` - `"5" + 3 - 1 + true` - `null ?? "KTU"` - `[] == false` - `typeof null` **Model Solution:** **Operators & Precedence (2 marks):** JavaScript operators have a defined precedence; higher precedence binds tighter. Multiplicative binds tighter than additive; `**` (exponentiation) is right-associative and binds tighter than `*`. The ternary, assignment, and nullish-coalescing (`??`) operators have low precedence. (See full table in Section 2.2.) **Step-by-step evaluations (1 mark each):** 1. **`let x = 4 + 3 * 2 ** 2;`** - Highest precedence: `2 ** 2` = `4` - Next: `3 * 4` = `12` - Next: `4 + 12` = `16` - **`x = 16`** ✅ 2. **`"5" + 3 - 1 + true`** - `"5" + 3` → string concatenation (left-associative) → `"53"` - `"53" - 1` → numeric subtraction → `52` - `52 + true` → `true` coerced to `1` → `53` - **Output: `53`** (type: `number`) ✅ 3. **`null ?? "KTU"`** - `??` returns the right operand only if the left is `null` or `undefined`. - Left is `null` → returns right. - **Output: `"KTU"`** ✅ 4. **`[] == false`** - Type coercion: `false` → `0` (Step 4 of algorithm) - `[] == 0` → `[]` → `""` → `0` (Steps 3 then 6 then 3) - **`true`** ✅ 5. **`typeof null`** - **Output: `"object"`** (a long-standing bug in JavaScript preserved for backward compatibility). ✅ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing `==` with `===`**: Most marks lost here. *Always default to `===`.* > 2. **Forgetting TDZ**: Students often say "`let` is not hoisted." Wrong — it **is** hoisted, just **not initialized**. > 3. **String vs numeric `+`**: `"5" + 3` is `"53"`, but `"5" - 3` is `2`. Direction matters! > 4. **`typeof null`** trap: Writing `"null"` costs a mark. Correct answer is `"object"`. > 5. **No semicolon mishaps**: Remember that `return\n value` is parsed as `return; value;` (returns `undefined`!). > 6. **Microtask vs macrotask**: Promises run **before** `setTimeout(0)`. Sequence them carefully. > 7. **DOM `getElementById` is case-sensitive** in HTML5; `"MyId"` ≠ `"myid"`. > 8. **`const` ≠ immutable object**: The **binding** is constant, the **contents** can still change: `const arr = [1,2]; arr.push(3);` is valid. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist for JavaScript (Module 4):** - **Definition** — JS is the *behavior* layer of the web (alongside HTML = structure, CSS = presentation). - **Engine** — V8 (Chrome/Node), SpiderMonkey (Firefox), JavaScriptCore (Safari). - **Execution** — Single-threaded, JIT-compiled, runs inside a host environment (browser or Node). - **Embedding** — Internal `
